${ }^{a}$ From Funahashi, S. (1998) Inorganic Reactions in Solutions, Shokabo, Tokyo, p. 246.

Table 2.5, the $k$ values vary greatly (from $10^{-7.5}$ to $10^{9.6} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ ) by metal ion [18a]. The average lifetimes of solvents in the primary solvation shell also vary widely because they are of the order of $(1 / k)$. The average lifetimes, determined for $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$from the NMR band widths are in the order NM $(0.05)<\mathrm{AN}(0.6)<\mathrm{THF}(1.0)<\mathrm{MeOH}$ (1.6) < water (3.3) < FA (4.0) < NMF (5.6) < DMF (8) ~ DMSO (8) < HMPA (15) [18b]. The values in parentheses show the lifetimes in nanoseconds. This shows that the lifetime increases with the increase in the solvating ability of the solvent.

The solvation numbers in the primary solvation shell can be estimated by NMR, IR and Raman spectroscopy or by isotope dilution method. For example, in the NMR peak area method, the solvent molecules in the primary solvation shell of a metal ion ( $\sim 1 \mathrm{M}$ ) give an ${ }^{1} \mathrm{H}$-NMR peak separated from that for the bulk solvent if they have lifetimes longer than $\sim 10^{-4} \mathrm{~s}$. The solvation number of the metal ion is determined directly from the ratio of the two peak areas. Though such metal ions are limited in number at room temperatures (see Table 2.5), they increase at low temperatures ( -60 or $-100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ). The solvation numbers obtained from NMR peak areas are usually six for such metal ions as $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}, \mathrm{Al}^{3+}, \mathrm{Ga}^{3+}, \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}, \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$, $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ti}^{3+}, \mathrm{V}^{3+}, \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ in water, $\mathrm{MeOH}, \mathrm{AN}, \mathrm{DMF}, \mathrm{DMSO}$ and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, though they are four for small metal ions such as $\mathrm{Be}^{2+}$ and for square planar $\mathrm{Pd}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Pt}^{2+}$. There is a tendency that bulky solvents, such as HMPA and trimethyl phosphate, give lower solvation numbers than other solvents. If the lifetime is too short to use the NMR method, the IR method can be applied. Figure 2.7 shows

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-035.jpg?height=478&width=857&top_left_y=1545&top_left_x=244)
Figure 2.7 FTIR spectra of $\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$ solution in $\mathrm{AN}\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ [19a] (see text).

the FTIR spectra of the $v(\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{N})$ stretching band for a $\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$ solution in $\mathrm{AN}\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$; (a) is for free AN molecules $\left(2253 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\right)$ and (b) is for AN molecules in the first solvation shell of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}\left(2276 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\right)[19 \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}]$. With increasing $\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$ concentration, the intensity of band (b) increases. Data analysis yields a solvation number close to four. The difference in $v$ between bands (a) and (b) increases with the cationic surface charge density in the order $\mathrm{NaClO}_{4}<\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{ClO}_{4}\right)_{2}<\mathrm{Sr}\left(\mathrm{ClO}_{4}\right)_{2}<\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{ClO}_{4}\right)_{2}<\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$, indicating the strengthening of the ion-solvent interaction. IR and Raman spectroscopies are useful to study the strength of ion-solvent interactions. In Ref. [19a], $\mathrm{ClO}_{4}{ }^{-}$was considered to be unsolvated in AN. However, a recent study by attenuated total reflectance FTIR spectroscopy showed that the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{N}$ stretching band for AN molecules associated with $\mathrm{ClO}_{4}{ }^{-}$has a significantly larger molar absorption coefficient than the same band for self-associated AN [19c,d]. Another FTIR study [19d] showed that iodide anion ( $\mathrm{I}^{-}$) interacts with the methyl group of AN, which is at the positive end of the molecular dipole. For IR studies of solvated ions in other aprotic solvents, see Ref. [19e,f,g].

X-ray and neutron diffraction methods and EXAFS spectroscopy are very useful in getting structural information of solvated ions. These methods, combined with molecular dynamics and Monte Carlo simulations, have been used extensively to study the structures of hydrated ions in water. Detailed results can be found in the review by Ohtaki [20a] and Ohtaki and Radnai [20b]. The structural study of solvated ions in nonaqueous solvents has not been as extensive, partly because the low solubility of electrolytes in nonaqueous solvents limits the use of X-ray and neutron diffraction methods that need electrolyte of $\sim 1 \mathrm{M}$. However, this situation has been improved by EXAFS (applicable at $\sim 0.1 \mathrm{M}$ ), at least for ions of the elements with large atomic numbers, and the amount of data on ion-coordinating atom distances and solvation numbers for ions in nonaqueous solvents are growing [18a,20a,21a,b]. For example, according to the X-ray diffraction method, the lithium ion in formamide (FA) has, on average, 5.4 FA molecules as nearest neighbors with an $\mathrm{Li}^{+}-\mathrm{O}$ distance of 224 pm , while the chloride ion is coordinated by 4.5 FA molecules and the $\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \cdots \mathrm{N}$ distance is 327 pm ; the amino group of FA interacts with the chloride ion in a bifurcated manner through the two hydrogen atoms [21a,b]. The solvation numbers obtained by these methods correspond to the number of solvent molecules in the first solvation shell immediately neighboring the ion; here, the solvent molecules may or may not interact strongly with the ion. ${ }^{7)}$ The structures of solvated halide ions ( $\mathrm{X}^{-}: \mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{Br}^{-}$and $\mathrm{I}^{-}$) in DMSO have been
7) Besides spectroscopic (EXAFS, NMR, IR, Raman) and scattering (X-ray and neutron diffraction) methods, transport properties (transference numbers and ionic mobilities) and thermodynamic properties (molar entropies of solvation, compressibilities, etc.) are used to obtain solvation numbers of ions in solution. The results obtained from transport and thermodynamic properties reflect the number of solvent molecules that behave or transport with the ion. Thus, for strongly
solvated ions, the results may include the solvent molecules in the primary and secondary solvation shells, or even more and larger values than those obtained by spectroscopic or scattering methods may be obtained. For the methods and the problems associated with the estimation of solvation numbers, see, for example, p. 61 and 139 in Ref. [2]; p. 28 in Ref. [7a]; p. 68 in Ref. [8]; p. 78 and 242 in Ref. [18a].
studied by Raman spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction [21c]. The positively charged S atom in DMSO interacts with $\mathrm{X}^{-}$and slightly positively charged methyl groups in the coordinating molecules also interact with $\mathrm{X}^{-}$. The $\mathrm{X}^{-}-\mathrm{S}, \mathrm{X}^{-} \cdots \mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{X}^{-} \cdots \mathrm{O}$ distances were determined as follows: $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}-\mathrm{S} 416, \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \cdots \mathrm{C} 363$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \cdots \mathrm{O} 543, \mathrm{Br}^{-}-\mathrm{S} 433, \mathrm{Br}^{-} \cdots \mathrm{C} 372$ and $\mathrm{Br}^{-} \cdots \mathrm{O} 544 ;$ and $\mathrm{I}^{-}-\mathrm{S} 437, \mathrm{I}^{-} \cdots \mathrm{C}$ 374 and $\mathrm{I}^{-} \cdots \mathrm{O} 520 \mathrm{pm}$. The coordination numbers of DMSO molecules were six, seven and eight for $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{Br}^{-}$and $\mathrm{I}^{-}$ions, respectively, with the uncertainty of $\pm 1$. Rather large uncertainties in the measured solvation numbers are due to the large fluctuations in the solvation structure of the anions.

### 2.2.3 <br> Ultrafast Ion Solvation Dynamics

Since the end of the 1980s, ultrafast ion solvation dynamics has been studied with great interest by combining femtosecond laser experiments, analytical theories and computer simulations [22]. This is because such ultrafast solvation dynamics is closely related to various chemical processes including electron-transfer reactions and ionic migrations (Sections 4.1.3 and 7.2.1) [23]. In typical studies of ion solvation dynamics, a fluorescent probe molecule is used that is nonpolar in the ground state but is highly polar (possibly ionic) in the electronically excited state. When the probe solute is in the ground state, the solvent dipoles around the solute remain randomly oriented. When the probe solute is excited by an ultrashort laser pulse, the solvent dipoles initially remain randomly oriented around the instantaneously created charge distribution, but, with time, they gradually reorient so that the system is energetically relaxed. Thus, if $E(0), E(t)$ and $E(\infty)$ denote the energies of the fluorescent solute at times $0, t$ and $\infty(0<t<\infty)$, respectively, there is a relation $E(0)>E(t)>E(\infty)$. If the emission energy of the excited fluorescent solute is measured as a function of time, it is observed that, with time, the emission maximum shifts to lower energy, i.e. to longer wavelength. This phenomenon is called timedependent fluorescence Stokes shift (TDFSS). The solvation dynamics is monitored by the decay of the solvation time correlation function $S(t)$ defined by

$$
S(t)=\frac{E(t)-E(\infty)}{E(0)-E(\infty)}
$$

As an example, Figure 2.8 shows the experimental $S(t)-t$ relation in water [22a,b]. The probe molecule was coumarin 343, which is ionic in the excited state. The experimental results are biphasic: an initial ultrafast Gaussian decay with a time constant of $\sim 55$ fs is followed by a slower biexponential decay of time constants 126 and 880 fs . The initial decay constitutes more than $60 \%$ of the total solvation. The relationship in Figure 2.8 has been simulated by molecular hydrodynamic theory using the dielectric relaxation parameters of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. It agrees well with the experimental results: the theoretical time constant for the initial Gaussian decay is equal to 52 fs and that for the slower biexponential decay is 134 and 886 fs . The initial decay has been shown to correspond to the intermolecular vibrational band originating from the $\mathrm{O} \cdots \mathrm{O}$ stretching mode of the $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{H} \cdots \mathrm{O}$ unit. Similar biphasic relations

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-038.jpg?height=700&width=723&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=350)
Figure 2.8 Experimentally obtained solvation time correlation function $S(t)$ for the solvation of coumarin 343 in water (taken from Ref. [22a]).

have also been obtained in AN and MeOH [22c,d,e] and, based on the theoretical studies, the initial ultrafast decay has been interpreted by the liberation motion or force free (inertial) motion of solvent molecules. Various factors complicate the study of solvation dynamics: different experimental techniques may give significantly different results, the excited probe solute may behave as a dipole rather than as an ion and the ultrafast decay may be due to the intramolecular relaxation of the excited probe solute [24]. Studies on ultrafast solvation dynamics are still under way and are providing valuable knowledge that helps understand the dynamic solvent effects on various chemical processes.

## 2.3 <br> Comparison of Ionic Solvation Energies in Different Solvents and Solvent Effects on Ionic Reactions and Equilibria

### 2.3.1 <br> Gibbs Energies of Transfer and Transfer Activity Coefficients of Ions

The various factors that contribute to ion solvation were discussed in Section 2.2.1. In this section, we deal with the solvent effects on chemical reactions more quantitatively $[8,25]$. To do this, we introduce two quantities, the Gibbs energy of transfer and the transfer activity coefficient.

If the solvation energy of species $i$ in solvent R (reference solvent) is expressed by $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}(i, R)$ and that in solvent $S$ (a solvent under study) by $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}(i, S)$, the difference
between the two is expressed by $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})$ and is called the Gibbs energy of transfer of species $i$ from solvent R to S :

$$
\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S})=\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}(i, \mathrm{~S})-\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}(i, \mathrm{R})
$$

If the species $i$ is electrically neutral, the value of $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})$ can be obtained by a thermodynamic method. For example, if the solubilities of $i$ in solvents R and S are $S_{\mathrm{R}}$ and $S_{\mathrm{S}}$, respectively, $\Delta \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})$ can be obtained by Eq. (2.4):

$$
\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S})=R T \ln \left(S_{\mathrm{R}} / S_{\mathrm{S}}\right)
$$

If the species $i$ is an electrolyte MX, which is electrically neutral, it is also possible to obtain the value of $\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}(i, R \rightarrow S)$ from the solubilities of MX in the two solvents.

However, if species $i$ is a single ion, the value of $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})$ cannot be obtained by purely thermodynamic means. It is necessary to introduce some extrathermodynamic assumptions. Various extrathermodynamic assumptions have been proposed. Some typical examples are described in the following list. For practical methods of obtaining the Gibbs energies of transfer for ionic species, see ${ }^{8)}$.

## (i) Reference ion/molecule assumption (assumption of a reference potential system)

When a univalent cation $\mathrm{I}^{+}$(or anion $\mathrm{I}^{-}$), which is large in size and symmetrical in structure, is reduced (or oxidized) in solvents $R$ and $S$ to form an electrically neutral $\mathrm{I}^{0}$, which has essentially the same size and structure as $\mathrm{I}^{+}$(or $\mathrm{I}^{-}$), we assume that $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{I}^{+}\right.$or $\left.\mathrm{I}^{-}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)=\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{I}^{0}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ or that the standard potentials of the redox system ( $\mathrm{I}^{+} / \mathrm{I}^{0}$ or $\mathrm{I}^{0} / \mathrm{I}^{-}$) in R and S are the same. Actually, such redox couples as bis(cyclopentadienyl)iron(III)/bis(cyclopentadienyl)iron(II) (ferrocenium ion/ferrocene, $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ ) and bis(biphenyl)chromium(I)/bis (biphenyl)chromium(0) ( $\mathrm{BCr}^{+} / \mathrm{BCr}$ ) seem nearly to meet these requirements
8) The followings are the practical procedures for obtaining the Gibbs energies of transfer and transfer activity coefficients of ionic species based on the extrathermodynamic assumptions (i), (ii) and (iii), described in the list:
Assumption (i): When we use the $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ couple as a solvent-independent potential reference, we measure the emfs of the cell $\mathrm{Pt} \mid \mathrm{Fc}^{+}$(picrate), $\mathrm{Fc}, \mathrm{AgClO}_{4}(\mathrm{R}$ or S$) \mid \mathrm{Ag}$. If the emfs in $R$ and $S$ are $E_{R}$ and $E_{S}$, respectively, we get the values of $\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ and $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ by the relation $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right.$, $\mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})=2.3 R T \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)=F$ ( $E_{\mathrm{S}}-E_{\mathrm{R}}$ ). Then, we measure the solubilities of a sparingly soluble silver salt $(\operatorname{Ag} X)$ in $R$ and $S$ to get the values of $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{sp}}(\operatorname{AgX}, \mathrm{R})$ and $\mathrm{p} K_{\text {sp }}(\mathrm{AgX}, \mathrm{S})$. Using these values, we calculate the values of $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{X}^{-}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ and $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{X}^{-}\right.$, $\mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})$. Then, by measuring the solubility of a sparingly soluble salt $\mathrm{M}^{+} \mathrm{X}^{-}$, we get the values of $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{M}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ and $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{M}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$. As an alternative method, if we get the voltammetric half-wave potentials for the
reduction of $\mathrm{M}^{+}$in R and S as the values against the half-wave potential of the $\mathrm{Fc}^{+}$/ Fc couple, we can directly get the values of $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{M}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ and $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{M}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ from the relation in Section 8.2.1.
Assumption (ii): We measure the solubilities of the reference electrolyte $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{AsPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}$ in R and S $\left(S_{\mathrm{R}}\right.$ and $\left.S_{\mathrm{S}}\right)$ and get $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{AsPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ from Eq. (2.4). Then, by the relation
$\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{As}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)=\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{~B}^{-}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)= (1 / 2) \Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{AsPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$, we get $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(i$, $\mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})$ and $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})$ for $i=\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{As}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{~B}^{-}$. Then, we measure the solubilities of $\mathrm{AgPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}$ in R and S and get $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ and $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$. The above procedures are then applicable.
Assumption (iii): We use a cell $\mathrm{Ag} \mid 0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{AgClO}_{4}(\mathrm{AN}) ;: 0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NPic}(\mathrm{AN}) ;: 0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{AgClO}_{4}$ (AN or S) $\mid \mathrm{Ag}$ and measure the emfs in AN and $S$ ( $E_{\mathrm{AN}}$ and $E_{\mathrm{S}}$ ). By neglecting the LJPs on the right side of the salt bridge $\left[0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Et}{ }_{4} \mathrm{NPic}\right. (\mathrm{AN})]$, we get $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{AN} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ by the relation $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{AN} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)=F\left(E_{\mathrm{S}}-E_{\mathrm{AN}}\right)$.
in various solvents. Thus, these redox couples are often used as reference systems having solvent-independent potentials.
This assumption, however, has some problems. One is that the relation $\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}\left(I^{+}\right.$ or $\left.\mathrm{I}^{-}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)=\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{I}^{0}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ does not hold if the relative permittivity of S is much different from that of R . If we divide the solvation energy of $\mathrm{I}^{+}$(or $\mathrm{I}^{-}$) into electrostatic and nonelectrostatic parts, the nonelectrostatic part of $\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}\left(I^{+}\right.$or $I^{-}$, $\mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})$ must be almost equal to $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{I}^{0}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$. However, as predicted from the Born equation, the electrostatic part of $\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{I}^{+}\right.$or $\left.\mathrm{I}^{-}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ is not negligible if there is a large difference between the relative permittivities of $S$ and $R .{ }^{9)}$
(ii) Reference electrolyte assumption If an electrolyte $\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-}$consists of a cation $\mathrm{A}^{+}$ and an anion $\mathrm{B}^{-}$that are large, symmetrical and of very similar size and structure, we can assume the relation $\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}\left(A^{+}, R \rightarrow S\right)=\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}\left(B^{-}, R \rightarrow S\right)$. As such an electrolyte, tetraphenylarsonium tetraphenylborate ( $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{AsPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}$ ) is the most popular. For $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{As}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{~B}^{-}$(both $\sim 0.43 \mathrm{~nm}$ in radius), the relation $\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{A}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)=\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{B}^{-}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ is almost valid for both nonelectrostatic and electrostatic parts, irrespective of the relative permittivities of $R$ and $S$. So far, this assumption is considered to be most reliable.
(iii) Assumption of negligible liquid junction potential The liquid junction potential (LJP) between electrolyte solutions in different solvents is assumed to be negligible at some particular junctions. The most popular assumption is that the LJP at $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NPic}(\mathrm{AN}) ; 0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{AgClO}_{4}(\mathrm{~S})$ is negligible (within $\pm 20 \mathrm{mV}$ ) [29]. The reliability and the limit of this assumption are discussed in Section 6.4.

Based on these and other extrathermodynamic assumptions, considerable data for the Gibbs energies of ionic transfer have been obtained. The data in Table 2.4 are the Gibbs energies of transfer from water to various nonaqueous solvents [30]. The enthalpies and entropies of ion transfer have also been obtained based on similar extrathermodynamic assumptions. They are important in getting the mechanistic information concerning the ionic transfer between different solvents. The values in Table 2.6 were obtained based on the reference electrolyte assumption [31]. Gritzner [32] determined the Gibbs energies, entropies and enthalpies of transfer of metal ions by polarography using the bis(biphenyl)chromium(I)/(0) couple as solvent-independent potential reference. Marcus compiled in detail these thermodynamic parameters of transfer, including those from water to mixed aqueous organic solvents [33].
9) If the radius of $\mathrm{Fc}^{+}$is 0.23 nm (Ref. [8], p. 162), the variation in the electrostatic solvation energy of $\mathrm{Fc}^{+}$on transfer from water ( $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=78$ ) to AN ( $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=35.9$ ) is equal to $4.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, while that on transfer from water to low-permittivity THF $\left(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=7.6\right)$ is equal to $35.8 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Here, 1 kJ $\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ corresponds to $\sim 10 \mathrm{mV}$. The influence of permittivity can be avoided by using the mean of the standard potentials of the $\mathrm{I}^{+} / \mathrm{I}^{0}$ and $\mathrm{I}^{0} / \mathrm{I}^{-}$couples as reference because the influ-
ences for both couples cancel out (see Eq. (2.3)). Such $\mathrm{I}^{+} / \mathrm{I}^{0}$ and $\mathrm{I}^{0} / \mathrm{I}^{-}$couples can be realized with cobaltocene [26] and some organic molecules [27] but in a limited number of solvents.

For the $\mathrm{Fc}^{+}$/Fc couple, another reason decreases its reliability as potential reference. In water, we cannot assume that the nonelectrostatic part of the hydration energy of $\mathrm{Fc}^{+}$is equal to that of Fc because the hydrated $\mathrm{Fc}^{+}$and Fc have different structures [28].

[^9]When we consider the solvent effect on the reactivity of a chemical species, it is convenient to use the transfer activity coefficients ${ }^{\mathbf{1 0}}$ instead of the Gibbs energies of transfer. The transfer activity coefficient $\gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})$ is defined by

$$
\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S})=\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}) /(2.303 R T)
$$

where $2.303 R T=5.71 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The value of $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})$ is negative if the solvation of $i$ is stronger in $S$ than in $R$, while it is positive if the solvation of $i$ is weaker in $S$ than in $R$. The value of $\log \gamma_{t}(i, R \rightarrow S)$ is directly related to the reactivity of species $i$ : if $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})=8$, the reactivity of species $i$ in S is $10^{8}$ times that in R , while if $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})=-8$, the reactivity of species $i$ in S is only $1 / 10^{8}$ that in R . Table 2.7 shows the values of $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S})$ for the transfer from water to various organic solvents. They were obtained from the values in Table 2.4 using Eq. (2.5). Of course, there is a relationship:

$$
\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(i, \mathrm{~S}_{1} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}_{2}\right)=\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}_{2}\right)-\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(i, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}_{1}\right)
$$

Various factors that influence ion solvation were discussed in Section 2.2.1. The values in Table 2.7 give quantitative information on the extent of those influences.

### 2.3.2 <br> Prediction of Solvent Effects by the Use of Transfer Activity Coefficients

We can use the transfer activity coefficients to predict solvent effects on chemical reactions and equilibria [25]. Some examples are shown below.

1. The solubility product constants of precipitate MX in solvents R and $\mathrm{S}\left[K_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{MX}, \mathrm{R})\right.$ and $\left.K_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{MX}, \mathrm{S})\right]$ are related by the following equations:

$$
K_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{MX}, \mathrm{~S})=\frac{K_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{MX}, \mathrm{R})}{\gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{M}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right) \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{X}^{-}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)}
$$

10) Just as in aqueous solutions, the activity of solute $i\left(a_{c, i}\right)$ in nonaqueous solutions is related to its (molar) concentration ( $c_{i}$ ) by $a_{c, i} =\gamma_{c, i} \mathcal{C}_{i}$, where $\gamma_{c, i}$ is the activity coefficient that is defined unity at infinite dilution. For
$\left(\mathrm{moll}^{-1}\right), a_{0}$ is the ion size parameter (m) and $A=1.824 \times 10^{6}\left(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} T\right)^{-3 / 2}\left(\mathrm{moll}^{-1}\right)^{-1 / 2}$ and $B=50.29 \times 10^{8}\left(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} T\right)^{-1 / 2}\left(\mathrm{moll}^{-1}\right)^{-1 / 2} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$. The values of $A$ and $B$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in some typical solvents are as follows:

|  | $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | MeOH | AN | PC | DMF | DMSO | THF |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}$ | 78.4 | 32.7 | 35.9 | 64.9 | 36.7 | 46.5 | 7.85 |
| $A\left(\mathrm{moll}^{-1}\right)^{-1 / 2}$ | 0.51 | 1.90 | 1.65 | 0.68 | 1.60 | 1.12 | 17.0 |
| $B \times 10^{-9}\left(\mathrm{moll}^{-1}\right)^{-1 / 2} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$ | 3.29 | 5.09 | 4.86 | 3.62 | 4.81 | 4.27 | 10.6 |

nonionic solutes, the activity coefficient remains near-unity up to relatively high concentrations ( $\sim 1 \mathrm{M}$ ). However, for ionic species, it deviates from unity except in very dilute solutions. The deviation can be estimated from the Debye-Hückel equation, $-\log \gamma_{c, i}=A z_{i}^{2} I^{1 / 2} /\left(1+a_{0} B I^{1 / 2}\right)$. Here, $I$ is the ionic strength and $I=(1 / 2) \sum c_{j} z_{j}^{2}$

The influence of ionic strength on $\gamma_{c, i}$ is great in solvents of lower permittivities. When we compare ionic activities in different solvents, we have to consider this activity coefficient, in addition to the transfer activity coefficient $\gamma_{\mathrm{t}}$. However, in reality, the influence of $\gamma_{c, i}$ is usually negligibly small compared to that of $\gamma_{t}$.

[^10]$$
\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{MX}, \mathrm{~S})=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{MX}, \mathrm{R})+\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{M}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)+\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{X}^{-}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)
$$

For example, using $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{AgCl}, \mathrm{W})=10.0, \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{W} \rightarrow \mathrm{AN}\right)=-4.1$, $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{W} \rightarrow \mathrm{AN}\right)=7.4$ in Eq. (2.6), we get the solubility product constant of AgCl in AN as $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{AgCl}, \mathrm{AN})=13.3$. This value agrees well with the experimental value of $12.4-12.9$. In PC, the estimated $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{AgCl}, \mathrm{PC})$ is 20.3 , while the experimental value is 19.9-20.2.
2. The relation between the dissociation constants of acid HA in solvents R and S $\left[K_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HA}, \mathrm{R})\right.$ and $\left.K_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HA}, \mathrm{S})\right]$ is expressed by

$$
\begin{aligned}
& K_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HA}, \mathrm{~S})=\frac{K_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HA}, \mathrm{R}) \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(\mathrm{HA}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S})}{\gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right) \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)} \\
& \mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HA}, \mathrm{~S})=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HA}, \mathrm{R})+\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right) \\
& +\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)-\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(\mathrm{HA}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S})
\end{aligned}
$$

If we know the transfer activity coefficients for $\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}$and HA, we can estimate the dissociation constant of acid HA in S from that in R . For example, for the transfer from water to DMSO, $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)=-3.4, \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right)=8.8$ and $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)=-0.8$ and, for the transfer from water to $\mathrm{AN}, \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$ 8.1, $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right)=10.7$ and $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)=0.4$. Using $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right. \mathrm{COOH}, \mathrm{W})=4.76$, we get $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}, \mathrm{DMSO}\right)=11.0$ and $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right.$, $\mathrm{AN})=23.2$, in fair agreement with the experimental values [34] of 12.6 in DMSO and 22.3 in AN, respectively.
3. The standard potential of the $\mathrm{M}^{+} / \mathrm{M}$ electrode in solvent $\mathrm{S}\left[E^{0}(\mathrm{~S})\right]$ is related to that in $\mathrm{R}\left[E^{0}(\mathrm{R})\right]$ by

$$
E^{0}(\mathrm{~S})-E^{0}(\mathrm{R})=0.059 \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{M}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)
$$

We can predict the value of $E^{0}(\mathrm{~S})$ from the known values of $E^{0}(\mathrm{R})$ and $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{M}^{+}\right.$, $\mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}$ ). Here, $E^{0}(\mathrm{~S})$ and $E^{0}(\mathrm{R})$ should be in a common potential scale. Of course, if the values of $E^{0}(\mathrm{~S})$ and $E^{0}(\mathrm{R})$ in a common scale are known, we can get the value of $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{M}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$. This is used to obtain the values of transfer activity coefficients (Sections 6.3.6 and 8.2.1).

Data on the Gibbs energies of transfer and the transfer activity coefficients are of practical use. The problem, however, is that the data now available are not always sufficiently reliable. It is desirable that reliable data are obtained systematically for as many chemical species as possible. For the compiled data, see Ref. [30].

## 2.4 <br> Solvent Effects on the Complexation of Metal Ions

The complexation of a solvated metal ion with a ligand (complexing agent) corresponds to the substitution of the solvent molecules existing in the first solvation
sphere by the molecules or ions of the ligand in solution. Here, the metal ion-solvent interaction competes with the metal ion-ligand interaction. Solvent molecules also interact with the free ligand and the ligand coordinated to the metal ion, affecting the reactivity of the ligand. Thus, the stability of a metal complex is easily influenced by solvent.

The complexation of metal ions with halide and thiocyanate ions has been studied in a variety of solvents, from both thermodynamic and structural aspects [6, 21a, 35]. An example is the stepwise complexation of a silver ion with halide ions $\mathrm{X}^{-}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{X}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{AgX} \quad K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{X}^{-}\right] \\
& \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+2 \mathrm{X}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{AgX}_{2}^{-} \quad \beta_{2}=\left[\mathrm{AgX}_{2}^{-}\right] /\left(\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{X}^{-}\right]^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Table 2.8 shows the values of $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{sp}}\left(=-\log K_{\mathrm{sp}}\right)$ and $\log \beta_{2}$. As described in Section 2.3, the solvent effect on $\mathrm{p} K_{\text {sp }}$ can be estimated approximately from the values of the transfer activity coefficients of $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$and $\mathrm{X}^{-}$. In Table 2.8, the values of $K_{\mathrm{sp}} \beta_{2} \left(=\left[\mathrm{AgX}_{2}{ }^{-}\right] /\left[\mathrm{X}^{-}\right]\right)$are between $10^{-5.4}$ and $10^{-3.4}$ in protic solvents $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{MeOH}\right)$ and between $10^{0.6}$ and $10^{1.3}$ in aprotic solvents (AN, PC, DMSO). The much larger $K_{\mathrm{sp}} \beta_{2}$ values in aprotic solvents mean that $\mathrm{AgX}_{2}{ }^{-}$is much more stable in aprotic solvents than in protic solvents. Thus, if an excess amount of the AgX precipitate is added to a solution containing $\mathrm{X}^{-}$, a considerable part of the $\mathrm{X}^{-}$ion is consumed to form soluble $\mathrm{AgX}_{2}{ }^{-}$. In contrast, if the AgX precipitate is added to a solution containing an excess amount of $\mathrm{X}^{-}$, the precipitate is dissolved almost completely,

Table 2.8 Solvent effects on the formation of precipitates and complexes of the silver ion with halide ions $\left(\mathrm{X}^{-}\right) .^{a}$
| Solvent | Indifferent electrolyte |  | $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ | $\mathbf{B r}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{I}^{-}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | $5 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaClO}_{4}$ | $\mathrm{p} K_{\text {sp }}$ | 10.10 | 12.62 | 16.35 |
|  |  | $\log \beta_{2}$ | 5.40 | 7.23 | 10.95 |
|  |  | $K_{\mathrm{sp}} \beta_{2}$ | $10^{-4.70}$ | $10^{-5.39}$ | $10^{-5.40}$ |
| MeOH | $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$ | $\mathrm{p} K_{\text {sp }}$ | 13.0 | 15.2 | 18.2 |
|  |  | $\log \beta_{2}$ | 7.9 | 10.6 | 14.8 |
|  |  | $K_{\mathrm{sp}} \beta_{2}$ | $10^{-5.1}$ | $10^{-4.6}$ | $10^{-3.4}$ |
| AN | $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$ | $\mathrm{p} K_{\text {sp }}$ | 12.4 | 13.2 | 14.2 |
|  |  | $\log \beta_{2}$ | 13.1 | 13.8 | 15.2 |

| PC | $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$ | $\mathrm{p} K_{\text {sp }}$ | 20.0 | 20.5 | 21.8 |
|  |  | $\log \beta_{2}$ | 20.9 | 21.2 | 22.8 |
|  |  | $K_{\mathrm{sp}} \beta_{2}$ | $10^{0.9}$ | $10^{0.7}$ | $10^{1.0}$ |
| DMSO | $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$ | $\mathrm{p} K_{\text {sp }}$ | 10.4 | 10.9 | 12.1 |
|  |  | $\log \beta_{2}$ | 11.7 | 12.0 | 13.0 |
|  |  | $K_{\mathrm{sp}} \beta_{2}$ | $10^{1.3}$ | $10^{1.1}$ | $10^{0.9}$ |


[^11]forming $\mathrm{AgX}_{2}{ }^{-}$. This is why an $\mathrm{AgX} / \mathrm{Ag}$ electrode in a high concentration of $\mathrm{X}^{-}$ cannot be prepared in aprotic solvents (see Section 6.1.2). In protic solvents, however, the formation of $\mathrm{AgX}_{2}{ }^{-}$is not appreciable, and it is possible to prepare an $\mathrm{AgCl} / \mathrm{Ag}$ electrode using a saturated KCl solution.

The complexation of metal ions with neutral molecules has also been studied extensively. We consider here the complexes of metal ions with macrocyclic ligands such as crown ethers and cryptands [36]. The $1: 1$ complexation of a metal ion $\mathrm{M}^{n+}$ with macrocyclic ligand $L$ is expressed by Eqs. (2.8) and (2.9), where $K$ is the complex formation constant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{M}^{n+}+\mathrm{L} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{ML}^{n+} \\
& K=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{n+}\right] /\left\{\left[\mathrm{M}^{n+}\right][\mathrm{L}]\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The formation constants ( $\log K$ ) for the complexes of dibenzo-18-crown-6 and cryptand[222] are, as shown in Table 2.9, markedly influenced by solvent.

If we express the complex formation constants in solvents R and S by $K_{\mathrm{S}}$ and $K_{\mathrm{R}}$, respectively, we get Eq. (2.10) from Eq. (2.9):

$$
\log K_{\mathrm{S}}-\log K_{\mathrm{R}}=\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{ML}^{n+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)-\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{M}^{n+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)-\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(\mathrm{~L}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S})
$$

However, the relationship in Eq. (2.11) is often obtained for complexes between univalent metal ions and cryptands if $R$ and $S$ are both dipolar aprotic solvents:

$$
\log K_{\mathrm{S}}-\log K_{\mathrm{R}} \approx \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{M}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)
$$

This relation tends to occur if the size of the univalent metal ion fits the size of the cavity of the cryptand or is smaller. Then, we get Eq. (2.12), and therefore Eq. (2.13), from Eq. (2.10):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{ML}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right) \approx \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(\mathrm{~L}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}) \\
& \Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{ML}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right) \approx \Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S})
\end{aligned}
$$

These results show that the metal ion in the complex $\mathrm{ML}^{+}$is almost shielded from outside solvent, by being captured in the cavity of the cryptand. Thus, the solvent-complex interaction is nearly the same as the solvent-ligand interaction (see Table 4.3). However, there are cases when Eqs. (2.12) and (2.13) are not valid. Then, the shielding is not complete and some influence of the metal ion-solvent interaction is observed. Equations (2.12) and (2.13) do not hold also when the solvent is protic, e.g. water. The oxygen and nitrogen atoms of the cryptand interact with the protic solvent and the interaction is stronger with the free cryptand than with the cryptand combined with the metal ion.

Although solvents have significant effects on the stabilities of macrocyclic ligand complexes, the selectivity of the ligand to metal ions is not much influenced by solvents. For example, in Table 2.9, both ligands are most selective to $\mathrm{K}^{+}$in all the solvents tested.

|  | $\mathrm{Rb}^{+}$ | $4.2{ }_{4}$ | $8.9_{8}$ | $9.2_{8}$ | $9.5_{0}$ | $9.0_{2}$ | $6.7_{3}$ | $5.8_{0}$ |

## 2.5 <br> Selective Solvation of Ions in Mixed Solvents

In order to achieve appropriate solvent properties, we often use mixtures of two or more solvents (mixed solvents). Although mixtures of water and organic solvents are most frequently used, mixtures of two organic solvents are also popular. A book that solely deals with mixed solvents has been published [37]. Mixed solvents play important roles in modern electrochemical technologies, as discussed in Chapter 12.

The compositions of mixed solvents are expressed by mole fraction ( $x$ ), mass fraction ( $w$ ) or volume fraction ( $\phi$ ). If a mixed solvent consists of $n_{A}$ moles of solvent $A$ and $n_{\mathrm{B}}$ moles of solvent $\mathrm{B}, x_{\mathrm{A}}, w_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\phi_{\mathrm{A}}$ for component A can be expressed by $x_{\mathrm{A}}=n_{\mathrm{A}} /\left(n_{\mathrm{A}}+n_{\mathrm{B}}\right), w_{\mathrm{A}}=n_{\mathrm{A}} /\left(n_{\mathrm{A}}+n_{\mathrm{B}} M_{\mathrm{B}} / M_{\mathrm{A}}\right)$ and $\phi_{\mathrm{A}}=n_{\mathrm{A}} /\left(n_{\mathrm{A}}+n_{\mathrm{B}} V_{\mathrm{B}} / V_{\mathrm{A}}\right)$. Here, $M_{i}$ is the molecular weight of component $i$ and $V_{i}$ the molar volume of $i$ before mixing. It is assumed that the volume of the mixed solvent is equal to the sum of the volumes of the two constituent solvents, although some volume contraction may occur by mixing.

The problem of relative permittivity of mixed solvents (denoted by $\varepsilon$ for simplicity) was theoretically studied by Debye and Onsager [38]. Onsager derived the following equation:

$$
\frac{(\varepsilon-1)(2 \varepsilon+1)}{9 \varepsilon}=\frac{\phi_{A}\left(\varepsilon_{A}-1\right)\left(2 \varepsilon_{A}+1\right)}{9 \varepsilon_{A}}+\frac{\phi_{B}\left(\varepsilon_{B}-1\right)\left(2 \varepsilon_{B}+1\right)}{9 \varepsilon_{B}}
$$

This equation can be written with good approximation as $\varepsilon=\phi_{A} \varepsilon_{A}+\phi_{B} \varepsilon_{B}$, indicating that the permittivity of the mixed solvent changes linearly between $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{B}}$ if it is

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-048.jpg?height=868&width=723&top_left_y=196&top_left_x=350)
Figure 2.9 Relative permittivities of water-organic solvent mixtures plotted against their volume fractions. Solvent: open circles AN; open triangles MeOH; open squares THF; filled circles DMSO; filled triangles DMF; filled squares Ac. (From the data in Table 7.1, Ref. [8].)

plotted against the volume fraction of the constituent solvents. When the interaction between the constituent solvents is weak, this linear relation holds well. However, when the interaction is strong, a large deviation from linearity is observed. Figure 2.9 shows the relationship between the relative permittivity and the volume fraction of mixtures of water and organic solvents. Because acetonitrile, acetone and tetrahydrofuran interact only weakly with water, near-linear relations are observed for their mixtures with water. However, for DMSO and DMF, which readily interact with water, large deviations from linearity are observed.

The acid-base properties of a mixed solvent is also an important factor influencing the behavior of solutes. Thus, the parameters of the acidity and basicity of mixed solvents have been studied to some extent [39]. Figure 2.10 shows the donor numbers of mixtures of nitromethane and other organic solvents. Because nitromethane has very weak basicity ( $D N=2.7$ ), the addition of small amounts of basic solvents (HMPA, DMSO, pyridine) increases the donor number remarkably. Figure 2.11, on the other hand, shows the acceptor numbers of mixtures of water and aprotic solvents. Because water is protic and selectively interacts with $\mathrm{Et}_{3} \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{O}$ (strong Lewis base), many of the relations curve upward. However, with HMPA, the relation curves downward because HMPA is a strong base and easily interacts with

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-049.jpg?height=624&width=783&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=207)
Figure 2.10 Donor numbers of mixtures of nitromethane with other solvents [16a].

water to weaken the interaction between water and $\mathrm{Et}_{3} \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{O}$. The acidity and basicity of mixed solvents are influenced not only by the acidity and basicity of the constituent solvents but also by the mutual interactions between the molecules of constituent solvents. At present, however, this cannot be treated theoretically.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-049.jpg?height=870&width=697&top_left_y=1121&top_left_x=205)
Figure 2.11 Acceptor numbers of mixtures of water and aprotic solvents [16a]. Aprotic solvents are shown on the curves.

For the properties and structures of binary solvent mixtures and the preferential solvation between the binary solvents, see Ref. [37].

In mixed solvents, ions are solvated competitively by the constituent solvents. If one solvent solvates more easily than the other, the so-called selective solvation occurs. In general, cations are selectively solvated by strongly basic solvents, anions by strongly acidic solvents. The ion solvation in mixed solvents has been studied extensively by measuring transport behavior and spectroscopic properties [40]. NMR is particularly useful in studying the solvated ionic species in mixed solvents. If the exchange between the solvent molecules in the primary solvation shell and those in the bulk solution is slow enough, isolated NMR peaks are observed for each of the solvated species, providing quantitative information. For example, if we measure the ${ }^{27}$ Al-NMR spectrum in DMF-DMSO mixtures of different compositions, we get seven peaks corresponding to $i=0-6$ in $\left[\mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{DMSO})_{i}(\mathrm{DMF})_{6-i}\right]^{3+}$. If the solvent exchange is rapid enough, as in the case of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$, we get a single NMR signal. Then, from the chemical shift and the peak width, we can determine the mean strength of ion solvation and the mean time of stay of solvent molecules in the solvation shell.

Attempts have been made to treat selective ion solvation in mixed solvents as ligand exchange reactions [41-43]. We express ion $X$ (cation or anion) existing in solvent A by $\mathrm{XA}_{n}$ and in solvent B by $\mathrm{XB}_{n^{\prime}}$. Here, $n \neq n^{\prime}$ if the molecular size of A is very different from that of B or if A is unidentate and B is bidentate (e.g. PC and DME in $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$solvation). Otherwise, it is usual that $n=n^{\prime}$. Then, the ligand exchange reaction in the mixture of $A$ and $B$ will proceed as follows with the increase in the concentration of B :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{XA}_{n}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{XA}_{n-1} \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{A}\left(K_{1}\right), \quad \mathrm{XA}_{n-1} \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{XA}_{n-2} \mathrm{~B}_{2}+\mathrm{A}\left(K_{2}\right), \ldots, \\
& \mathrm{XA}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{n-2}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{XAB}_{n-1}+\mathrm{A}\left(K_{n-1}\right), \quad \mathrm{XAB}_{n-1}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{XB}_{n}+\mathrm{A}\left(K_{n}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_{i}$ is the equilibrium constant. If X is selectively (preferentially) solvated by B , all of the above reactions will proceed within small concentration of $B$. However, if the selectivity is small or the exchange process can proceed ideally following the statistical probability, the relation $K_{i}=(n-i+1) i^{-1} \beta_{n}^{1 / n}$, where $\beta_{n}=\left(K_{1} K_{2} \ldots K_{n}\right)$, is expected. The Gibbs energy of transfer of $X$ from solvent $A$ to solvent mixture $(A+B)$ is given as follows [41]:.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{~A} \rightarrow \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B}) \\
& =\sum \phi_{i} \mu_{i}^{\mathrm{o} \text { elec }}-\mu_{0}^{\mathrm{o} \text { elec }}-R T \ln \left[1+\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(x_{\mathrm{B}} / x_{\mathrm{A}}\right)^{\mathrm{i}} \prod_{j=1}^{i} K_{j}\right]-n R T \ln x_{\mathrm{A}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\phi_{i}$ is the fraction of $\mathrm{XA}_{n-i} \mathrm{~B}_{i}$ and $\sum \phi_{i}=1, \mu_{i}^{\mathrm{o}}$ elec and $\mu_{0}^{\mathrm{o}}$ elec are the chemical potentials of long-range interactions (electrostatic in origin and treated by Born theory) of $\mathrm{XA}_{n-i} \mathrm{~B}_{i}$ and $\mathrm{XA}_{n}$, respectively. If solvents A and B are isodielectric, the first two terms on the right side in Eq. (2.14) is zero. Ionic preferential solvation has been handled variously, usually as the deviation from the simple solvation process as in Eq. (2.14) [42].

Cox et al. [43] expressed the Gibbs energies of transfer of ion X from solvent A to solvent $B$ and to the mixture ( $A+B$ ) by Eqs. (2.15a) and (2.15b), respectively:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{~A} \rightarrow \mathrm{~B})=-R T \ln \beta_{n}^{\prime} \\
& \Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{~A} \rightarrow \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B})=-n R T \ln \phi_{\mathrm{A}}-R T \ln \left[1+\sum_{i=1}^{n} \beta_{i}^{\prime}\left(\phi_{\mathrm{B}} / \phi_{\mathrm{A}}\right)^{i}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $\phi_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\phi_{\mathrm{B}}$ are the volume fractions of A and B , respectively, $\beta^{\prime}{ }_{i}$ is the complex formation constant of $\mathrm{XA}_{n}+i \mathrm{~B} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{XA}_{n-i} \mathrm{~B}_{i}+i \mathrm{~A}$, that is obtained in the solvent almost pure in $A$ (i.e. very small in the concentration of $B$ ). The solvation of $B$ should be stronger than that of $A$. These equations are important in showing that the data for the complexation between X and B in solvent A are applicable to predicting the solvation of $X$ in solvent $B$ and in mixed solvent $(A+B)$. As discussed in detail in Section 6.3.6, these equations are valid as long as the permittivities of A and B do not differ significantly.

The Gibbs energy of ionic transfer to mixed solvents can be obtained thermodynamically (by solubility measurement or potentiometry) or spectroscopically (by NMR, for example), and various data are available. The Gibbs energies of transfer from water to mixed aqueous organic solvents have been compiled in [44a] for cations and [44b] for anions. The enthalpies and entropies of transfer of electrolytes and ions from water to mixed aqueous organic solvents have also been compiled in [44c].

## 2.6 <br> Ion Association and Solvent Permittivities

In the above sections, we considered electrolytes that are ionophores. ${ }^{\mathbf{1 1})}$ Ionophores, such as sodium chloride, are ionic in the crystalline state and are expected to dissociate into free ions in dilute solutions. In fact, in high-permittivity solvents ( $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}>40$ ), ionophores dissociate almost completely into ions unless the solutions are of high concentration. When an ionophore is completely dissociated in the solution, its molar conductivity $\Lambda$ decreases linearly with the square root of the concentration $c\left(<10^{-2} \mathrm{M}\right)$ :

$$
\Lambda=\Lambda^{\infty}-S c^{1 / 2}
$$

where $\Lambda^{\infty}$ is the molar conductivity at infinite dilution and $S$ is the Onsager slope (Section 7.1.1). This is used to confirm the complete dissociation of an electrolyte.

With the decrease in permittivity, however, complete dissociation becomes difficult. Some part of the dissolved electrolyte remains undissociated and forms ion pairs. In low-permittivity solvents, most of the ionic species exist as ion pairs.

[^12]![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-052.jpg?height=942&width=779&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=345)

Figure 2.12 Relations between the ion association constants ( $\log K_{\mathrm{A}}$ ) and the reciprocal of solvent permittivity $\left(1 / \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}\right)$ (solid line) and between the degree of ion association ( $\alpha$ ) and $\log \left(c K_{\mathrm{A}}\right)$ (dotted curve) (open circles: $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NPic}$ in $\mathrm{AN}, \mathrm{NB}, \mathrm{MeOH}$,

Ac, Py, DCE, o-dichlorobenzene, acetic acid, chlorobenzene and benzene; filled squares: KCl in ethanolamine, $\mathrm{MeOH}, \mathrm{EtOH}$, acetic acid and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$-dioxane mixtures). The solid line was obtained using Eq. (2.19) for $a=0.6 \mathrm{~nm}$.

Ion pairs contribute neither ionic strength nor electric conductivity to the solution. Thus, we can detect the formation of ion pairs by the decrease in molar conductivity $\Lambda$. In Figure 2.12, the logarithmic values of ion association constants ( $\log K_{\mathrm{A}}$ ) for tetrabutylammonium picrate ( $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NPic}$ ) and potassium chloride ( KCl ) are plotted against $\left(1 / \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}\right)[45]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{A}^{+}+\mathrm{B}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-} \quad(\text { ion pair }) \\
& K_{\mathrm{A}}=\left[\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-}\right] /\left(\left[\mathrm{A}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{B}^{-}\right]\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Figure 2.12 also shows a curve representing the relation between the values of $\log \left(c K_{\mathrm{A}}\right)$ and the degree of ion association ( $\alpha$ ). This curve shows that, for $10^{-2} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KCl}$ in EtOH ( $\log K_{\mathrm{A}}=2$ ), $\log \left(c K_{\mathrm{A}}\right)=0$ and $38 \%$ of KCl exists as ion pairs. For 0.1 M KCl in acetic acid ( $\log K_{\mathrm{A}}=6.2$ ), $\sim 99.9 \%$ of KCl exists as ion pairs; the fraction dissociated into free ions is only $0.1 \%$. Usually, in solvents with $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}<10$, the fraction of electrolyte that is dissociated into ions is small except in very dilute solutions.

Conversely, in solvents with $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}>40$, the fraction of electrolyte that forms ion pairs is small except in fairly concentrated solutions. ${ }^{12)}$

The theories of Bjerrum (1926) and Fuoss (1958) on ion association are described in detail in a recent review [46] or books [47]. According to the Debye-Hückel theory, the activity of an ion in the solution deviates from its concentration because of the electrostatic interaction between ions, and the deviation increases with increasing ionic strength (see footnote 10). In the theory, it was postulated that the energy of electrostatic ionic interaction is much less than the kinetic energy of thermal agitation. This is true if the distance between ions is large enough. However, with decreasing distance between ions, the energy of electrostatic interaction increases and approaches the energy of thermal agitation. According to Bjerrum's theory, the energy of electrostatic interaction between two ions $\left(\left|z_{+} z_{-}\right| e^{2} / 4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} r\right)$ becomes equal to $2 k_{\mathrm{B}} T$ ( $k_{\mathrm{B}}$ : Boltzmann constant) at a critical distance $q$ and, if two ions of opposite charges are at distance $r$ that satisfies $r \leq q$, they are associated; if $r>q$, however, they remain unassociated. Moreover, the distance $r$ cannot become smaller than the 'distance of closest approach' $a$, which is equal to the sum of the radii of the associated cation and anion. Therefore, ion association occurs if $a \leq r \leq q$. If $a>q$, ion association does not occur and all ions remain free.

Here, the distance $q$ is given by Eq. (2.16):

$$
q=\frac{\left|z_{+} z_{-}\right| N_{\mathrm{A}} e^{2}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} R T}
$$

where $N_{\mathrm{A}}$ is the Avogadro constant. In aqueous solutions at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the value of $q$ is 0.35 nm for univalent ions and 1.4 nm for divalent ions. For many of the univalent hydrated ions in water, the relation $a>q$ holds and their association is difficult. However, in solvents of lower permittivities, the relation $q>a$ holds for most electrolytes, and the ion association is more pronounced.

The probability of two ions of opposite charges being located at a distance $r$ is shown in Figure 2.13. The shaded region in the figure is where ion association is expected to occur: thus, the larger the difference between $a$ and $q$, the easier is the ion association. According to Bjerrum, the ion association constant $K_{\mathrm{A}}$ (in molar concentration) is expressed by Eq. (2.17):

$$
K_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{4 \pi N_{\mathrm{A}}}{1000} \int_{a}^{q} r^{2} \exp \left(\frac{2 q}{r}\right) \mathrm{d} r
$$

An alternative approach to ion association was proposed by Fuoss. He defined the ion pair as two oppositely charged ions that are in contact, i.e. at a distance of $r=a$, and derived the following equation for the ion association constant:

$$
K_{\mathrm{A}}=\left(4 \pi N_{\mathrm{A}} / 3000\right)(a / \mathrm{m})^{3} \exp (2 q / a)
$$

12) Considerable ion association may occur even in high-permittivity solvents, e.g. $K_{\mathrm{A}}=2.97 \times 10^{4}$ for $\mathrm{CF}_{2} \mathrm{HCOOLi}$ and $0.62 \times 10^{4}$ for $\mathrm{CFH}_{2} \mathrm{COOLi}$ in $\mathrm{PC}\left(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \sim 64\right)$. A linear relation has been found between the mean charge
density of oxygen atoms, $q(\mathrm{O})$, for $\mathrm{CF}_{x} \mathrm{H}_{y} \mathrm{COO}^{-}(x+y=3)$, obtained by MNDO calculation, and $\log K_{\mathrm{A}}$ for the association of $\mathrm{CF}_{x} \mathrm{H}_{y} \mathrm{COO}^{-} \mathrm{Li}^{+}$in DMSO (Ref. [53b]).

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-054.jpg?height=732&width=719&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=345)
Figure 2.13 The probability for two ions of opposite signs existing at distance $r$. The scale on the abscissa is for an aqueous solution.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-054.jpg?height=776&width=737&top_left_y=1174&top_left_x=350)
Figure 2.14 Comparison of the $\log K_{\mathrm{A}}-\log \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}$ relation obtained by Bjerrum's theory (solid curve) and that obtained by Fuoss' theory (dotted curve). The case of a $1: 1$ electrolyte with $a=0.5 \mathrm{~nm}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-055.jpg?height=285&width=1021&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=209)
Figure 2.15 Three types of ion pairs.

Equation (2.19) is obtained from Eq. (2.18).

$$
\log K_{\mathrm{A}}=\log \left(4 \pi N_{\mathrm{A}} a^{3} / 3000\right)+0.434\left|z_{+} z_{-}\right| N_{\mathrm{A}} e^{2} /\left(4 \pi a \varepsilon_{0} \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} R T\right)
$$

From this equation, a linear relation is expected between $\log K_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\left(1 / \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}\right)$. The solid line in Figure 2.12, obtained by assuming $a=0.6 \mathrm{~nm}$ in Eq. (2.19), agrees well with the experimental results.

The two curves in Figure 2.14 are the relationships between $\log K_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\log \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}$ for a 1:1 electrolyte. The solid curve was obtained by Bjerrum's theory (Eq. (2.17)) and the dotted curve by Fuoss' theory (Eq. (2.19)), both assuming $a=0.5 \mathrm{~nm}$. The big difference between the two theories is that, according to Bjerrum's theory, ion association does not occur if $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}$ exceeds a certain value ( $\sim 50$ in Figure 2.14), although this value depends on the value of $a$. Both theories are not perfect and could be improved. In recent treatments of ion association, noncoulombic short-range interactions between ions are also taken into account [48]. By introducing noncoulombic interactions, $W^{*}(r)$, Eq. (2.17) is modified to a form as in Eq. (2.20):

$$
K_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{4 \pi N_{\mathrm{A}}}{1000} \int_{a}^{q} r^{2} \exp \left[\frac{2 q}{r}-\frac{W^{*}(r)}{k_{\mathrm{B}} T}\right] \mathrm{d} r
$$

For example, in methylformate, the $W^{*}(r)$ values are negative for $\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}, \mathrm{NaClO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$ and positive for $\mathrm{LiAsF}_{6}$ and $\mathrm{NaBPh}_{4}$ [49]. Negative $W^{*}(r)$ values suggest structure-making effects that tend to stabilize the ion pair, while positive $W^{*}(r)$ values indicate structure-breaking effects that destabilize the ion pair. Thus, $K_{\mathrm{A}}$ values for perchlorates are larger than those expected from Eq. (2.17). Beside this treatment, the ion association has been dealt with by such treatments as the mean spherical approximation (MSA) [50] and the consideration of activity coefficients of charged symmetrical ion pairs [51].

Moreover, as schematically shown in Figure 2.15, three different types of ion pairs, i.e. solvent-separated ( $\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{SSB}^{-}$), solvent-shared ( $\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{SB}^{-}$) and contact ( $\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-}$) types, may be formed depending on the strength of ion-solvent interactions, complicating the treatment of ion association. If we consider the following multistep equilibrium, the total ion association constant ( $K_{\mathrm{A}}$ ) is expressed by Eq. (2.21):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{A}_{\text {solv }}^{+}+\mathrm{B}_{\text {solv }}^{-} \stackrel{K_{1}}{\rightleftarrows} \mathrm{~A}^{+} \mathrm{SSB}^{-} \stackrel{K_{2}}{\rightleftarrows} \mathrm{~A}^{+} \mathrm{SB}^{-} \stackrel{K_{3}}{\rightleftarrows} \mathrm{~A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-} \\
& K_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{SSB}^{-}\right]+\left[\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{SB}^{-}\right]+\left[\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{A}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{B}^{-}\right]}=K_{1}\left(1+K_{2}+K_{2} K_{3}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-056.jpg?height=446&width=1187&top_left_y=189&top_left_x=281)
Figure 2.16 Left: Measured (points) and fitted (lines) IR spectrum in the region of the $v_{3}^{\mathrm{ClO}}$ vibration of $0.73 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$ in AN at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Bands $a$ and $b$ are from the free $\mathrm{ClO}_{4}{ }^{-}$, bands $1,1^{\prime}$ and 2 are from $\mathrm{ClO}_{4}{ }^{-}$in the contact ion pair. Right: Fractions of free ions (ION), solvent-shared ion pairs (SSIP) and contact ion pairs (CIP) in $\mathrm{LiClO}_{4} / \mathrm{AN}\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ [53a].

Different types of ion association can be identified by using such methods as infrared, Raman and dielectric relaxation spectroscopies [52,53]. For example, the relative concentrations of free ions ( $c_{\text {ION }}$ ), solvent-shared ion pairs ( $c_{\text {SSIP }}$ ) and contact ion pairs ( $c_{\text {CIP }}$ ) in $\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}-\mathrm{AN}\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ were obtained by combining infrared and dielectric relaxation spectroscopic measurements [53]. Because $\mathrm{ClO}_{4}{ }^{-}$was practically unsolvated in AN, a solvent-separated ion pair was not formed. In the IR spectrum of Figure 2.16 (left), the integrated intensity of bands $1,1^{\prime}$ and 2 gave $c_{\text {CIP }}$, while that of bands a and b gave ( $c_{\text {ION }}+c_{\text {SSIP }}$ ). The total ion pair concentration ( $c_{\text {CIP }}+c_{\text {SSIP }}$ ) was obtained from the dispersion amplitude of the dielectric relaxation spectrum. ${ }^{13)}$ The results are shown in Figure 2.16 (right). The association constant, $K_{\mathrm{A}}=19.8 \mathrm{dm}^{3} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, agreed well with the conductimetric result, $K_{\mathrm{A}}=21.8 \mathrm{dm}^{3} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.

In low-permittivity solvents, ions of opposite charges easily form ion pairs. However, if the electrolyte concentrations are increased in these solvents, the formation of triple ions and quadrupoles also occurs as follows:

$$
\begin{array}{lll}
\mathrm{A}^{+}+\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-} & \rightleftarrows & \mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-} \mathrm{A}^{+} \\
\mathrm{B}^{-}+\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-} & \rightleftarrows & \mathrm{B}^{-} \mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-} \\
\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-}+\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-} & \rightleftarrows & \left(\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{-}\right)_{2}
\end{array}
$$

The positive and negative triple ions can remain without being associated even in low-permittivity solvents, mainly because the triple ions are large. Because the triple ions are conductive, their formation can be detected by the increase in molar conductivity that occurs with increasing electrolyte concentrations (Figure 7.2).
13) Static solution permittivity $\varepsilon(c)$ and static solvent permittivity $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{s}}(c)$ for solutions of various electrolytes at various concentrations (c) have been obtained by dielectric relaxation spectroscopy [54]. Ion pairs contribute to permittivity if their lifetime is longer than their relaxation time. However, free ions do not contribute to permittivity. Thus, $\varepsilon(c)=\varepsilon_{s}(c)$
for completely dissociated electrolyte solutions and $\varepsilon(c) \geq \varepsilon_{\mathrm{s}}(c)$ for associated electrolyte solutions. The relaxation times of the different ion pair types in Eq. (2.21) are generally so close to one another that only the total ion pair concentration is obtained by dielectric relaxation methods [53].

Highly concentrated electrolyte solutions in low-permittivity solvents are sometimes very conductive and used as electrolyte solutions for lithium batteries. Thus, the conductimetric behavior of triple ions has been studied in detail (Section 7.1.3) [55]. Triple ions are also formed in high-permittivity protophobic aprotic solvents: for example, they are formed in acetonitrile between $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$and anions such as $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$, $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}$and $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COO}^{-}$[56]. In AN, these simple ions are only weakly solvated, are highly reactive (Table 2.7) and are stabilized by triple ion formation. In such cases, noncoulombic interactions seem to play an important role.
