## 3

## Acid-Base Reactions in Nonaqueous Solvents

This chapter discusses acid-base reactions in nonaqueous solvents, with particular emphasis on how they differ from those in aqueous solutions. The problem of pH in nonaqueous solutions is also discussed. The Brønsted acid-base concept that an acid is a proton donor and a base is a proton acceptor is adopted. The relation between an acid $\mathbf{A}$ and its conjugate base $\mathbf{B}$ is expressed by

$$
\mathbf{A} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathbf{B}
$$

The reaction between an acid $\mathbf{A}_{1}$ and a base $\mathbf{B}_{2}$ is a proton transfer from $\mathbf{A}_{1}$ to $\mathbf{B}_{2}, \mathbf{A}_{1}$ becoming its conjugate base $\mathbf{B}_{1}$ and $\mathbf{B}_{2}$ its conjugate acid $\mathbf{A}_{2}$ :

$$
\mathbf{A}_{1}+\mathbf{B}_{2} \rightleftarrows \mathbf{B}_{1}+\mathbf{A}_{2}
$$

Acid-base reactions in nonaqueous solvents have been extensively studied. Many books and reviews on acid-base equilibria and acid-base titrations in nonaqueous solvents are available. References [1-3] are particularly useful.

## 3.1 <br> Solvent Effects on Acid-base Reactions

We consider a process in which an HA-type acid, such as hydrogen chloride or acetic acid, dissolves in a solvent and dissociates into $\mathrm{H}^{+}$and $\mathrm{A}^{-}$. This process occurs in two steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Ionization : } \quad \mathrm{HA} \rightleftarrows\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}\right)_{\text {solv }} \quad K_{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{\left[\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}\right)\right]}{[\mathrm{HA}]} \\
& \text { Dissociation : } \quad\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}\right)_{\text {solv }} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{H}_{\text {solv }}^{+}+\mathrm{A}_{\text {solv }}^{-} \quad K_{\mathrm{D}}=\frac{\gamma^{2}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]}{\left[\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}\right)\right]}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\gamma$ represents the activity coefficients of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$and $\mathrm{A}^{-}$, and the activity coefficients of electrically neutral species are assumed equal to unity. In the first step, solvent molecules S act as a Lewis base (electron pair donor) to the H atom and as a Lewis

Table 3.1 Effect of solvents on the difficulties of ionization of acid
HA and the dissociation of ion pair ( $\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}$) solv.
| Solvent classification | Ionization of HA | Dissociation of $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}\right)_{\text {solv }}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| High-permittivity amphiprotic solvents | Easy | Easy |
| High-permittivity aprotic solvents | Difficult or fairly easy ${ }^{a}$ | Easy |
| Low-permittivity amphiprotic solvents | A little difficult or easy | Difficult |
| Low-permittivity aprotic solvents | Difficult | Difficult |


${ }^{a}$ Difficult in protophobic solvents, but fairly easy in protophilic solvents.
acid (electron pair acceptor) to the A atom, as shown by $m(\mathrm{~S}) \xrightarrow{\delta+\curvearrowright \delta-} \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{A} \rightarrow n(\mathrm{~S})$, and ionize the H and A atoms, resulting in the breaking of the $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{A}$ covalent bond and the formation of the ion pair $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}\right)_{\text {solv }}$. If the $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{A}$ bond is intrinsically weak, the ionization occurs easily. For a given acid, however, the ionization becomes easy, if solvent molecules interact strongly with the H and A atoms. In the second step, the ion pair $\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}\right)_{\text {solv }}$ dissociates into free ions. The dissociation process is easy if the solvent has a high permittivity and the distance of closest approach between $\mathrm{H}^{+}$and $\mathrm{A}^{-}$is large enough. Thus, two important solvent properties, i.e. relative permittivity and donor-acceptor properties, significantly influence both ionization and dissociation processes. In Table 3.1, solvents are classified into four groups according to values of relative permittivity and strengths of donor-acceptor properties; the degrees of difficulty of the ionization and dissociation processes are shown for each class.

The acid dissociation constants, $K_{\mathrm{a}}$, which are determined by potentiometry or conductimetry, usually correspond to ${ }^{\mathbf{1})}$

$$
K_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\gamma^{2}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HA}]+\left[\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}\right)\right]}=\frac{K_{\mathrm{I}} K_{\mathrm{D}}}{1+K_{\mathrm{I}}} .
$$

In order that the acid HA dissociates almost completely into $\mathrm{H}^{+}$and $\mathrm{A}^{-}$, i.e. to get a large $K_{\mathrm{a}}$ value, it is essential that both the ionization and the dissociation processes occur easily. This means that both $K_{\mathrm{I}}$ and $K_{\mathrm{D}}$ should be large enough, as is often the case in amphiprotic solvents of high permittivities. In the following sections, we discuss the characteristics of acid-base reactions in each of the four classes of solvents.

### 3.1.1 <br> Acid-Base Reactions in Amphiprotic Solvents of High Permittivity

The solvents of this class are usually of high permittivity ( $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}>20$ ) and of fair or strong acidity (proton donor capacity) and basicity (proton acceptability) [4]. Water is the

1) For example, $K_{\mathrm{I}}, K_{\mathrm{D}}$ and $K_{\mathrm{a}}$ in acetic acid are 1.0 , $2.7 \times 10^{-5}$ and $1.3 \times 10^{-5}$, respectively, for $\mathrm{HClO}_{4}$, and $5.37,9.4 \times 10^{-7}$ and $7.9 \times 10^{-7}$, respectively, for pyridinium ion $\left(\mathrm{PyH}^{+}\right)$(see Ref. [13c]).
most important solvent in this class. In water, the HA- and BH ${ }^{+}$-type acids dissociate as in Eqs. (3.1) and (3.2):

$$
\mathrm{HA}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{A}^{-}
$$

$$
\mathrm{BH}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{B}
$$

Here, $\mathrm{NH}_{4}{ }^{+}, \mathrm{RNH}_{3}{ }^{+}, \mathrm{R}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}{ }^{+}, \mathrm{R}_{3} \mathrm{NH}^{+}$and $\mathrm{PyH}{ }^{+}$(R: alkyl; Py: pyridine) are examples of $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$-type acids. An acid ( HA or $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$), with an intrinsically strong proton donor capacity, completely dissociates in water and forms its conjugate base $\left(\mathrm{A}^{-}\right.$or B$)$ and $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$. Practically, HA or $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$do not remain in the solution. This acid is called a strong acid. Examples of strong acids in water are hydrochloric, nitric and perchloric acids. The hydronium ion ( $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$) is the strongest acid that can remain in water. The strengths of the acids, which are stronger than $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$, cannot be differentiated and are equivalent to the strength of $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$. This phenomenon is called the leveling effect. For acids that are weaker than $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$, the reactions in Eq. (3.1) or (3.2) proceed only partially to the right. These acids are called weak acids and their acid strengths are measured by the values of the acid dissociation constant defined by Eq. (3.3) or (3.4):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& K_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{a\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}\right)}{a(\mathrm{HA})}=\frac{\gamma^{2}\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HA}]} \\
& K_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{a\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right) a(\mathrm{~B})}{a\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+}\right)}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right][\mathrm{B}]}{\left[\mathrm{BH}^{+}\right]}
\end{aligned}
$$

A base ( $\mathrm{A}^{-}$or B ) in water reacts as in Eq. (3.5) or (3.6) and forms its conjugate acid ( HA or $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$) and a hydroxide ion $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{A}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{HA}+\mathrm{OH}^{-} \\
& \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{BH}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$

The strongest base in water is $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$. For bases that are much stronger than $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$, the reactions in Eq. (3.5) or (3.6) proceed completely to the right and form $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$. These bases are called strong bases; their base strengths are equivalent to that of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$and cannot be differentiated. For bases that are weaker than $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$, the reactions in Eq. (3.5) or (3.6) proceed only partially to the right. These bases are called weak bases; their base strengths are compared by the values of the base dissociation constant $K_{\mathrm{b}}$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& K_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{a(\mathrm{HA}) a\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)}{a\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}\right)}=\frac{[\mathrm{HA}]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]} \\
& K_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{a\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)}{a(\mathrm{~B})}=\frac{\gamma^{2}\left[\mathrm{BH}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{B}]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Table 3.2 Temperature effects on the ion product constant of water $\left(\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{w}}=-\log K_{\mathrm{W}}\right)$.
| Temperature, ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ | 0 | 15 | 25 | 35 | 50 | 100 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{W}}$ | 14.943 | 14.346 | 13.996 | 13.680 | 13.262 | 12.31 |


If water molecules interact with each other as an acid and a base, autoprotolysis (autoionization) occurs, as in Eq. (3.7)

$$
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}
$$

The autoprotolysis constant (autoionization constant) of water, $K_{\mathrm{W}}=a\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right) a \left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right) \approx\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$, is often called the ion product constant of water. The value of $-\log K_{\mathrm{W}}$ varies with temperature as shown in Table 3.2. In water, there exists the following relation between $K_{\mathrm{a}}$ of an acid and $K_{\mathrm{b}}$ of its conjugate base:

$$
K_{\mathrm{a}} \times K_{\mathrm{b}}=K_{\mathrm{W}}
$$

Among amphiprotic solvents of high permittivities, there are water-like neutral solvents (e.g. methanol and ethanol), more acidic protogenic solvents (e.g. formic acid) and more basic protophilic solvents (e.g. 2-aminoethanol). There are also amphiprotic mixed solvents, such as mixtures of water and alcohols and water and 1,4-dioxane. The acid-base equilibria in amphiprotic solvents of high permittivity can be treated by methods similar to those in aqueous solutions. If the solvent is expressed by SH , the acid HA or $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$will dissociate as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{HA}+\mathrm{SH} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}+\mathrm{A}^{-} \\
& \mathrm{BH}^{+}+\mathrm{SH} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}+\mathrm{B}
\end{aligned}
$$

The acid dissociation constant is expressed by Eq. (3.8) or (3.9):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& K_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{a\left(\mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}\right)}{a(\mathrm{HA})}=\frac{\gamma^{2}\left[\mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HA}]} \\
& K_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{a\left(\mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}\right) a(\mathrm{~B})}{a\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+}\right)}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}\right][\mathrm{B}]}{\left[\mathrm{BH}^{+}\right]}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the base $\mathrm{A}^{-}$or B , the reactions in Eq. (3.10) or (3.11) occur with solvent SH :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{A}^{-}+\mathrm{SH} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{HA}+\mathrm{S}^{-} \\
& \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{SH} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{BH}^{+}+\mathrm{S}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$

The base dissociation constant $K_{\mathrm{b}}$ is expressed by Eq. (3.12) or (3.13):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& K_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{a(\mathrm{HA}) a\left(\mathrm{~S}^{-}\right)}{a\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}\right)}=\frac{[\mathrm{HA}]\left[\mathrm{S}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]} \\
& K_{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{a\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{~S}^{-}\right)}{a(\mathrm{~B})}=\frac{\gamma^{2}\left[\mathrm{BH}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{S}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{B}]}
\end{aligned}
$$

The autoprotolysis (autoionization) of an amphiprotic solvent SH occurs as in Eq. (3.14), and the autoprotolysis (autoionization) constant, $K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ for pure SH, is defined by Eq. (3.15):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{SH}+\mathrm{SH} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}+\mathrm{S}^{-} \\
& K_{\mathrm{SH}}=a\left(\mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{~S}^{-}\right)=\gamma^{2}\left[\mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{S}^{-}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The strongest acid in solvent SH is $\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}$(lyonium ion), while the strongest base in SH is $\mathrm{S}^{-}$(lyate ion). The strengths of the acids that are much stronger than $\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}$are made equivalent to that of $\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}$, while the strengths of the bases that are much stronger than $\mathrm{S}^{-}$are made equivalent to that of $\mathrm{S}^{-}$. Here, the acid strength of $\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}$ differs from one solvent to another. For example, in formic acid as solvent, $\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}$is very strongly acidic and some strong acids in water behave as weak acids. On the other hand, in 2-aminoethanol as solvent, $\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}$is only weakly acidic, and some weak acids in water behave as strong acids.

It is possible to compare the strengths of weak acids by the values of their acid dissociation constants $K_{\mathrm{a}}$. Figure 3.1 shows the titration curves for acids ( HA or $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$) of different $K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values. The ordinate shows $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$, which is defined by $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}=-\log a\left(\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}\right)$. The $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ corresponds to the pH in aqueous solutions

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-064.jpg?height=852&width=679&top_left_y=1028&top_left_x=219)
Figure 3.1 Calculated titration curves of a strong acid and weak acids of various $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values with a strong base. In the solvent of $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}=24$ and at the acid concentration of $10^{-2} \mathrm{M}$. The effect of activity coefficient and that of dilution was neglected. (The dashed curve is for the case of $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}=14$ (water)).

(see Section 3.2). The $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ of nonaqueous solutions can be measured with a glass pH electrode or some other pH sensors (see Sections 3.2.1 and 6.2). For the mixture of a weak acid $\mathbf{A}$ and its conjugate base $\mathbf{B}, \mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ can be expressed by the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation:

$$
\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{a(\mathbf{B})}{a(\mathbf{A})}\left[=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{a\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}\right)}{a(\mathrm{HA})} \quad \text { or } \quad \mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{a(\mathrm{~B})}{a\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+}\right)}\right]
$$

In Figure 3.1, if the effect of activity coefficient is considered, the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ at the halfneutralization point ( $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H} 1 / 2}$ ) can be expressed by $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H} 1 / 2}=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \gamma$ for $\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{HA}$ and by $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H} 1 / 2}=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}-\log \gamma$ for $\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{BH}^{+} . \gamma$ shows the activity coefficient of $\mathrm{A}^{-}$or $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$. In the solvents of this class, just as in water, the solution containing a weak acid and its conjugate base (or a weak base and its conjugate acid) has a buffer action against the pH variation. The buffer action is maximum at $c(\mathbf{A})=c(\mathbf{B})(c$ : analytical concentration) if $[c(\mathbf{A})+c(\mathbf{B})]$ is kept constant.

If we consider two solvents of different permittivities (I and II), the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ value of an acid $\mathbf{A}$ in the two solvents should differ by the amount expressed by Eq. (3.16), according to the electrostatic Born theory:

$$
\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{II}}-\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{I}}=\frac{0.217 N e^{2}}{R T}\left(\frac{1}{r_{\mathrm{H}}}+\frac{z_{\mathrm{B}}^{2}}{r_{\mathrm{B}}}-\frac{z_{\mathrm{A}}^{2}}{r_{\mathrm{A}}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}, \mathrm{II}}}-\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}, \mathrm{I}}}\right)
$$

Here, A denotes an acid of type $\mathrm{HA}^{-}, \mathrm{HA}$ or $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$, and $z_{i}$ and $r_{i}$ denote the charge and the radius, respectively, of species $i$. The influence of permittivity on $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ depends on the charges, radii and the charge locations of the acid and its conjugate base. Table 3.3 shows the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of some acids and acid-base indicators in water, methanol and ethanol [3]. The solvent effects on $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ are smaller for $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$-type acids than for $\mathrm{HA}^{-}$or HA-type acids. For the $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$-type acids, $z_{\mathrm{A}}=1$ and $z_{\mathbf{B}}=0$ in Eq. (3.16), and the influence of solvent permittivity is expected to be small.

Table 3.3 Relation between the charges of acids and their $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values in water, methanol and ethanol.
| Acids | Charges | $\mathrm{p} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}$ (in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ ) | $\mathbf{p} \boldsymbol{K}_{\mathbf{a}}$ (in MeOH) | $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ (in EtOH) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Acetic acid | 0 | 4.75 | 9.7 | 10.4 |
| Benzoic acid | 0 | 4.20 | 9.4 | 10.1 |
| Salicylic acid | 0 | 2.98 | 7.9 | 8.6 |
| Phenol | 0 | 9.97 | 14.2 | 15.3 |
| Picric acid | 0 | 0.23 | 3.8 | 3.9 |
| Anilinium | +1 | 4.60 | 6.0 | 5.7 |
| Methyl orange | +1 | 3.45 | 3.8 | 3.4 |

Relative permittivities, $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}$, are 78.5 in water, 32.6 in MeOH and 24.6 in EtOH. From p. 130 of Ref. [3].

### 3.1.2 <br> Acid-Base Reactions in Aprotic Solvents of High Permittivity

The solvents of this class are often called dipolar aprotic solvents. They are polar and of very weak acidity (proton donor capacity, hydrogen bond donor capacity and electron pair acceptability). However, with regard to basicity (proton acceptability, hydrogen bond acceptability and electron pair donor capacity), some are stronger than water (protophilic) and some are much weaker than water (protophobic). Examples of protophilic aprotic solvents are DMSO, DMF, DMA, NMP and HMPA; protophobic aprotic solvents include AN, PC, NM and TMS (see Table 1.1 for abbreviations).

The characteristic of acid-base reactions in dipolar aprotic solvents, compared to that in dipolar amphiprotic solvents, is the easy occurrence of homo- and heteroconjugation reactions [ $2,3,5$ ]. However, before discussing the homo- and heteroconjugations, we first discuss the solvent effects on the acid dissociation constants in dipolar aprotic solvents.

## Solvent Effects on Acid Dissociation Constants

Table 3.4 shows the $p K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of various acids in some popular organic solvents and in water [6]. ${ }^{2)}$ Solvent effects on $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values are significant. For example, the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ of acetic acid is 4.76 in water, 12.6 in DMSO and 22.3 in AN. The solvent effects can be predicted by using the transfer activity coefficients for the species participating in the dissociation equilibrium (Section 2.3). The relation between the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of acid HA in solvents R (water in this case) and S is given by Eq. (2.7):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HA}, \mathrm{~S})-\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HA}, \mathrm{R}) \\
& =\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)+\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)-\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(\mathrm{HA}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S})
\end{aligned}
$$

For acetic acid, the transfer activity coefficients of $\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}$and HA are as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{DMSO}: \quad \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)=-3.4, \\
& \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right)=8.8, \quad \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)=-0.8 \\
& \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{AN}: \quad \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)=8.1, \\
& \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right)=10.7, \quad \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)=0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

The solvation of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$is stronger in protophilic DMSO but much weaker in protophobic AN than in amphiprotic water. The solvation of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}$is much weaker in aprotic AN and DMSO than in water (for the strong solvation of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}$in water, see Section 2.2.1). As for the solvation of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$, there is not much difference among the three solvents. From Eq. (2.7) and $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}=4.76$ in water, we get $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}=11.0$
2) In Ref. [6], $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values have been compiled for many acids and conjugate acids of bases in 12 popular dipolar aprotic solvents (Ac, AN, DMF, DMA, DMSO, HMPA, MIBK, NMP, NM, PC,

Py, TMS). The data described for each acid are the conditions and the method for measurement, $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ and homoconjugation constants, and the reference.

In the case of picric acid (HPic), the solvation of $\mathrm{Pic}^{-}$in aprotic solvents is comparable to or slightly stronger than the solvation in water (Table 2.7). Polarizable Pic $^{-}$easily interacts with polarizable aprotic solvents by dispersion forces, while water does not interact as strongly with $\mathrm{Pic}^{-}$by hydrogen bonding because the negative charge of $\mathrm{Pic}^{-}$is delocalized. The $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ of picric acid is 0.38 in water, -1 in DMSO, and 11.0 in AN (Table 3.4). We can attribute most of the difference in $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ to the difference in the solvation of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$.
$\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$acids are also included in Table 3.4. The following relation can predict the solvent effect of those acids on $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+}, \mathrm{S}\right)-\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+}, \mathrm{R}\right)= & \log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)+\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(\mathrm{~B}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}) \\
& -\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+}, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{~S}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Usually, of the three terms on the right-hand side of Eq. (3.17), the first is most important and the influence of the second and third terms is relatively small. However, there are cases when the influence of the second and third terms has an important role. For example, the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ of the anilinium ion is 10.7 in AN and 3.6 in DMSO. Because $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{AN} \rightarrow \mathrm{DMSO}\right)=-11.5$, we get $\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}(\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{AN} \rightarrow$ DMSO) $-\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+}, \mathrm{AN} \rightarrow \mathrm{DMSO}\right)=+4.4$ from Eq. (3.17). This value is not negligible. Generally, $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$is stabilized in basic solvents and B is stabilized in acidic solvents. Thus, we can attribute the above value ( +4.4 ) to the stabilization of the anilinium ion ( $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$) in strongly basic DMSO.

Now, we consider the solvent effects on the mutual relationship between the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of different acids. Figure 3.2 shows the relations between $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ of nonorthosubstituted phenols and the Hammett $\sigma$ values of the substituents [8]. Good linear relations are observed in four solvents. ${ }^{3)}$ It is of special interest that the slopes in AN, DMF and DMSO are almost the same and are nearly 2.0 times the slope in water. Similar linear relations have also been obtained for nonortho-substituted benzoic acids: the slopes in AN, DMSO and DMF are the same and 2.4 times that in water [8]. The effects of substituents on the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ of phenols and benzoic acids are much larger in aprotic solvents than in water. The reason for this is that the anions (conjugate bases) of the acids are hydrated in water by hydrogen bonding: in a series of substituted acids, the acids with higher $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values give anions with higher basicities. Thus, the hydrogen bond between the anions and water is stronger for the acids with higher $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values. The anions are stabilized by the increased strength of the hydrogen bonding,
3) If we plot $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values in one solvent against those in another, we often get a linear relation if the acids are of the same type ( HA or $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$ type). In particular, between two aprotic solvents, the slope of the linear relation is nearly unity. Here, for $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$-type acids in a given aprotic solvent, the difference in $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values is
closely related to the difference in the enthalpies of dissociation, i.e. the difference in the $\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{H}^{+}$bond energies. On the other hand, the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of a given $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$-type acid in different solvents are related to the difference in the entropies of dissociation [9].

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-070.jpg?height=852&width=627&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=209)
Figure 3.2 Relations between the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of nonorthosubstituted phenols and the Hammett $\sigma$ values of the substituents [8]. Substituents: 1, none; 2, 4-chloro; 3, 4-bromo; 4, 3-chloro; 5, 3-trifluoromethyl; 6, 3,4-dichloro; 7, 3,5-dichloro; 8, 3nitro; 9, 3,4,5-trichloro; 10, 4-cyano; 11, 4-nitro; 12, 3,5-dinitro; 13, 3-chloro-4-nitro; 14, 3-trifluoromethyl-4-nitro; 15, 3,4-dinitro.

and the conjugate acids dissociate more easily. Therefore, in water, the hydrogen bonding of water to anions tends to suppress the substituent effect on $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$. In aprotic solvents with poor hydrogen bond donor capacities, such a suppression effect is not observed. This fact is of analytical importance: when two or more acids are to be titrated simultaneously, the resolution is better in aprotic solvents than in water. The resolutions in MeOH and EtOH are between those of water and aprotic solvents.

In Table 3.4, the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 1}$ and $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 2}$ values of some diprotic acids are also shown. For sulfuric acid, o-phthalic acid and malonic acid, the values of ( $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 2}-\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 1}$ ) in dipolar aprotic solvents are much higher than those in water. The large ( $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 2}-\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 1}$ ) values are mainly due to the fact that, for those acids in aprotic solvents, monoanions $\mathrm{HA}^{-}$are highly stabilized by strong intramolecular hydrogen bonding, while dianions $\mathrm{A}^{2-}$ are unstable because two adjacent negative charges, which repel each other, cannot be stabilized by the solvation of aprotic solvents. For dicarboxylic acids, the intramolecular hydrogen bonding of $\mathrm{HA}^{-}$is possible only if the distances between -COOH and $-\mathrm{COO}^{-}$are small enough. Thus, the values of ( $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 2}-\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 1}$ ) in AN and DMSO are 13.2 and 8.7, respectively, for o-phthalic acid, but 3.7 and 2.5, respectively, for $m$-phthalic acid. For more details, see $\operatorname{Refs}[2,5,10]$.

## Homo- and Heteroconjugation Reactions

In dipolar aprotic solvents, the dissociation process of some acids (HA) is complicated by homo- and heteroconjugation reactions. In the homoconjugation reaction, some part of the $\mathrm{A}^{-}$ions formed by the dissociation of HA (Eq. (3.18)) reacts with undissociated HA to form $\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}$, as shown by Eq. (3.19):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{HA} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{A}^{-} \\
& \mathrm{A}^{-}+\mathrm{HA} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{HA}_{2}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$

In the heteroconjugation reaction, $\mathrm{A}^{-}$reacts with an acid other than HA . If we express the acid by HR, the heteroconjugation reaction can be shown by Eq. (3.20) and $\mathrm{AHR}^{-}$is formed:

$$
\mathrm{A}^{-}+\mathrm{HR} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{AHR}^{-}
$$

Here, HR may be water or an acidic impurity in the solvent. Sometimes $\mathrm{A}^{-}$ reacts with more than one molecule of HA or HR to form $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{HA})_{n}{ }^{-}$or $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{HR})_{n}{ }^{-} (n=2,3, \ldots)$. The homo- and heteroconjugations are due to the hydrogen bonding of HA or HR to $\mathrm{A}^{-}{ }^{4)}$

Homo- and heteroconjugation reactions occur when the $\mathrm{A}^{-}$ion is solvated only weakly and is very reactive. This occurs in aprotic solvents when $\mathrm{A}^{-}$is small in size or when the negative charge of $\mathrm{A}^{-}$is localized on a small oxygen atom, as in the case of carboxylate ions. The homo- and heteroconjugation reactions are also influenced by the solvations of HA and HR and are easier in protophobic solvents, in which HA and HR are solvated only weakly. Thus, the homo- and heteroconjugation reactions tend to occur most easily in protophobic aprotic solvents, such as NM, AN and PC. Some examples of the homoconjugation constants, defined by Eq. (3.21), are shown in Table 3.5.

$$
K^{\mathrm{f}}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}^{-}\right)=\frac{a\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}^{-}\right)}{a\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}\right) a(\mathrm{HA})} \approx \frac{\left[\mathrm{HA}_{2}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right][\mathrm{HA}]}
$$

It is apparent from the $\log K^{f}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right)$values that the homoconjugation occurs more easily in protophobic AN and PC than in protophilic DMF and DMSO. ${ }^{5)}$ In protophilic DMF and DMSO, fairly strong solvation of HA tends to suppress the homoconjugation. In the cases of 2,6-dinitrophenol and picric acid, the homoconjugation is almost negligible even in protophobic aprotic solvents (see Table 3.5 for the difference from 3,5-dinitrophenol). In these cases, the anion $\mathrm{A}^{-}$has a delocalized
4) Homoconjugation also occurs with $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$-type acids ( $\mathrm{BH}^{+}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{H}^{+}$). However, the extent of homoconjugation is small or negligible for $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$acids of most amines, although for $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$of some tertiary phosphine oxides ( $\mathrm{R}_{3} \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{O}$ ), homoconjugation occurs to a considerable extent [6].
5) Homoconjugation occurs slightly even in amphiprotic solvents such as water and alcohols. The increase in solubility of benzoic acid in concentrated aqueous solutions of alkali metal benzoate (salting-in) has been explained by a
mechanism involving homoconjugation. In general, homoconjugation increases the solubility of sparingly soluble salts. If HA is added to the saturated solution of sparingly soluble salt MA, the species $\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}$is formed by homoconjugation. Because the radius of $\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}$ is larger than that of $\mathrm{A}^{-}$, the solubility of $\mathrm{MHA}_{2}$ is higher than that of MA, for electrostatic reasons. By measuring the influence of HA on the solubility of MA, it is possible to determine the homoconjugation constant (see Section 7.3.2).

Table 3.5 Homoconjugation constants of acids $\left[\log K^{f}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}\right)\right]$
| Acids | AN | PC | DMF | DMSO |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hydrogen chloride | 2.2 | 4.2 | 2.2 | - |
| Acetic acid | 3.7 | 4 | 2.6 | 1.5 |
| Benzoic acid | 3.6 | 4.0 | 2.4 | 1.8 |
| Salicylic acid | 3.3 | 3.2 | 1.7 | 1.5 |
| $p$-Nitrophenol | 3.7 | 3.6 | 2.2 | 1.8 |
| 3,5-Dinitrophenol | 4.6 | - | 3.2 | 1.5 |
| 2,6-Dinitrophenol | $K^{f} \sim 0$ | - | $K^{f} \sim 0$ | $K^{\mathrm{f}} \sim 0$ |
| Picric acid | 0.3 | $K^{f} \sim 0$ | $K^{f}=0$ | $K^{f}=0$ |


negative charge and is polarizable, and it interacts, by dispersion forces, with an aprotic solvent that is also polarizable. In these cases, the acid HA is also polarizable and is stabilized by interacting with solvent.

The titration curve of a weak acid HA with a strong base (usually $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NOH}$ ) is influenced significantly by homoconjugation (Figure 3.3) [11]. When the homoconjugation is significant, the titration curve is divided into two parts on either side of the half-neutralization point. Before the half-neutralization point, the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ is lower than in the absence of homoconjugation. After the half-neutralization point, however, the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ is higher than in the absence of homoconjugation. This phenomenon is explained as follows: the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ of the solution is determined by Eq. (3.22), irrespective

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-072.jpg?height=762&width=649&top_left_y=1148&top_left_x=214)
Figure 3.3 The effect of homoconjugation on the neutralization titration curve of a weak acid HA . Values of $K^{f}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right)\left(C_{\mathrm{a}}+C_{\mathrm{s}}\right): 0$ for a, 10 for b, $10^{2}$ for c, $10^{3}$ for d and $10^{4}$ for e. The original figure in Ref. [11] was modified.

of the occurrence of homoconjugation:

$$
\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{a\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}\right)}{a(\mathrm{HA})}=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{\gamma\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HA}]}
$$

Before the half-neutralization point, $\mathrm{A}^{-}$formed by the addition of titrant (strong base) reacts with the excess HA to form $\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}$, thus the value of the $\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right] /[\mathrm{HA}]$ ratio is kept lower than in the absence of homoconjugation. On the other hand, after the halfneutralization point, the remaining HA reacts with excess $\mathrm{A}^{-}$to form $\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}$, thus the value of the $\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right] /[\mathrm{HA}]$ ratio is higher than in the absence of homoconjugation. At the half-neutralization point, $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H} 1 / 2}=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \gamma$ irrespective of the occurrence of homoconjugation. From the influence of homoconjugation on the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ titration curve, we can determine the homoconjugation constant. ${ }^{6)}$ A practical procedure is given in Section 6.3.1. Conductimetry can also be applied to determine the homoconjugation constant, as described in Section 7.3.2.

In the absence of homoconjugation, as is usually the case in aqueous solutions, the mixture of a weak acid and its conjugate base or a weak base and its conjugate acid works as a $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}(\mathrm{pH})$ buffer. The buffer capacity is maximum when $c(\mathbf{A})=c(\mathbf{B})$ (see p. 67). In the presence of homoconjugation, however, the problem of the pH buffer is much more complicated. ${ }^{7)}$ Figure 3.4 shows the effect of homoconjugation
6) According to Kolthoff and Chantooni [11], the following relation is obtained, in a good approximation, among $a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right), K_{\mathrm{a}}, K^{f}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right)$, the analytical concentration of $\mathrm{HA}\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{a}} \equiv[\mathrm{HA}]\right. +\left[\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right]$), and that of $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NA}\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}} \equiv\left[\mathrm{A}^{-}\right]\right. \left.+\left[\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right]\right)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)^{2} \gamma^{2} C_{\mathrm{s}}-a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right) \gamma K_{\mathrm{a}}\left\{\left(C_{\mathrm{a}}+C_{\mathrm{s}}\right)\right. \\
& \left.\quad+K^{\mathrm{f}}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}^{-}\right)\left(C_{\mathrm{s}}-C_{\mathrm{a}}\right)^{2}\right\}+K_{\mathrm{a}}^{2} C_{\mathrm{a}}=0
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, we assume that $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \ll C_{\mathrm{s}}, \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NA}$ and $\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NHA}_{2}$ are completely dissociated and the solution is free from acidic or basic impurities that may influence the dissociation equilibrium. If the values of $K_{\mathrm{a}}, K^{f}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right), C_{\mathrm{a}}$ and $C_{\mathrm{s}}$ are known, we can get $a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$by solving the qua-
where $r$ is defined by $r=a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right) \gamma / K_{\mathrm{a}}$ and is equal to the ratio of $a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right) \gamma$ at a given point on the titration curve and that at the half-neutralization point because $K_{\mathrm{a}}=a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)_{1 / 2} \gamma_{1 / 2}$.
7) The buffer capacity ( $\beta$ ) of a solution is defined as the number of moles of strong base needed to increase the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ of 11 of the solution by one unit and can be expressed by

$$
\beta=\mathrm{d} C_{\mathrm{s}} / \mathrm{dp} a_{\mathrm{H}}=-2.3\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left(\mathrm{d} C_{\mathrm{s}} / \mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\right)
$$

Thus, the buffer capacity $\beta$ at each point of the titration curve is inversely proportional to the slope of the tangential line to the curve. When homoconjugation occurs, $\beta$ is expressed approximately by the following relation [11]:

$$
\beta=\frac{-2.3\left\{K_{\mathrm{a}} K^{\mathrm{f}}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}^{-}\right)-\left(C_{\mathrm{a}}-C_{\mathrm{s}}\right)^{2}+K_{\mathrm{a}}\left(C_{\mathrm{a}}+C_{\mathrm{s}}\right)-2 C_{\mathrm{s}}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\right\}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]+4 K_{\mathrm{a}} K^{\mathrm{f}}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}^{-}\right)\left(C_{\mathrm{a}}-C_{\mathrm{s}}\right)^{2}-K_{\mathrm{a}}^{2} /\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}
$$

dratic equation. Figure 3.3 shows the simulated titration curves for various values of $K^{f}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right) \times\left(C_{\mathrm{a}}+C_{\mathrm{s}}\right)$. If we compare the experimental titration curve with the simulated ones (curvefitting method), we can get the value of $K^{f}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right)$ (see Figure 6.6). The value of $K^{f}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right)$can also be obtained by the following relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& K^{\mathrm{f}}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}^{-}\right)=\left\{r^{2} C_{\mathrm{s}}-r\left(C_{\mathrm{a}}+C_{\mathrm{s}}\right)+C_{\mathrm{a}}\right\} / \\
& \left\{r\left(C_{\mathrm{a}}-C_{\mathrm{s}}\right)^{2}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

At the half-neutralization point, however, this relation does not hold and $\beta$ is expressed as follows, if $K^{\mathrm{f}}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right)\left(C_{\mathrm{a}}+C_{\mathrm{s}}\right) \gg 1$ :

$$
\beta_{1 / 2}=(2.3 / 2)\left\{\left(C_{\mathrm{a}}+C_{\mathrm{s}}\right) / 2 K^{\mathrm{f}}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}^{-}\right)\right\}^{1 / 2}
$$

The buffer capacities for various $K^{f}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right) \times\left(C_{\mathrm{a}}+C_{\mathrm{s}}\right)$ values are shown in Figure 3.4 against the values of ( $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}-\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}-\log \gamma$ ), which is shown on the ordinate in Figure 3.3.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-074.jpg?height=783&width=679&top_left_y=198&top_left_x=221)
Figure 3.4 The effect of homoconjugation on the buffer capacity $(\beta)$ of a solution containing HA and $\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NA}$ [11]. Values of $K^{\mathrm{f}}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right)\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{a}}+\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}}\right)$ : 0 for $\mathrm{a}, 1$ for $\mathrm{b}, 10$ for $\mathrm{c}, 10^{2}$ for d and $10^{3}$ for e .

on the buffer capacity ( $\beta$ ) of a buffer consisting of a weak acid HA and its conjugate base $\mathrm{A}^{-}$. On the abscissa of the figure, zero corresponds to the solution of $C_{\mathrm{a}}=C_{\mathrm{s}}$ (see footnote 6 for $C_{\mathrm{a}}$ and $C_{\mathrm{s}}$ ), and the positive and negative sides are for $C_{\mathrm{a}}<C_{\mathrm{s}}$ and $C_{\mathrm{a}}>C_{\mathrm{s}}$, respectively. By the increase in the homoconjugation constant, the buffer capacity at $C_{\mathrm{a}}=C_{\mathrm{s}}$ decreases and the $\beta$ versus $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ curve shows a minimum there. Therefore, to get a good pH buffer in an aprotic solvent, we have to select an acid/ conjugate base couple that is free from homoconjugation. In practice, to calibrate a glass pH electrode in aprotic solvents, a $1: 1$ mixture of picric acid and picrate (usually an $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}$ salt) or a weak base (e.g. aniline, imidazole, guanidine) and its conjugate acid (see footnote 4) is used.

The term heteroconjugation is justified only when the acid HR is much weaker than HA , otherwise the reaction $\mathrm{HR}+\mathrm{A}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{R}^{-}+\mathrm{HA}$ more or less occurs. The most important HR acid is water. The heteroconjugation constants of water to some anions have been determined in AN: for example, $K_{\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{HR})^{-}}^{\mathrm{f}} \approx 0.5$ for picrate, $K_{\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{HR})^{-}}^{\mathrm{f}}=2.3, K_{\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{HR})_{2}{ }^{-}}^{\mathrm{f}}=15.2$ and $K_{\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{HR})_{3}{ }^{-}}^{\mathrm{f}}=10.2$ for benzoate, $K_{\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{HR})^{-}}^{\mathrm{f}}=3.6$ and $K_{\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{HR})_{2}}^{\mathrm{f}}=8.0$ for methanesulfonate [12].

If water is added to a pH buffer in a dipolar aprotic solvent, the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ of the buffer is affected. Generally, if the buffer is in a protophobic aprotic solvent and consists of an acid/conjugate base couple that easily homoconjugates, the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ of the buffer is influenced considerably by the addition of hydrogen bond donors or acceptors. Here again, the effects of homo- and heteroconjugations are important. Figure 3.5a shows

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-075.jpg?height=744&width=853&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=352)
Figure 3.5 Effects of the addition of proton donors and acceptors on the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ of the benzoic acid-benzoate buffers in AN [12].
Solutions: (a) $3.6 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{HBz}+30.5 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NBz}$ and (b) 65 mM $\mathrm{HBz}+2.18 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NBz}$ (see text).

the effects of hydrogen bond donors (water, $\mathrm{MeOH}, \mathrm{BuOH}, p$-bromophenol) and acceptor (DMSO) on the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ of the [ 3.6 mM benzoic acid ( HBz ) $+30.5 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NBz}$ ] solution in $\mathrm{AN}\left(\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}=23.4\right)[12]$. The addition of hydrogen bond donors (HR) decreases the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ of the solution. The decrease in $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ is due to the decrease in the concentration of free $\mathrm{Bz}^{-}$by the heteroconjugation reaction $\mathrm{Bz}^{-}+\mathrm{HR} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Bz} (\mathrm{HR})^{-}$. The influence of DMSO, which is a poor hydrogen bond donor, is very small. Figure 3.5b shows the influence on the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ of the $(65 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{HBz}+2.18 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NBz}$ ) solution in AN ( $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}=16.8$ ). Because $\mathrm{Bz}^{-}$exists as $\mathrm{Bz}(\mathrm{HBz})^{-}$, by the homoconjugation with HBz , weak hydrogen bond donors such as water and alcohols cannot influence $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$. Only $p$-bromophenol, a strong hydrogen bond donor, competes with HBz in reacting with $\mathrm{Bz}^{-}$and decreases $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ (for $\mathrm{A}^{-}$of benzoate and HR of $p$-bromophenol, $K_{\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{HR})^{-}}^{\mathrm{f}}=3.6 \times 10^{2}$ and $K_{\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{HR})_{2}}^{\mathrm{f}}=3.9 \times 10^{3}$, while $K_{\mathrm{HA}_{2}}^{\mathrm{f}}=4.0 \times 10^{3}$ ). DMSO, a hydrogen bond acceptor, increases $\mathrm{p} a \mathrm{H}$, because it reacts with the free HBz and decreases its activity.

On the other hand, the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ of picric acid/picrate buffers in AN are not influenced much by water and alcohols of up to 0.5 M , because in buffers, homo- and heteroconjugation reactions do not occur appreciably [12]. In protophilic DMSO, the influences of hydrogen bond donors and acceptors on the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ of buffers are smaller than in protophobic AN. DMSO solvates to hydrogen bond donor HR, and suppresses the heteroconjugation, while DMSO suppresses the influence of a hydrogen bond acceptor by competing with it.

### 3.1.3 <br> Acid-Base Reactions in Amphiprotic Solvents of Low Permittivity

$t$-Butanol ( $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=12$, neutral), acetic acid ( $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=6.2$, protogenic) and ethylenediamine ( $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=12.9$, protophilic) are examples of amphiprotic solvents of low permittivity [3,4]. In these solvents, an acid HA is ionized rather easily and forms an ion pair because the solvents can work both as an electron pair donor and acceptor:

$$
\mathrm{HA} \rightleftarrows\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}\right)_{\text {solv }}
$$

However, because of the low solvent permittivities, the dissociation of the ion pair to free ions is difficult. Thus, even a strong acid in water behaves as a weak acid, e.g. the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ of $\mathrm{HClO}_{4}$ is 3.9 in $t$-butanol, 4.9 in acetic acid, and 3.1 in ethylenediamine.

In these solvents, ion pairs are also formed by the interactions between a base B and the solvent SH (Eq. (3.23)) and by the acid-base reaction between an acid HA and a base B (Eq. (3.24)):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{SH} \rightleftarrows\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+}, \mathrm{S}^{-}\right)_{\text {solv }} \\
& \mathrm{HA}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftarrows\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+}, \mathrm{A}^{-}\right)_{\text {solv }}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here again, the dissociation of ion pairs to free ions does not occur appreciably because of the low permittivities. In these solvents, even tetraalkylammonium salts can dissociate only slightly, as shown by the low dissociation constant ( $1.6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{M}$ ) of $\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NPic}$ in acetic acid (see also Section 2.6).

For acid-base equilibria in acetic acid, the original reports by Kolthoff and Bruckenstein are important [13]. Reference [4] provides a comprehensive review of acid-base equilibria in amphiprotic solvents.

### 3.1.4 <br> Acid-Base Reactions in Aprotic Solvents of Low Permittivity

In Section 1.2, some aprotic solvents of low permittivity (Py, THF, diethyl ether, MIBK) were classified as dipolar aprotic solvents because they have some basicity and behave like polar solvents. Therefore, the solvents considered here are aliphatic or aromatic hydrocarbons and halogenated hydrocarbons that are classified as inert solvents [3,14]. The solvents of this class interact only very weakly with acids and bases, and the ionization and dissociation of acids do not occur appreciably in them. However, if both an acid (HA) and a base (B) are added to the solvent, a salt BHA is formed by the neutralization reaction

$$
\mathrm{HA}+\mathrm{B} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{BHA}
$$

The salt is formed by hydrogen bonding and is usually in a polymer state $(\mathrm{BHA})_{n}$. The bond between BH and A is ionized to various degrees, from nonionized ( $\mathrm{BH} \ldots \mathrm{A})_{n}$ to completely ionized $\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+} \ldots \mathrm{A}^{-}\right)_{n}$. The ionization becomes more pronounced with increasing acid-base interactions. When acid HA forms a dimer $(\mathrm{HA})_{2}$ in the inert solvent, as in the case of carboxylic acids in benzene, the salts denoted by $\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{HA})_{2} \left[\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+} \ldots \mathrm{A}^{-} \ldots \mathrm{HA}\right),(\mathrm{AH} \ldots \mathrm{B} \ldots \mathrm{HA})\right.$, etc. $]$ are formed.

The formation of the salt BHA can be detected quantitatively by such methods as potentiometry, conductimetry, UV/vis and IR spectroscopies, dielectric polarization measurement, and differential vapor pressure measurement. If the equilibrium constant of Eq. (3.25) is expressed by $K=[\mathrm{BHA}] /([\mathrm{HA}][\mathrm{B}])$, we get

$$
\log ([\mathrm{BHA}] /[\mathrm{HA}])=\log K+\log [\mathrm{B}] \text { or } \log ([\mathrm{BHA}] /[\mathrm{B}])=\log K+\log [\mathrm{HA}]
$$

By plotting the relation of $\log [\mathrm{BHA}] /[\mathrm{HA}]$ versus $\log [\mathrm{B}]$ or $\log [\mathrm{BHA}] /[\mathrm{B}]$ versus $\log [\mathrm{HA}]$, a linear relation of unit slope can be obtained, and the intercept corresponds to the value of log $K$. In fact, the values of log $K$ have been obtained for various acid-base couples in inert solvents [ 3,14 ] and they are usually between 2 and 7 . If the values of log $K$ are obtained for various bases using a given acid as reference, it is possible to compare the strengths of the bases. On the other hand, if the values of $\log K$ are obtained for various acids using a given base as reference, it is possible to compare the strengths of the acids.

## 3.2 <br> pH Scales in Nonaqueous Solutions

### 3.2.1 <br> Definition of pH in Nonaqueous Solutions

pH , defined by $\mathrm{pH}=-\log a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$, is widely used as a measure of acid-base properties of aqueous solutions. However, the term ' pH ' is also used as a measure of acid-base properties of nonaqueous solutions. ${ }^{8)}$

In 1985, the IUPAC Commission of Electroanalytical Chemistry defined the pH for solutions in organic solvents of high permittivity and in water-organic solvent mixtures [15]. According to them, the pH is notionally defined by Eq. (3.26), where $m$ shows the molality and $\gamma$ the activity coefficient:

$$
\mathrm{pH}=-\log a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)=-\log \left\{m\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right) \gamma\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right) / m^{0}\right\}
$$

However, because $a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$is a single ion activity, which is thermodynamically indeterminable, an operational pH definition is given as outlined in (1) and (2) below:

1. The pH values of the solutions X and S in solvent s and the emfs of cells (I) and (II), $E(\mathrm{~S})$ and $E(\mathrm{R})$, are related by Eq. (3.27).

Reference electrode|salt bridge in solvent $\mathrm{s}_{\|}^{\|}$solution X in solvent $\mathrm{s}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \mathrm{Pt}$ (I)
Reference electrode|salt bridge in solvent $\mathrm{s}_{\|}^{\|}$solution S in solvent $\mathrm{s}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \mathrm{Pt} \quad$ (II)
8) In Section 3.1, we dealt with the $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ value, defined by $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}=-\log a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$, for solutions in amphiprotic and aprotic solvents of high permittivity. Hereafter, the symbol pH is used instead of $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$.

$$
\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{X})=\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{~S})+\frac{E(\mathrm{~S})-E(\mathrm{X})}{\ln 10 \times(R T / F)}
$$

Here, X is the test solution and S is the standard solution. A pH glass electrode may be used instead of the hydrogen electrode in cells (I) and (II).
2. $0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}{ }^{-1}$ potassium hydrogen phthalate ( KHPh ) in solvent s is used as the most fundamental pH buffer (Reference Value pH Standard, RVS) and the reference pH values $\left(\mathrm{pH}_{\mathrm{RVS}}\right)$ are assigned to it. Other pH buffers can also be used as primary standards if pH values are appropriately assigned to them. In assigning pH values to RVS and the primary standards, an absolute method, which uses the Harned cell and the Bates-Guggenheim convention, is employed.

The above notional and operational pH definitions for solutions in nonaqueous and mixed solvents are the copy of the IUPAC Recommendations 1985 for aqueous solutions [16a]. These recommendations for aqueous solutions were, however, criticized and were replaced by the new one (Recommendations 2002) [16b]. The differences between Recommendations 1985 and 2002 are explained in Section 6.2.1; one of them is that the Reference Value pH Standard ( $0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \mathrm{KHPh}$ ) in Recommendation 1985 is not special but is treated as one of the primary standards in Recommendations 2002. Here, the pH values for the primary standards in Recommendations 2002 are the same as those for the RVS and the primary standards in Recommendations 1985.

Application of Recommendations 2002 to nonaqueous and mixed solvents has been tried, as described in Section 6.2.2 [15c]. However, in this case also, the pH values for the standard solutions do not differ between the two recommendations. Thus, we can use the standard solutions for Recommendations 1985. At present, pH values for $0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \mathrm{KHPh}$ and some other primary standards have been reported for $100 \%$ formamide and the mixtures between water and 12 organic solvents (see footnote 6 in Section 6.2) [17]. When these standard solutions are available, pH can be determined accurately with a pH meter and a glass electrode, just as in aqueous solutions.

However, difficult problems arise there when the IUPAC method is applied to the solutions in neat organic solvents or water-poor mixed solvents. For example, in these solvents often the KHPh is not soluble enough and the buffer action of KHPh is too low in solutions of aprotic nature [18]. ${ }^{9)}$ Another problem is that the response of the glass electrode is often very slow in nonaqueous solvents, ${ }^{\mathbf{1 0}}$ although this has been considerably improved by the use of pH -ISFETs [19]. These difficulties for pH measurements in nonaqueous and mixed solvents are dealt with in detail in Section 6.2 , with the practical methods and their applications.
9) The low buffer capacities of the KHPh solution in solvents of aprotic nature is caused by the increase in the ( $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 2}-\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 1}$ ) value of phthalic acid. In $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{DMF}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$-DMSO mixtures, the buffer capacity of $0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \mathrm{KHPh}$ is not enough if the water content is less than $\sim 30 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{v} \%$ [18].
10) The glass electrode responds especially slowly in protophilic aprotic solvents such as DMSO and DMF, sometimes taking 1 h to reach a steady potential. In such cases, the use of $\mathrm{Si}_{3} \mathrm{~N}_{4}$ - and $\mathrm{Ta}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$-type pH-ISFETs is very promising because they almost respond instantaneously and with Nernstian or nearNernstian slopes [19].

### 3.2.2 <br> pH Windows in Nonaqueous Solvents and pH Scales Common to Multisolvents

## pH Windows in Nonaqueous Solvents

In aqueous solutions, the pH of an acid solution in which $a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)=1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ or $1 \mathrm{moll}^{-1}(=\mathrm{M})$, depending on the definition [16], is equal to zero. Because the ion product constant of water, $K_{\mathrm{W}}\left[=a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)\right]$, is equal to $10^{-14} \mathrm{M}^{2}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the pH of the solution of $a\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)=1 \mathrm{M}$ is equal to 14 . Of course, there exist aqueous solutions of $\mathrm{pH}<0$ or $\mathrm{pH}>14$. But it is reasonable to assume that the pH window of water is approximately between 0 and 14 and its width is $\sim 14\left(=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{W}}\right)$.

In an amphiprotic solvent SH , the autoprotolysis occurs as in Eq. (3.28), and the autoprotolysis constant $K_{\mathrm{SH}}\left[=a\left(\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{~S}^{-}\right)\right]$is constant at a given temperature:

$$
\mathrm{SH}+\mathrm{SH} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}+\mathrm{S}^{-}
$$

If the activity of $\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}$or the pH is known, the activity of the lyate ion $\mathrm{S}^{-}$is obtained by the relation $a\left(\mathrm{~S}^{-}\right)=K_{\mathrm{SH}} / a\left(\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}\right)$. Some $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ values in pure and mixed solvents are listed in Tables 3.4 and 6.6. Most of the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ values in amphiprotic solvents are less than 20. Therefore, the widths of the pH windows are usually less than 20 in amphiprotic solvents.

In most aprotic solvents, which have weak proton donor capacities, it is difficult to get stable lyate ions. The lyate ion in DMSO (dimsyl ion, $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{SOCH}_{2}{ }^{-}$) is somewhat stable. Alkali metal dimsyls in DMSO are strongly basic and have been used as titrants (Ref. [5], p. 356), but this is rather exceptional. Generally, aprotic solvents do not undergo appreciable autoprotolysis. It is especially true for protophobic aprotic solvents, which have very weak proton donating and accepting abilities. Efforts to determine $K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ values have been made even in such solvents. However, if a small amount of water is present as an impurity in such solvents, the $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions formed from water may complicate the autoprotolysis process. The $K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ values determined may not be the product $a\left(\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{~S}^{-}\right)$but the product $a\left(\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$ or even $a\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$(see Section 6.2.3). Moreover, $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion added as $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NOH}$ cannot remain stable because of Hoffmann degradation ( $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NOH}$ forms alkene, $\mathrm{R}_{3} \mathrm{~N}$ and water). Impurities other than water may also affect the $K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ values. Thus, it is very difficult to get true $K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ values in aprotic solvents and the literature data highly vary [6]. The $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ values for aprotic solvents are usually higher than 20 (Tables 3.4 and 6.6) and, especially for some protophobic aprotic solvents, they may approach $\sim 40$. Thus, the pH windows are much wider in aprotic solvents than in water (see below).

In aprotic solvents, in which $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ values are not definitive, it is difficult to estimate the activity of the lyate ion from the pH of the solution. It is different from the case in water, in which $a\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$can be estimated from the pH value. Although the pH scale in aprotic solvents has such a disadvantage, it is still useful to quantitatively understand the acid-base aspects of chemical phenomena. Wider use of the pH concept in nonaqueous solutions is desirable.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-080.jpg?height=884&width=783&top_left_y=191&top_left_x=207)
Figure 3.6 pH windows in various solvents shown by a common pH scale. The pH scale in water is used as reference. The values in parentheses correspond to $-\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{W} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ and $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$, respectively.

## pH Scales Common to Multisolvents

In the previous section, the pH of the solution of $a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)=1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ was defined equal to zero in each solvent. ${ }^{11)}$ However, the solvation of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$differs from one solvent to another and, even in a solution of $\mathrm{pH}=0$, the reactivity of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$differs drastically by solvent. In order to compare the acid-base properties in different solvents, it is convenient to define a pH scale that is common to various solvents [20]. Figure 3.6 shows the pH windows in various solvents in such a common pH scale, using the pH in water as reference. In the figure, the left margin of the pH window corresponds to the value of $-\log \gamma_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}, \mathrm{W} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)$ (see Table 2.7) and the width of the window corresponds to the value of $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$. If the solvent is of weaker basicity than water, the pH window expands to the left (more acidic) side than water. On the other hand, if the solvent is of weaker acidity than water, the pH window expands to the right (more basic) side than water.

These expanded pH windows open up various chemical possibilities:
11) If the density of the solvent is between 0.8 and $1.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$, the difference between the pH on molality and that on molarity is within 0.1 .
(i) In solvents with a wide pH region on the left, the solvated protons $\left(\mathrm{SH}_{2}{ }^{+}\right)$have a very strong acidity and some acids, which behave as strong acids in water, tend to behave as weak acids of different strengths. (The term differentiating solvent is used for a solvent that can differentiate the strengths of acids (or bases) that are equivalent in water.) Thus, they can be determined separately by titration. Moreover, in such a solvent, some bases, which are too weak to titrate in water, can be titrated and their strengths can be determined.
(ii) In solvents with a wide pH region on the right, the lyate ion ( $\mathrm{S}^{-}$) has very strong basicity and some bases, which behave as strong bases in water, tend to behave as weak bases of different strengths. They can be determined separately by titration. Moreover, in such a solvent, some acids, which are too weak to titrate in water, can be titrated and their strengths can be determined.

In protophobic aprotic solvents, the pH window expands on both sides and all of the advantages in (i) and (ii) can be realized. Figure 3.7 shows the titration curve for a mixture of several acids in 4-methyl-2-pentanone (MIBK). In the figure, $\mathrm{HClO}_{4}$ and HCl , which are strong acids in water, are differentiated, and phenol, which is a very weak acid, can accurately be determined [21]. Acid-base titrations in nonaqueous solvents have wide applicability [ $1,3,22$ ]. The titrations are especially useful in determining medicinal substances because many of them are weak acids or bases. The indicator method can be used to detect end points. However, potentiometric

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-081.jpg?height=822&width=601&top_left_y=1132&top_left_x=352)
Figure 3.7 Potentiometric titration curve of a mixture of acids in MIBK [21]. Titrated with $0.2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NOH}$ using a glass electrode-Pt electrode system.

| DMF | $5.5\left(6.1{ }^{b}\right)$ | 7.0 (7.8 ${ }^{c}$ ) |
| DMSO | 5.4 (5.8 ${ }^{b}$ ) | $8.2\left(9.3^{c}\right)$ |

| DMA | 7.3 | 10.0 |
| HMPA | 8.0 | 11.0 |


$\beta_{1}=\left[\mathrm{BH}^{+}\right] /\left([\mathrm{B}]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\right), \beta_{2}=\left[\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{H}^{+}\right] /\left([\mathrm{B}]^{2}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\right)$.
${ }^{a}$ From Ref. [23a] (Izutsu and Hiraoka) except ${ }^{b}$ and ${ }^{c}$.
${ }^{b}$ By Kolthoff et al. [23b]
${ }^{c}$ By Ugo et al. [23c]
titrations that use a pH meter, a pH sensor and an automatic burette are most useful. Valuable physicochemical information, including acid dissociation constants, can be obtained from potentiometric titration curves (Chapter 6).

The hydrogen ion in protophobic aprotic solvents is very reactive. For example, judging from the values of transfer activity coefficient, $\mathrm{H}^{+}$is $10^{8}$ times more reactive in AN than in water. Thus, if basic substances are added to the solution in AN, they easily combine with $\mathrm{H}^{+}$. Table 3.6 shows the complex formation constants of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ with some basic (donor) solvents [23]. Complexation of up to two solvent molecules occurs by the addition of small amounts of donor solvents. ${ }^{\mathbf{1 2}}$ If water is added as donor solvent to the $\mathrm{H}^{+}$solution in AN, up to four water molecules are combined with $\mathrm{H}^{+}$. The formation constants for the reactions $\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{W} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{W}}{ }^{+}, \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{~W} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{~W}}{ }^{+}, \mathrm{H}^{+}+3 \mathrm{~W} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{H}_{3 \mathrm{~W}}{ }^{+}$and $\mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{~W} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{H}_{4 \mathrm{~W}}{ }^{+}$have been determined spectrophotometrically using Hammett indicators [24]. They are $\log \beta\left(\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{W}}{ }^{+}\right)=2.2$, $\log \beta\left(\mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{~W}}{ }^{+}\right)=3.9, \log \beta\left(\mathrm{H}_{3 \mathrm{~W}}{ }^{+}\right)=4.8$ and $\log \beta\left(\mathrm{H}_{4 \mathrm{~W}}{ }^{+}\right)=5.3$, respectively. Figure 3.8 shows the distribution of each species as a function of free water concentration (i.e. total $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ minus $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ combined to $\mathrm{H}^{+}$). It is apparent that even when the concentration of free water is as low as 0.01 M , the fraction of free $\mathrm{H}^{+}$is only about $30 \%$ and, if the concentration of free water is 1 M , more than half of the $\mathrm{H}^{+}$exists as $\mathrm{H}_{4 \mathrm{~W}}{ }^{+}$. Moreover, $\mathrm{H}^{+}$in AN gradually reacts with the AN molecules and loses its reactivity. The $\mathrm{H}^{+}$in other protophobic aprotic solvents also tends to react with water, with other basic impurities and with the solvent itself. Sulfolane (TMS) is said to be the only protophobic solvent that does not react appreciably with $\mathrm{H}^{+}$. To get a wide pH window on the acid side (the left side in Figure 3.6), the solvent must be free of water and other basic impurities, and the acid solution must be prepared fresh, just before measurement. Electrolytic (or coulometric) generation of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$is convenient for this purpose: dry $\mathrm{H}^{+}$can be obtained, for example, by anodic oxidation of hydrogen absorbed into a palladium electrode [25].

To get a wide pH window on the basic side (the right side in Figure 3.6), the solvent must be aprotic and free of water and other acidic impurities. Because stable lyate
12) When $\mathrm{H}^{+}$in AN is titrated with a protophilic solvent as in Table 3.6, a clear pH jump occurs on the titration curve at the equivalence point.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-083.jpg?height=598&width=864&top_left_y=198&top_left_x=357)
Figure 3.8 Step-wise complexation of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$in AN with water, indicated as a function of the free water concentration [24].

ions cannot be obtained in most aprotic solvents, $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NOH}(\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{Et}$ or Bu$)$ is often used as a strong base for titration. Alkali metal hydroxides are insoluble and are not applicable in aprotic solvents. However, $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NOH}$ in aprotic solvents is also unstable because it is decomposed (Hoffmann degradation). Therefore, while titrating the acids in aprotic solvents, the standard solution of $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NOH}$ in methanol, (toluene + methanol) or 2-propanol is used as a titrant. In that case, some amount of alcohol is introduced with the titrant and may decrease the width of the pH window. There is also gradual decomposition of the added $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NOH}$. These factors must be kept in mind when carrying out pH titrations in aprotic solvents.

## 4

## Redox Reactions in Nonaqueous Solvents

This chapter deals with the fundamental aspects of redox reactions in nonaqueous solutions. In Section 4.1, we discuss solvent effects on the potentials of various types of redox couples and on reaction mechanisms. Solvent effects on redox potentials are important in connection with the electrochemical studies of such basic problems as ion solvation and electronic properties of chemical species. We then consider solvent effects on reaction kinetics, paying attention to the role of dynamic solvent properties in electron-transfer (ET) processes. In Section 4.2, we deal with the potential windows in various solvents to show the advantages of nonaqueous solvents as media for redox reactions. In Section 4.3, we describe some examples of practical redox titrations in nonaqueous solvents. Because many of the redox reactions are realized as electrode reactions, the subjects covered in this chapter will also appear in Part Two in connection with electrochemical measurements.

## 4.1 <br> Solvent Effects on Various Types of Redox Reactions

### 4.1.1 <br> Fundamentals of Redox Reactions

A redox reaction includes an electron-transfer process. For example, if $\mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$ is added to an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$, a redox reaction, $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{Ce}^{4+} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+\mathrm{Ce}^{3+}$, occurs and $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ (reducing agent) is oxidized to $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$, donating an electron to $\mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$. On the other hand, $\mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$ (oxidizing agent) is reduced to $\mathrm{Ce}^{3+}$, gaining an electron from $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$. The oxidation of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ to $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ also occurs at an electrode kept at appropriate potentials. In this case, the electrode gains an electron from $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$. In redox reactions, electron transfer occurs with the aid of either oxidizing or reducing agents or with the aid of electrodes.

The redox properties of a solution are expressed quantitatively by the redox potential, which is measured as the potential of an inert redox electrode (e.g. platinum electrode) immersed in the solution under study. Thus, for an aqueous solution containing the oxidized and reduced forms (Ox, Red) of the reaction Ox $+n \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \operatorname{Red}$, the redox potential is equal to the emf of cell (I):

$$
\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\left(p=p^{0}\right)\right| \mathrm{H}^{+}(a=1)(\mathrm{w}){ }_{\|} \mathrm{Ox}\left(a_{\mathrm{Ox}}\right), \operatorname{Red}\left(a_{\text {Red }}\right)(\mathrm{w}) \mid \mathrm{Pt}
$$

The electrode on the left of the junction (\#) is the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE), the potential of which is defined as zero at all temperatures. $P^{0}$ is the standard pressure and is equal to $10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$ ( 1 bar ) by the IUPAC Recommendation (1982). ${ }^{\mathbf{1}}$ The liquid junction potential at ${ }_{\|}^{\|}$is kept negligible by an appropriate salt bridge. The potential of the electrode on the right of the junction is expressed by the Nernst equation:

$$
E=E^{0}+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{a_{\mathrm{Ox}}}{a_{\mathrm{Red}}}
$$

where $a_{\mathrm{Ox}}$ and $a_{\text {Red }}$ are the activities of Ox and Red, respectively, and $E^{0}$ is the standard redox potential, which is equal to the redox potential at $a_{\mathrm{Ox}}=a_{\mathrm{Red}}=1 . E^{0}$ is related to the equilibrium constant $K$ of reaction (4.2) by $E^{0}=(R T / F) \ln K$.

$$
(1 / n) \mathrm{Ox}+(1 / 2) \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftarrows(1 / n) \operatorname{Red}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \quad K=\frac{a_{\operatorname{Red}^{+}}^{1 / n} a_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}}{a_{\mathrm{Ox}}^{1 / n} a_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}^{1 / 2}}
$$

Each redox couple has its own $E^{0}$ value. If we rewrite Eq. (4.1) using the concentrations of Ox and Red, we get

$$
E=E^{0^{\prime}}+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{[\mathrm{Ox}]}{[\mathrm{Red}]}
$$

$E^{0^{\prime}}$ is the potential at $[\mathrm{Ox}]=[\mathrm{Red}]$ and is often called the formal potential. The relation between $E^{0}$ and $E^{0^{\prime}}$ is expressed by $E^{0^{\prime}}=E^{0}+(R T / n F) \ln \left(\gamma_{\mathrm{Ox}} / \gamma_{\text {Red }}\right)$, where $\gamma$ denotes the activity coefficient.

We now consider the titration of $\operatorname{Red}_{1}$ with an oxidizing agent $\mathrm{Ox}_{2}$, where the oxidation of $\operatorname{Red}_{1}$ proceeds by $\operatorname{Red}_{1} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Ox}_{1}+n_{1} \mathrm{e}^{-}$and the reduction of $\mathrm{Ox}_{2}$ by $\mathrm{Ox}_{2}+n_{2} \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \operatorname{Red}_{2}$. The titration reaction is expressed by Eq. (4.4).

$$
n_{2} \operatorname{Red}_{1}+n_{1} \mathrm{Ox}_{2} \rightleftarrows n_{2} \mathrm{Ox}_{1}+n_{1} \operatorname{Red}_{2}
$$

In Figure 4.1, the titration curve passes I (before the $100 \%$ titration) and I' (after the $100 \%$ titration) if $n_{1}=n_{2}=1$, II and II' if $n_{1}=n_{2}=2$, I and II' if $n_{1}=1$ and $n_{2}=2$ and II and I' if $n_{1}=2$ and $n_{2}=1$. In all cases, the potential is equal to the formal potential of the $\mathrm{Ox}_{1} / \operatorname{Red}_{1}$ couple ( $E_{1}^{0^{\prime}}$ ) at $50 \%$ titration, while it is equal to the formal potential of the $\mathrm{Ox}_{2} / \operatorname{Red}_{2}$ couple ( $E_{2}^{0^{\prime}}$ ) at $200 \%$ titration. If we consider, for simplicity, the case of $n_{1}=n_{2}=n$, the equilibrium constant of reaction (4.4), given by $K=a_{\mathrm{Ox}_{1}} a_{\operatorname{Red}_{2}} /\left(a_{\operatorname{Red}_{1}} a_{\mathrm{Ox}_{2}}\right)$, is related to $E_{1}^{0}$ and $E_{2}^{0}$ by $K=\exp \left[(n F / R T)\left(E_{2}^{0}-E_{1}^{0}\right)\right]$. The increase in the value of ( $E_{2}^{0}-E_{1}^{0}$ ) increases the value of $K$, and the redox reaction (4.4) proceeds more completely to the right.

Some typical redox couples and metal ion-metal couples are shown below, with the corresponding Nernst equations. The effects of solvents on these reactions will be discussed in the next section.

1) Before 1982, $p^{0}=101325 \mathrm{~Pa}$ ( 1 atm ) was used. The difference in the standard pressure has a negligible influence on the value of standard potential, as shown by $E^{0}(101325 \mathrm{~Pa})=E^{0} \left(10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}\right)+0.17 \mathrm{mV}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-087.jpg?height=689&width=635&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=207)
Figure 4.1 Curves for the potentiometric titration of $\operatorname{Red}_{1}$ with oxidizing agent $\mathrm{Ox}_{2}$. Before the $100 \%$ titration, curve I is for $n_{1}=1$ and II for $n_{1}=2$, where $n_{1}$ is for $\mathrm{Ox}_{1}+n_{1} \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \operatorname{Red}{ }_{1}$. After $100 \%$ titration, curve $\mathrm{I}^{\prime}$ is for $n_{2}=1$ and $\mathrm{II}^{\prime}$ for $n_{2}=2$, where $n_{2}$ is for $\mathrm{Ox}_{2}+n_{2} \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \operatorname{Red}_{2}$ (see text).

1. $\mathrm{M}^{n+}+n \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{M}$ (M : metal)

$$
E=E_{\mathrm{a}}^{0}+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln a\left(\mathrm{M}^{n+}\right)
$$

2. $\mathrm{M}^{n+}+n \mathrm{e}^{-}+\mathrm{Hg} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{Hg}) \quad(\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{Hg}):$ metal amalgam $)$

$$
E=E_{\mathrm{b}}^{0}+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{a\left(\mathrm{M}^{n+}\right) a(\mathrm{Hg})}{a(\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{Hg}))}
$$

3. $\mathrm{Ox}+n \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \operatorname{Red}$

$$
E=E_{\mathrm{c}}^{0}+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{a_{\mathrm{Ox}}}{a_{\mathrm{Red}}}
$$

4. $\mathrm{Ox}+m \mathrm{H}^{+}+n \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \operatorname{Red}$

$$
E=E_{\mathrm{d}}^{0}+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{a_{\mathrm{Ox}}}{a_{\mathrm{Red}}}+\frac{m R T}{n F} \ln a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)
$$

(Hydrogen ions participate in the reduction, and the potential is influenced by the pH of the solution; the potential shift being $-59.16(\mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{n}) \mathrm{mV} / \mathrm{pH}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.)
5. $\mathrm{ML}_{p}^{(n+p b)+}+n \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{M}+p \mathrm{~L}^{b} \quad\left(\mathrm{ML}_{p}^{(n+p b)+}\right.$ :metal comples; $b$ : zero or negative)

$$
E=E_{\mathrm{e}}^{0}+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{a\left(\mathrm{ML}_{p}^{(n+p b)+}\right)}{a\left(L^{b}\right)^{p}}, \quad E_{\mathrm{e}}^{0}=E_{\mathrm{a}}^{0}-\frac{R T}{n F} \ln K
$$

( $K$ is the complex formation constant and $K=a\left(\mathrm{ML}_{p}^{(n+p b)+}\right) /\left\{a\left(\mathrm{M}^{n+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{~L}^{b}\right)^{p}\right\}$. With the increase in $K, E_{\mathrm{e}}^{0}$ shifts to the negative direction.)

| Metals | $E^{0}\left(\mathrm{M}^{n+} / \mathrm{M}\right)$ | $\mathrm{E}^{0}\left(\mathrm{M}^{n+} / \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{Hg})\right)$ |

| Na | -2.717 (V versus SHE) | -1.959 (V versus SHE) |
