${ }^{a}$ Ref. [27a]. $\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$.
activity $a(i)$, we can determine the change by measuring the change in $V_{\mathrm{G}}$. The advantage of the ISFET is that it is easy to miniaturize. ISFETs have been developed for various ions. Among them, pH -ISFETs with a gate of $\mathrm{Si}_{3} \mathrm{~N}_{4}, \mathrm{Ta}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ or $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ are commercially available, combined with the $\mathrm{pH} / \mathrm{mV}$ meters for use with them. Recently, pH -ISFETs have been used in routine pH measurements in aqueous solutions. Very promising features of pH -ISFETs in nonaqueous solutions will be described in Chapter 6.

Reference electrodes The primary reference electrode for aqueous solutions is the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE), expressed by $\mathrm{H}^{+}(a=1)\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\left(p=10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}\right)\right|$ Pt (see footnote 1 in Section 4.1). Its potential is defined as zero at all temperatures. In practical measurements, however, other reference electrodes that are easier to handle are used [27]. Examples of such reference electrodes are shown in Table 5.4, with their potentials against the SHE. All of them are electrodes of the second kind. The saturated calomel electrode (SCE) used to be widely used, but today the saturated silver-silver chloride electrode is the most popular.

### 5.7.2 <br> Potentiometric Titrations

Acid-base, redox, precipitation and chelometric titrations are usually dealt with in textbooks on analytical chemistry. The titration curves in these titrations can be obtained potentiometrically by using appropriate indicator electrodes, i.e. a pH glass electrode or pH -ISFET for acid-base titrations, a platinum electrode for redox titrations, a silver electrode or ISEs for precipitation titrations and ISEs for
chelometric titrations. Potentiometric titration curves are often measured with the aid of an automatic burette or an automatic titrator. Measurements of potentiometric titration curves are useful not only for chemical analyses but also for elucidating reaction stoichiometry and for obtaining physicochemical data, such as acid dissociation constants, complex formation constants, solubility products and standard redox potentials. Potentiometric titrations play important roles in chemical studies in nonaqueous solutions, as described in Chapter 6.

## 5.8 <br> Conductimetry - A Method that Is Not Based on Electrode Reactions

Conductimetry is a method of obtaining analytical and physicochemical information by measuring the conductivities of electrolyte solutions [28]. Conductivity cells have two or four electrodes, but the processes that occur at or near the electrodes are not directly related to the information obtained by conductimetric measurements.

Figure 5.37 shows an electrolyte solution between two plane electrodes. The conductivity of the solution ( $\kappa$ ) is expressed by $\kappa=L / A R$, where $L$ is the distance between the two electrodes (cm), $A$ is the electrode area $\left(\mathrm{cm}^{2}\right)$ and $R$ is the electrical resistance of the solution $(\Omega)$. For dilute electrolyte solutions, the conductivity $\kappa$ is proportional to the concentrations of the constituent ions, as in Eq. (5.39):

$$
\kappa=\sum\left|z_{i}\right| F u_{i} c_{i}=\sum \lambda_{i} c_{i}
$$

Here, $u_{i}$ is the electric mobility of ion $i$ and the proportionality constant $\lambda_{i}\left(=\left|z_{i}\right| F u_{i}\right)$ is the ionic conductivity or the molar conductivity of ion $i$.

If a strong electrolyte, consisting of $v_{+}$pieces of cation $\mathrm{B}^{z+}$ and $v_{-}$pieces of anion $\mathrm{B}^{z-}$, is dissolved to prepare a solution of concentration $c$, the following relations exist:

$$
v_{+} z_{+}=-v_{-} z_{-}=v_{-}\left|z_{-}\right|, \quad c=\frac{c_{+}}{v_{+}}=\frac{c_{-}}{v_{-}}
$$

where $c_{+}$and $c_{-}$are the concentrations of $\mathrm{B}^{z+}$ and $\mathrm{B}^{z-}$, respectively. By substituting these relations in Eq. (5.39), we get

$$
\kappa=\left(u_{+}+u_{-}\right) z_{+} v_{+} F c=\left(u_{+}+u_{-}\right)\left|z_{-}\right| v_{-} F c=\left(v_{+} \lambda_{+}+v_{-} \lambda_{-}\right) c
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-153.jpg?height=296&width=737&top_left_y=1723&top_left_x=350)
Figure 5.37 Ionic conductivity in electrolyte solutions.

The value $\Lambda$, given by Eq. (5.40), is the molar conductivity of an electrolyte:

$$
\Lambda=\kappa / c=\left(u_{+}+u_{-}\right) z_{+} v_{+} F=\left(u_{+}+u_{-}\right)\left|z_{-}\right| v_{-} F=v_{+} \lambda_{+}+v_{-} \lambda_{-}
$$

If the conductivity $\kappa$ and the electrolyte concentration $c$ are expressed in the respective SI units $\left(\mathrm{S} \mathrm{m}^{-1}\right.$ and $\left.\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{m}^{-3}\right)$, the unit for the molar conductivity $\Lambda$ is $\mathrm{S} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. However, $\kappa, c$ and $\Lambda$ are often expressed in the respective cgs units $\left(\mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}, \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}\right.$ and $\mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ). In this case, Eq. (5.40) should be replaced by Eq. (5.41):

$$
\Lambda\left(\mathrm{S} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)=1000 \kappa\left(\mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\right) /\left\{c\left(\mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}\right)\right\}
$$

If $\Lambda, \lambda_{+}$and $\lambda_{-}$at an infinite dilution are expressed by $\Lambda^{\infty}, \lambda_{+}^{\infty}$ and $\lambda_{-}^{\infty}$, respectively,

$$
\Lambda^{\infty}=v_{+} \lambda_{+}^{\infty}+v_{-} \lambda_{-}^{\infty}
$$

The values of $\Lambda, \lambda_{+}$and $\lambda_{-}$decrease with increasing electrolyte concentration due to the influence of ion-ion interactions. Kohlrausch found the following experimental relation for dilute solutions of strong electrolytes:

$$
\Lambda=\Lambda^{\infty}-k c^{1 / 2}
$$

where $k$ is a constant. A similar relation has been obtained theoretically. According to Debye-Hückel-Onsager limiting law.

$$
\Lambda=\Lambda^{\infty}-\left(A \Lambda^{\infty}+B\right) c^{1 / 2}
$$

where $A$ and $B$ are parameters that depend on ionic charges, viscosity and relative permittivity of solvent and temperature (see footnote 1 in Chapter 7). If we plot the value of $\Lambda$ against $c^{1 / 2}$, we get the approximate value of $\Lambda^{\infty}$ (for method of obtaining more reliable $\Lambda^{\infty}$ value, see Chapter 7). In order to get the values of $\lambda_{+}^{\infty}$ and $\lambda_{-}^{\infty}$, we use transport numbers. The transport number is the fraction of the current carried by a given ion. The transport number of ion $i, t_{i}$, is expressed by

$$
t_{i}=\frac{\left|z_{i}\right| u_{i} c_{i}}{\sum\left|z_{i}\right| u_{i} c_{i}}=\frac{\lambda_{i} c_{i}}{\sum \lambda_{i} c_{i}}
$$

For 1: 1 electrolyte:

$$
t_{+}=\frac{u_{+}}{u_{+}+u_{-}}=\frac{\lambda_{+}}{\lambda_{+}+\lambda_{-}}, \quad t_{-}=1-t_{+}
$$

For example, if we know the values of $t_{\mathrm{K}}$ and $t_{\mathrm{Cl}}$ for a KCl solution at infinite dilution, we can get $\lambda_{\mathrm{K}}^{\infty}$ and $\lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}}^{\infty}$ from the value of $\Lambda_{\mathrm{KCl}}^{\infty}$. Once $\lambda_{\mathrm{K}}^{\infty}$ and $\lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}}^{\infty}$ are obtained, we can get the $\lambda^{\infty}$ values for other ions by using Kohlrausch's law of independent ionic migration. For example, the value of $\lambda_{\mathrm{Na}}^{\infty}$ can be obtained by the relation $\lambda_{\mathrm{Na}}^{\infty}=\left(\Lambda_{\mathrm{NaCl}}^{\infty}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{KCl}}^{\infty}+\lambda_{\mathrm{K}}^{\infty}\right)$. Thus, only data on transport numbers for $\mathrm{K}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ in the KCl solution are needed.

For the solution of a weak electrolyte, the $\Lambda-c^{1 / 2}$ relation is quite different from that for the solution of a strong electrolyte. Figure 5.38 shows the $\Lambda-c^{1 / 2}$ relations for $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ (weak electrolyte) and HCl (strong electrolyte). The value of $\Lambda$ for

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-155.jpg?height=963&width=643&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=352)
Figure $5.38 \Lambda-c^{1 / 2}$ relationship in aqueous solutions of strong acid HCl and weak acid $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ decreases abruptly with increase in concentration. If a weak electrolyte consists of $v_{+}$of cation $\mathrm{B}^{z+}$ and $v_{-}$of anion $\mathrm{B}^{z-}$, the following relations exist:

$$
\kappa=\alpha\left(v_{+} \lambda_{+}+v_{-} \lambda_{-}\right) c, \quad \Lambda=\alpha\left(v_{+} \lambda_{+}+v_{-} \lambda_{-}\right)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the degree of dissociation of the electrolyte. If $\alpha$ is small enough, the dissociated ions are dilute and behave ideally. In that case

$$
\alpha \approx \Lambda / \Lambda^{\infty}
$$

For a 1: 1 electrolyte, the apparent dissociation constant $K^{\prime}$ is given by

$$
K^{\prime}=\frac{a^{2} c}{1-\alpha} \approx \frac{\Lambda^{2} c}{\Lambda^{\infty}\left(\Lambda^{\infty}-\Lambda\right)}
$$

We can get an approximate value of $K^{\prime}$ from Eq. (5.43) by measuring $\Lambda$ and $c$. If we consider the effect of concentration on the behavior of dissociated ions, we get Eqs. (5.44) from Eq. (5.42):

$$
\alpha=\Lambda /\left[\Lambda^{\infty}-\left(A \Lambda^{\infty}+B\right)(\alpha c)^{1 / 2}\right]
$$

The conductivity of a solution is measured using an AC bridge with a two-electrode conductance cell on one arm (Figure 5.39a); a balance is sought, manually or automatically, by adjusting the variable resistance and capacitance in another arm

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-156.jpg?height=511&width=942&top_left_y=186&top_left_x=207)
Figure 5.39 Circuits for conductivity measurements with twoelectrode cell (a) and four-electrode cell (b). (a) S: AC voltage source; D: detector; I, II, III: bridge elements. (b) S: constant current source; POT: potentiometer; $R_{\mathrm{s}}$ : variable resistor; C and $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ : electrodes for current flow; P and $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}$ : electrodes for voltage measurement.

of the bridge. Usually, AC voltage of a few volts and $\sim 1 \mathrm{kHz}$ is applied to the cell. The impedance caused by the double-layer capacity at the electrodes does not affect the measured values of conductivity. In some cases, the conductance is measured with a four-electrode cell, as shown in Figure 5.39b. For practical methods of measurement, see the reviews in Ref. [28].

Conductimetry is used to detect and determine ionic species in solution. It plays important roles in environmental analyses and in the detection of ion chromatography. It is also used in acid-base, precipitation and chelometric titrations to detect end points. However, the biggest advantage of conductimetry is displayed in the fundamental studies of solution chemistry. Its applications to chemical studies in nonaqueous solutions will be discussed in Chapter 7.

## 5.9 <br> Electrochemical Instrumentation - Roles of Operational Amplifiers and Microcomputers

The previous sections outlined various electrochemical techniques. For most of these techniques, instruments are commercially available and we can use them conveniently. The common features of modern electrochemical instruments are that operational amplifiers and microcomputers play important roles in them. These are discussed in this section.

### 5.9.1 <br> Application of Operational Amplifiers in Electrochemical Instrumentation

Operational amplifiers, which are the main components of an analogue computer, were first used in electrochemical instrumentation at the beginning of the 1960s [29].

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-157.jpg?height=356&width=765&top_left_y=196&top_left_x=350)
Figure 5.40 An operational amplifier.

Because they are extremely useful in measuring and controlling the electrode potentials and the currents that flow at the electrodes, electrochemical instruments were completely modernized by their introduction. Today, most electrochemical instruments are constructed using operational amplifiers. Knowledge of operational amplifiers will help the reader to understand electrochemical instruments and to construct a simple apparatus for personal use.

1. Basic operational amplifier circuits An operational amplifier is generally a differential amplifier, as shown in Figure 5.40, with an inverting input (-), a noninverting input ( + ) and an output. Ideally, it has the following characteristics: (i) an infinite input impedance (actually $\sim 10^{15} \Omega$ maximum), (ii) an almost infinite gain $A$ (actually $A=10^{4}-10^{6}$ ), (iii) an output voltage $E_{\mathrm{o}}$ given by $E_{\mathrm{o}}=A\left(E_{+}-E_{-}\right)$ and (iv) a large (but finite) output voltage and a high output current (e.g. $\pm 10 \mathrm{~V}$ and $\pm 10 \mathrm{~mA}$ ). From these characteristics, the following can be concluded:
(1) At each of the two inputs, almost no current flows from the external circuit.
(2) The value of ( $E_{+}-E_{-}$) is very close to zero (typically $< \pm 0.1 \mathrm{mV}$ ) and, if the noninverting input ( + input) is grounded, the inverting input (- input) is held at virtual ground.
Some important circuits constructed with an operational amplifier are shown in Figure 5.41. Their functions are easily understood by use of (1) and (2) above.

Inverting, multiplying or dividing circuit In Figure 5.41a, point $s$ is held at virtual ground, i.e. the potential at point $s$ is actually equal to zero from (2) above. The current $i_{\mathrm{i}}$, which is equal to ( $E_{\mathrm{i}} / R_{\mathrm{i}}$ ), flows through the resistor $R_{\mathrm{i}}$ and then, by (1), through the resistor $R_{\mathrm{f}}$. Thus,

$$
E_{\mathrm{o}}=-E_{\mathrm{i}} \times\left(\frac{R_{\mathrm{f}}}{R_{\mathrm{i}}}\right)
$$

Circuit (a) works as an inverter of the input voltage because $E_{\mathrm{o}}=-E_{\mathrm{i}}$ for $R_{\mathrm{i}}=R_{\mathrm{f}}$. It also works as a multiplying (or dividing) circuit if the values of $R_{\mathrm{i}}$ and $R_{\mathrm{f}}$ are selected appropriately.

Summing circuit In Figure 5.41b, because point s is held at virtual ground, the currents $i_{1}=\left(E_{1} / R_{1}\right), i_{2}=\left(E_{2} / R_{2}\right)$ and $i_{3}=\left(E_{3} / R_{2}\right)$ flow through the resistors $R_{1}$,

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-158.jpg?height=1043&width=1145&top_left_y=189&top_left_x=205)
Figure 5.41 Feedback circuits for mathematical operations with an operational amplifier.

$R_{2}$ and $R_{3}$, respectively, and join at the summing point s to flow through $R_{\mathrm{f}}$. Thus,

$$
E_{\mathrm{o}}=-\left\{E_{1}\left(\frac{R_{\mathrm{f}}}{R_{1}}\right)+E_{2}\left(\frac{R_{\mathrm{f}}}{R_{2}}\right)+E_{3}\left(\frac{R_{\mathrm{f}}}{R_{3}}\right)\right\}
$$

If $R_{1}=R_{2}=R_{3}=R_{\mathrm{f}}$,

$$
E_{o}=-\left(E_{1}+E_{2}+E_{3}\right)
$$

This summing circuit is used in polarography and voltammetry to superimpose AC, SW or pulse voltage to DC applied voltage.

Integrating and differentiating circuits In Figure 5.41c, the capacitor $C$ is charged by current $i_{\mathrm{i}}=\left(E_{\mathrm{i}} / R\right)$. Thus, the quantity of charged electricity is expressed by $Q=\int_{0}^{t} i_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{d} t=(1 / R) \int_{0}^{t} E_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{d} t$. Because point s is a virtual ground, the output voltage is

$$
E_{\mathrm{o}}=-\frac{1}{C} \int_{0}^{t} i_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{~d} t=-\frac{1}{R C} \int_{0}^{t} E_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{~d} t
$$

This integrating circuit is used to give linear and cyclic voltage scans in polarography and voltammetry. It is also used as a coulometer in coulometry.
A simple differentiating circuit is shown in Figure 5.41d. If the capacitor in Figure 5.41c and d is replaced by a diode, logarithmic and exponential circuits can be obtained.

Voltage and current followers Figure 5.41e shows a circuit for a voltage follower. From (1) and (2) above, $E_{\mathrm{o}}=E_{\mathrm{i}}$. Moreover, by using an appropriate operational amplifier, the impedance of the noninverting input can be kept very high ( $\sim 10^{13} \Omega$ or more). Thus, the voltage $E_{\mathrm{i}}$ of a high-impedance source can be transmitted to a low-impedance circuit, keeping its magnitude and sign unchanged. This circuit is often used in constructing $\mathrm{mV} / \mathrm{pH}$ meters of high input impedance. It is also used as a voltage follower in a potentiostat. It can prevent a current flowing through the reference electrode, keeping the potential unchanged (VF in Figure 5.42b). The circuit in Figure 5.41f is a current follower and converts the input current to the output voltage $E_{\mathrm{o}}=-R_{\mathrm{f}} i_{\mathrm{i}}$, which can be measured or recorded. Because point s is a virtual ground, the circuit can be inserted in the grounded current-carrying circuit, as CF in Figure 5.42b, without introducing any impedance.
2. Potential and current controls in electrochemical instrumentation by use of operational amplifiers It is usual in electrochemical measurements to control the potential of the working (or indicator) electrode or the electrolytic current that flows through the cell. A potentiostat is used to control electrode potential and a galvanostat is used to control electrolytic current. Operational amplifiers play important roles in both of these.

Circuit for potential control and a potentiostat Figure 5.42a shows a potential control circuit. By (1) and (2) above, point s is held at virtual ground and current $i \left(=E / R_{1}\right)$ flows through resistor $R_{1}$ and then through $R_{2}$. If $R_{1}=R_{2}$, the potential at point $x$ is held equal to $-E$, irrespective of the values of impedance $Z_{1}$ and $Z_{2}$. This relation is used in the circuit of a potentiostat as in Figure 5.42b, where the potential of the RE is equal to $-E$ against ground. Because the working electrode (WE) is grounded, its potential is equal to $E$ against the RE, irrespective of the

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-159.jpg?height=421&width=1186&top_left_y=1545&top_left_x=286)
Figure 5.42 Voltage control circuit with the aid of an operational amplifier (a) and a circuit of potentiostat (b). VF: voltage follower; CF: current follower.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-160.jpg?height=300&width=952&top_left_y=196&top_left_x=204)
Figure 5.43 Current control circuit with the aid of an operational amplifier (a) and a circuit of galvanostat (b).

impedance of the electrolytic solution and the reaction at the electrode. Here, VF inserted between the resistor $R_{2}$ and RE is a voltage follower, which prevents the current flow through RE. On the other hand, CF between WE and the ground is a current follower or a current-to-voltage converter. The current that flows at the WE is measured as a voltage. It should be noted that the performance of the potentiostat is unaffected by the insertion of voltage and current followers.

Circuit for current control and a galvanostat Figure 5.43a shows a current control circuit. The current $i(=E / R)$ flows through the resistor $R$ and then through the impedance $Z$. This circuit is used in a galvanostat as shown in Figure 5.43b. The current through the cell is controlled at $i(=E / R)$, irrespective of the cell impedance and the reaction at the electrode. When the potential of the working (or indicator) electrode WE is to be measured, an RE is inserted in the cell and the voltage between WE and RE is measured (Figure 5.31b) using a voltage follower and a millivolt meter.

Three-electrode instruments for polarography and voltammetry In Figure 5.44, if $E$ connected to point a is a DC voltage source that generates a triangular voltage cycle, we can use the circuit of Figure 5.44 for measurements in DC polarography as well as in linear sweep or cyclic voltammetry. An integrating circuit as in Figure 5.41c is applicable to generate a triangle voltage cycle. In Figure 5.44, bold lines show positive feedback $i R$ compensation. ${ }^{15)}$ When a sine-wave or squarewave voltage of small amplitude is to be superimposed on the DC voltage, its voltage source is connected to point $b$. Then, the potential of the indicator electrode (WE) is controlled at a value equal to the sum of the voltages at points a and b . Of course, appropriate modifications of the current measuring circuit are necessary to get AC or SW polarograms and voltammograms.
15) Positive feedback $i R$ compensation in threeelectrode measurements: As described in Section 5.3, the influence of $i R$ drop is significant in two-electrode polarography or voltammetry. The influence is eliminated considerably with three-electrode instruments if the tip of the reference electrode is placed near the surface of the indicator electrode. However, there still remains some $i R$ drop, which occurs by the residual resistance
at the indicator electrode or in the solution between the surface of the indicator electrode and the tip of the reference electrode. To eliminate the $i R$ drop, the positive feedback circuit as in Figure 5.44 is used. By adjusting $R_{\mathrm{c}}$, the voltage that is equal to the uncompensated $i R$ drop is added to the applied voltage. For more detailed method of overcoming the influence of solution resistance, see Ref. [30].

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-161.jpg?height=845&width=956&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=345)
Figure 5.44 Circuit for a three-electrode polarograph. The bold lines are for positive feedback $i R$ drop compensation. $C_{f}$ is a capacitor to decrease the current fluctuation.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-161.jpg?height=834&width=645&top_left_y=1194&top_left_x=345)
Figure 5.45 A personal computer-aided electrochemical apparatus.

### 5.9.2 <br> Application of Personal Computers in Electrochemical Instrumentation

In modern instrumentation in polarography and voltammetry, a personal computer (PC) is often connected to a potentiostat, by inserting an analogue-to-digital converter (ADC) and a digital-to-analogue converter (DAC) between them (Figure 5.45) [31]. The DAC converts digital signals from the PC to analogue signals and transfers them to the potentiostat. The ADC, on the other hand, converts analogue signals from the potentiostat to digital signals and transfers them to the PC. This digital instrumentation offers various advantages.
(i) It is suitable for staircase, square-wave or pulse polarography and voltammetry. Using a simple computer program, the PC generates a staircase, square-wave or pulse voltage to be applied to the cell, and then it reads the current flowing through the cell circuit as digital signals and processes them in a variety of ways. In SW and pulse polarography and voltammetry, the program samples only the current that is solely faradaic (see Figure 5.15).
(ii) The digital instrumentation is useful in rapid-scan cyclic voltammetry. Data of the cyclic voltammogram are first stored in a memory device and then processed or graphically presented. With a sophisticated digital instrument and an ultramicroelectrode, potential sweeps of $1000000 \mathrm{Vs}^{-1}$ or more are possible.
(iii) The digital simulation makes mechanistic studies of electrode processes by cyclic voltammetry much easier. Cyclic voltammograms readily provide qualitative information about electrode processes but, without the simulation technique, it is rather difficult to get quantitative mechanistic information from them.

## 6 <br> Potentiometry in Nonaqueous Solutions

Just as in aqueous solutions, potentiometry is the most fundamental and powerful method of measuring pH , ionic activities and redox potentials in nonaqueous solutions. In this chapter, we deal with the basic techniques of potentiometry in nonaqueous solutions and then discuss how potentiometry is applicable to studies of chemistry in nonaqueous solutions. Some topics in this field have been reviewed in Ref. [1a].

## 6.1 <br> Basic Techniques of Potentiometry in Nonaqueous Solutions

In potentiometry, we measure the emf of a cell consisting of an indicator electrode and a reference electrode. For emf measurements, we generally use a $\mathrm{pH} / \mathrm{mV}$ meter of high input impedance. The potential of the reference electrode must be stable and reproducible. If there is a liquid junction between the indicator electrode and the reference electrode, we should take the liquid junction potential (LJP) into account.

In aqueous solutions, the method of measuring electrode potentials has been well established. The standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) is the primary reference electrode and its potential is defined as zero at all temperatures. Practical measurements employ reference electrodes that are easy to use, the most popular ones being a silver-silver chloride electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (Table 5.4). The magnitude of the liquid junction potential between two aqueous electrolyte solutions can be estimated by the Henderson equation. However, it is usual to keep the LJP small either by adding the same indifferent electrolyte in the two solutions or by inserting an appropriate salt bridge between the two solutions.

In contrast, in nonaqueous solutions, the method of measuring electrode potential has not been well established. The most serious problem is the reference electrode; there is no primary reference electrode such as the SHE in aqueous solutions and no reference electrode as reliable as the aqueous $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}$ electrode. Thus, various reference electrodes have been employed in practical measurements, making the comparison of potential data difficult. As will be described later, various efforts are being made to improve this situation.

Table 6.1 Potentiometric indicator electrodes for use in nonaqueous solutions.
| Property measured | Indicator electrodes |
| :--- | :--- |
| Redox potential | Inert redox electrodes (Pt, Au, glassy carbon, etc.) |
| pH | pH glass electrode; pH-ISFET; iridium oxide pH sensor |
| Ionic activities | Electrodes of the first kind ( $\mathrm{M}^{n+} / \mathrm{M}$ and $\mathrm{M}^{n+} / \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{Hg})$ electrodes); univalent cation-sensitive glass electrode (alkali metal ions, $\mathrm{NH}_{4}{ }^{+}$); solid-membrane ion-selective electrodes ( $\mathrm{F}^{-}$, halide ions, heavy metal ions); polymermembrane electrodes ( $\mathrm{F}^{-}, \mathrm{CN}^{-}$, alkali metal ions, alkaline earth metal ions) |


### 6.1.1 <br> Potentiometric Indicator Electrodes for Nonaqueous Solutions

Potentiometric indicator electrodes for aqueous solutions were dealt with in Section 5.7.1. Some of them are also applicable in nonaqueous solutions, if they are durable and can respond appropriately. Examples of such indicator electrodes are given in Table 6.1. A platinum electrode and other inert electrodes are used to measure the redox potential. Metal and metal amalgam electrodes are used to measure the activities of the corresponding metal ions. Univalent cation-sensitive glass electrodes and some solid-membrane ion-selective electrodes (ISEs) are useful to measure the activities of the cations and anions to which they respond. The conventional pH glass electrode is the most popular for pH measurements in nonaqueous solutions, but other pH sensors such as pH -ISFETs are also applicable. Although various types of liquid-membrane ISEs have been developed for aqueous solutions, most of them are not applicable in nonaqueous solutions, because either their sensing membrane or supporting body is destroyed in organic solvents. However, as shown in Table 6.1 and Section 6.3.2, new polymer-membrane ISEs have been developed and used successfully in nonaqueous solutions [2]. Applications of ISEs in nonaqueous solutions have been reviewed [3] and will be discussed in Sections 6.2 and 6.3.

### 6.1.2 <br> Reference Electrodes for Nonaqueous Solutions

The reference electrodes used in nonaqueous systems can be classified into two types. One type uses, in constructing a reference electrode, the same solvent as that of the solution under study. The other type is an aqueous reference electrode, usually an aqueous $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}$ electrode or SCE. Some reference electrodes are listed in Table 6.2 and are briefly discussed below. For other types of reference electrodes used in nonaqueous solutions, see Ref. [4].

## Reference Electrodes that Use the Same Solvent as the Solution Under Study

Silver-silver ion electrode This is the most popular reference electrode used in nonaqueous solutions. Since Pleskov employed it in acetonitrile (AN) in 1948, it has been used in a variety of solvents. It has a structure as shown in Figure 6.1a

and is easy to construct. Its potential is usually reproducible within 5 mV if it is prepared fresh using pure solvent and electrolyte. The stability of the potential, however, is not always good enough. The potential is stable in AN because $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$is strongly solvated in it. In propylene carbonate (PC) and nitromethane (NM), however, $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$is solvated only weakly and the potential is easily influenced by the presence of trace water and other impurities. ${ }^{\mathbf{1})}$ In dimethylformamide (DMF),

(a) $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{Ag}^{+}$electrode and (b) $\mathrm{Pt} / \mathrm{I}_{3}{ }^{-}, \mathrm{I}^{-}$electrode [7] $\left(\mathrm{TEAP}=\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}\right)$.

1) If the solvation of $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$is weak, adding 1 or $2(\mathrm{v} / \mathrm{v}) \% \mathrm{AN}$ to the $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$solution is effective in improving the stability of the potential of the $\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}$ electrode (Ref. [24b]).
however, $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$is slowly reduced to $\mathrm{Ag}^{0}$, causing a gradual potential shift to the negative direction. ${ }^{2)}$ The shift reaches several tens of millivolts after a few days.

Silver-silver cryptate electrode This type of electrode was developed by this author in order to improve the stability of the silver-silver ion electrode [5]. It has a structure of $\mathrm{Ag} / 5 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{AgCrypClO}{ }_{4}, 5 \mathrm{mM}$ Cryp, $50 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}(\mathrm{~S})$, where Cryp and $\mathrm{AgCryp}{ }^{+}$denote cryptand(22) and its complex with $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$, respectively, and can be prepared in various solvents. Its potential is given by $E=E^{0}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+} /\right. \mathrm{Ag})-0.059 \log K+0.059 \log \left(\left[\mathrm{AgCryp}^{+}\right] /[\right.$Cryp $\left.]\right)$( $K$ : formation constant of $\mathrm{AgCryp}{ }^{+}$). Because the electrolyte solution is an $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ion buffer, the potential is stable if the ratio $\left[\mathrm{AgCryp}{ }^{+}\right] /[\mathrm{Cryp}]$ is kept constant. Thus, the potential is less influenced by solvent impurities than in the case of an $\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}$ electrode. Moreover, as shown in Table 4.3, the potential of this electrode is not so much influenced by the solvent. Recently, Lewandowski et al. used cryptand(222) to develop a similar reference electrode [6].

Silver-silver chloride electrode In protic solvents such as methanol, this type of electrode can be constructed similarly as in aqueous solutions. In aprotic solvents, however, it is difficult to prepare a silver-silver chloride electrode of high $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ concentrations. The $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ion in aprotic solvents strongly interacts with chloride ion to form $\mathrm{AgCl}_{2}{ }^{-}$complex (Section 2.4). Thus, the precipitate of AgCl is easily dissolved in solutions of high $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$concentrations. When we construct a silver-silver chloride electrode in an aprotic solvent, we keep the free $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ concentration low, using a solution of a chloride salt that is either sparingly soluble or of dilute concentration, and saturate AgCl in it. Examples of such electrodes are listed in Table 6.3. Though it takes some time to prepare the AgCl -saturated solution, electrodes of this type work well with a long-term potential stability.

Pt $/ \mathbf{I}_{3}{ }^{-}, \mathbf{I}^{-}$electrode This electrode, proposed by Coetzee and Gardner [7], is of a double-junction type as shown in Figure 6.1b. It is easy to construct in many solvents, and the potential is stable and reproducible. Because the electrode reaction has a high exchange current, the potential shift caused by the current flow

2) DMF works as a reducing agent $\left(2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\right. \mathrm{HCONMe}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Ag}^{0}+\mathrm{Me}_{2} \mathrm{NCOOH} 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}$). The reduction process has been used to produce monolayers and stable colloids of
silver nanoparticles (Pastoriza-Santos, I. and Liz-Marzán, L.M. (2000) Pure Appl. Chem., 72, 83).
is relatively small and the potential quickly returns to the equilibrium value when the current is stopped. This is an advantage over a silver-silver ion electrode.

We have previously found that for each of the above reference electrodes, if we construct several pieces at a time and in the same way, their potentials agree to within 1 mV for 1-2 days and their potential shifts are $\sim 0.1 \mathrm{mV} / 5 \mathrm{~h}$ or less [5]. Thus, under well-controlled conditions, all of the above reference electrodes work well as reference electrodes. However, even among the electrodes of the same type, the potentials may differ by 10 mV or more, if they are constructed or used under different conditions. It is difficult to compare such potential data to within $10 \mathrm{mV} .^{\left.2^{\prime}\right)}$

## Use of Aqueous Reference Electrodes

Aqueous reference electrodes, such as SCE and Ag/AgCl electrodes, are often used in nonaqueous systems by dipping their tips into nonaqueous solutions of the salt bridge. The tip should not be dipped directly into the solution under study because the solution is contaminated with water and the electrolyte (usually KCl ). When we use such aqueous reference electrodes, we must take the liquid junction potential between aqueous and nonaqueous solutions (see the footnote in Table 6.2) into account. If we carefully reproduce the composition of the solutions at the junction, the LJP is usually reproducible within $\pm 10 \mathrm{mV}$. This is the reason why aqueous reference electrodes are often used in nonaqueous systems. However, the LJP sometimes exceeds 200 mV and it is easily influenced by the electrolytes and the solvents at the junction (Section 6.4). The use of aqueous reference electrodes should be avoided, if possible.

### 6.1.3 <br> Method of Reporting Electrode Potentials in Nonaqueous Solutions (IUPAC Recommendation)

Because there is no truly reliable reference electrode for use in nonaqueous solutions, various reference electrodes have so far been used. Thus, accurate mutual comparison of the potential data in nonaqueous systems is often difficult. To improve the situation, the IUPAC Commission on Electrochemistry proposed a method for reporting electrode potentials [8]. It can be summarized as follows:

1. Either $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ or $\mathrm{BCr}^{+} / \mathrm{BCr}$ couple is selected as reference redox system and the electrode potentials in any solvent and at any temperature are reported as values referred to the (apparent) standard potential of the system. Which of the two couples to select depends on the potential of the system under study; the potential of the reference redox system should not overlap that of the system under study. Because the difference between the standard potentials of the two couples is almost solvent independent $(1.132 \pm 0.012 \mathrm{~V}$, see Table 6.4), the potential referred to one system can be converted to the potential referred to the other.
$2^{\prime}$ ) Various other reference electrodes exist. Among them, the $\mathrm{Li}^{+} / \mathrm{Li}$ reference electrode is used very often in studying lithium batteries.

2. In fact, the potentiometric or voltammetric measurement is carried out using a conventional reference electrode (e.g. $\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}$ electrode). ${ }^{3)}$ After the measurement in the test solution, Fc or $\mathrm{BCr}^{+}\left(\mathrm{BPh}_{4}{ }^{-}\right.$salt) is added to the solution and the half-wave potential of the reference system is measured by polarography or voltammetry. Here, the half-wave potential for the reference system is almost equal to its formal potential. Thus, the potential for the test system is converted to the value versus the formal potential of the reference system. The example in Figure 6.2 is for the situation where both the test and the reference systems are measured by cyclic voltammetry, where $E_{1 / 2}=\left(E_{\mathrm{pc}}+E_{\mathrm{pa}}\right) / 2$. Curve 1 was obtained before the addition of Fc and curve 2 was obtained after the addition of Fc . It is essential that the half-wave potential of the test system is not affected by the addition of the reference system.
3. In reporting the potential data, the reference redox system used should be indicated by such symbols as $E_{1 / 2(\mathrm{BCr})}$ and $E_{(\mathrm{FC})}^{0}$. Detailed information should also be given concerning the cell construction, solvent purification, impurities in the solution, etc.

For the $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ and $\mathrm{BCr}^{+} / \mathrm{BCr}$ couples, the electrode reactions are reversible or nearly reversible in most nonaqueous solvents and the half-wave potentials are not much influenced by solvent impurities such as water. Thus, the half-wave potentials will not vary widely even when they are measured by different persons or at different laboratories. Moreover, the potentials of the $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ and $\mathrm{BCr}^{+} / \mathrm{BCr}$ couples are considered almost solvent independent. This justifies the comparison of the potential data in different solvents as far as they are based on this proposal. The data in Table 6.4
3) The reference electrode of the $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ couple can be prepared in PC and AN by inserting a platinum wire in the equimolar solution of Fc and $\mathrm{Fc}^{+}\left(\mathrm{Pic}^{-}\right)$. But it is impossible in DMF
and DMSO, because $\mathrm{Fc}^{+}$is rapidly reduced to Fc in those solvents. The $\mathrm{Fc}^{+}$/ Fc couple is useful as a reference redox system rather than as a reference electrode.

are useful in determining the mutual relations between the potentials of the $\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}$ and $\mathrm{Hg}^{2+} / \mathrm{Hg}$ electrodes and the $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ and $\mathrm{BCr}^{+} / \mathrm{BCr}$ couples [9].

### 6.1.4 <br> Liquid Junction Potential Between Electrolyte Solutions in the Same Solvent

If two electrolyte solutions that are of different concentrations but in the same solvent contact each other at a junction, ion transfers occur across the junction (Figure 6.3). If the rate of transfer of the cation differs from that of the anion, a charge separation occurs at the junction and a potential difference is generated. The potential difference tends to retard the ion of higher rate and accelerate the ion of lower rate. Eventually, the rates of both ions are balanced and the potential difference reaches a constant value. This potential difference is called the liquid junction potential [10]. As for the LJP between aqueous solutions, the LJP between nonaqueous solutions can be estimated using the Henderson equation. Generally, the LJP, $E_{\mathrm{j}}$, at the junction $c_{1} \operatorname{MX}(\mathrm{~s}) \mid c_{2}$ NY(s) can be expressed by Eq. (6.1).

$$
E_{\mathrm{j}}=-\frac{R T}{F} \cdot \frac{\sum z_{i} u_{i}\left[c_{i}(2)-c_{i}(1)\right]}{\sum z_{i}^{2} u_{i}\left[c_{i}(2)-c_{i}(1)\right]} \ln \frac{\sum z_{i}^{2} u_{i} c_{i}(2)}{\sum z_{i}^{2} u_{i} c_{i}(1)}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-174.jpg?height=794&width=615&top_left_y=196&top_left_x=207)
Figure 6.3 Liquid junction potential between two solutions in the same solvent.

where $z_{i}, u_{i}$ and $c_{i}$ denote, respectively, the charge number, mobility and concentration of ion $i$ and $\Sigma$ denotes the sum over all ionic species. Table 6.5 lists some LJP values calculated from Eq. (6.1).

In electrochemical measurements, the LJP is usually kept small or at a constant value. There are two ways of doing this. One is to add an indifferent electrolyte or a

[^16]supporting electrolyte of the same concentration to solutions 1 and 2 . The other is to insert, between solutions 1 and 2, a salt bridge of the concentrated solution of an electrolyte whose cationic and anionic mobilities are approximately the same. In aqueous solutions, KCl and $\mathrm{KNO}_{3}$ are used as such a salt bridge electrolyte. In nonaqueous solutions, tetraethylammonium picrate ( $\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NPic}$ ) is often a candidate for such a salt bridge electrolyte because, in various solvents, the molar conductivity of $\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$is close to that of $\mathrm{Pic}^{-}$(see Table 6.5). However, there are solvents in which the molar conductivities of $\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Pic}^{-}$are considerably different (e.g. TMS and MeOH). In such cases, we use electrolytes such as (i-Am) ${ }_{3} \mathrm{BuNBPh}_{4}$ instead (Section 7.2).

In electrochemical measurements in nonaqueous systems, we sometimes use a cell with a junction between electrolyte solutions in different solvents. The problem of the LJP between different solvents is rather complicated and is discussed in Section 6.4.

## 6.2 <br> pH Measurements in Nonaqueous and Mixed Solvents

pH measurements in nonaqueous and mixed solvents were briefly discussed in Chapter 3. In this section, practical methods of pH measurements in nonaqueous and mixed solvent systems are considered, emphasizing the difference from those in aqueous solutions.

### 6.2.1 <br> IUPAC Method of pH Measurements in Aqueous Solutions

Before the adoption of the new IUPAC recommendation (Recommendation 2002) [11], the old recommendation (Recommendation 1985) [12] served as a guideline for pH measurement in dilute aqueous solutions. However, the old one was criticized as being metrologically unsatisfactory [13] and was replaced with the new one that is metrologically sound.

According to Recommendation 2002, the pH of dilute aqueous solutions ( $\leq 0.1 \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}$ ) can be determined by the following procedure:

The first step of the procedure is to define pH notionally by

$$
\mathrm{pH}=-\log a_{\mathrm{H}}=-\log m_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{H}} / m^{0}
$$

where $a$ is relative activity, $m$ is molality, $\gamma$ is activity coefficient and $m^{0}$ is standard molality. Because pH is a single-ion quantity and is thermodynamically immeasurable, a convention is necessary for its evaluation.

Thus, the second step is to determine primary pH standards or $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{PS})$ values by means of an absolute method, which requires no reference, by using a Harned cell without transference (I).

$$
\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \text { buffer } \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{Cl}^{-}|\mathrm{AgCl}| \mathrm{Ag}
$$

The reaction and the Nernst equation for cell I are expressed by

$$
\begin{aligned}
& (1 / 2) \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{AgCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{~s})+\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\
& E_{\mathrm{I}}=E_{\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}}^{0}-k \log \left[\left(m_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{H}} / m^{0}\right)\left(m_{\mathrm{Cl}} \gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}} / m^{0}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $E_{\mathrm{I}}$ is the emf of cell $\mathrm{I}, E_{\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}}^{0}$ is the standard potential of the $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}$ electrode, $\gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}$ is the activity coefficient of the $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ion and $k=(R T / F) \ln$ 10. The equation can be rearranged to give the acidity function, $\mathrm{p}\left(a_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}\right)$, since $a_{\mathrm{H}}=m_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{H}} / m^{0}$.

$$
\mathrm{p}\left(a_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}\right)=-\log \left(a_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}\right)=\left(E_{\mathrm{I}}-E_{\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}}^{0}\right) / k+\log \left(m_{\mathrm{Cl}} / m^{0}\right)
$$

The values of pH for the primary buffer solutions can be obtained in the following way: (1) Fill the Harned cell with HCl at, for example, $m_{\mathrm{HCl}}=0.01 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ and determine the value of $E_{\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}}^{0}$; (2) Fill the Harned cell with a buffer at a known ionic strength and measure $\mathrm{p}\left(a_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}\right)$ for at least three molarities of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$to determine $\mathrm{p}\left(a_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}\right)^{0}$ by extrapolation $m_{\mathrm{Cl}} \rightarrow 0$ and (3) Use the Bates-Guggenheim convention $\left\{\log \gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}^{0}=-A\left(I_{\mathrm{s}}^{0}\right)^{1 / 2} /\left[1+1.5\left(\left(I_{\mathrm{s}}^{0}\right) / m^{0}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]\right.$, where A is the Debye-Hückel limiting slope and $\left(I_{\mathrm{s}}^{0}\right)$ the ionic strength of the buffer with zero added chloride\} to get $\gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}^{0}$ and calculate pH .

Seven pH buffers have hitherto been designated as primary standards: they are (i) saturated (at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) potassium hydrogen tartrate, (ii) $0.1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ potassium dihydrogen citrate, (iii) $0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ potassium hydrogen phthalate, (iv) $0.025 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ disodium hydrogen phosphate $+0.25 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ potassium dihydrogen phosphate, (v) $0.03043 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ disodium hydrogen phosphate $+0.008695 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ potassium dihydrogen phosphate, (vi) $0.01 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ disodium tetraborate and (vii) $0.025 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ sodium hydrogen carbonate $+0.025 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ sodium carbonate. The values of $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{PS})$ for these buffers between 0 and $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ have been given, though with an uncertainty of 0.004 .

The third step is to get secondary pH standards $(\mathrm{SS})$ or $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{SS})$ values that are obtained by comparing with primary standards or $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{PS})$. Several cells with liquid junction are used: for example, $\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right|$ buffer $\mathrm{S}_{2}{ }^{\prime} \mathrm{KCl}\left(\geq 3.5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}\right)_{1}$ 'buffer $\mathrm{S}_{1}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \mathrm{Pt}$ and $\mathrm{Ag}|\mathrm{AgCl}| \mathrm{KCl}\left(\geq 3.5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}\right)$ 'buffer $\mathrm{S}_{1}$ or $\mathrm{S}_{2}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \mathrm{Pt}$ are with uncertainties of pH $(\mathrm{SS}) \sim 0.015$ and $\mathrm{Ag}|\mathrm{AgCl}| \mathrm{KCl}\left(\geq 3.5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}\right)$ 'buffer $\mathrm{S}_{1}$ or $\mathrm{S}_{2}$ glass electrode is with an uncertainty of $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{SS}) \sim 0.02$.

The fourth step is the practical pH measurement. The cell assembly for pH measurement is the cell, $\mathrm{Ag}|\mathrm{AgCl}| \mathrm{KCl}\left(\geq 3.5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}\right)$ 'solution $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{S})$ or $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{X}) \mid$ glass electrode and a pH meter. The uncertainty of the measurement is different by the calibration method: 0.3 for 1-point calibration, 0.02-0.03 for 2-point calibration and 0.01-0.03 for 5-point calibration.

The pH measurement is thus represented by the traceability chain $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{X}) \rightarrow \mathrm{pH} (\mathrm{SS}) \rightarrow \mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{PS}) \rightarrow \mathrm{pH}$ as defined, and each step has stated uncertainties.

In Recommendation 1985, the notional pH in aqueous solutions is defined by $\mathrm{pH}=-\log a_{\mathrm{H}}$. But, this equation can be rewritten by $\mathrm{pH}=-\log m_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{H}} / m^{0}$ or $\mathrm{pH}=-\log c_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{H}} / c^{0}$, depending on whether molal concentration, $m_{\mathrm{H}}\left[\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}\right]$, or
molar concentration, $c_{\mathrm{H}}\left[\mathrm{moll}^{-1}\right]$, is used. The operational pH is defined by the following (1) and (2):

1. Cell (II) contains a sample solution $X$ of $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{X})$ and its emf is equal to $E(\mathrm{X})$, while cell (III) contains the standard solution S of $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{S})$ and its emf is equal to $E(\mathrm{~S})$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Reference electrode } \mid \mathrm{KCl}\left(\mathrm{aq} ., \geq 3.5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}\right){ }_{11}^{11} \text { solution } \mathrm{X}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \mathrm{Pt} \\
& \text { Reference electrode } \mid \mathrm{KCl}\left(\mathrm{aq} ., \geq 3.5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}\right){ }_{11}^{11} \text { solution } \mathrm{S}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \mathrm{Pt}
\end{aligned}
$$

The relation among $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{X}), \mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{S}), E(\mathrm{X})$ and $E(\mathrm{~S})$ is given by

$$
\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{X})=\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{~S})+\{E(\mathrm{~S})-E(\mathrm{X})\} / \ln 10(R T / F)
$$

2. The $0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ potassium hydrogen phthalate solution is defined as the Reference Value pH Standard (RVS), and the reference pH values, $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{RVS})$, at various temperatures between 0 and $95^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are assigned to it. Six other buffer solutions are also used as primary pH standards and the values of $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{PS})$ are also designated. In addition to RVS and PS, operational pH standards (OS) may be used; the values of $\mathrm{OS}, \mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{OS})$, are obtained with a cell $\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \mathrm{OS}{ }_{\|}^{\|} \mathrm{KCl} \geq 3.5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}{ }_{\|} \mathrm{RVS}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \mathrm{Pt}$ at $0-95^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

Here, the solutions of the RVS plus six PSs in Recommendation 1985 coincide with those of seven PSs in Recommendation 2002. Moreover, the values of $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{RVS})$ and pH (PS) had been obtained by the same method as the primary method in Recommendation 2002. Therefore, the pH values of the primary standards in Recommendation 2002 and those of $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{RVS})$ plus $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{PS})$ in Recommendation 1985 are quite the same.

Practically, the pH measurements are conducted by using pH -sensitive glass electrode or some other pH sensors. The difference in pH between the sample and the standard solutions, $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{X})-\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{S})$, is given by (1). and the value of $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{S})$ is given by (2).; thus, we can know the value of $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{X})$. For dilute aqueous solutions ( $\leq 0.1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ ), pH determined by this method agrees with the notional pH by $\mathrm{pH}=-\log a\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right) \pm 0.02$. For solutions of ionic strength appreciably higher than $0.1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$, as in the case of seawater, the above method needs slight modification [14].

### 6.2.2 <br> Methods of pH Measurements in Nonaqueous and Mixed Solvents

The following two methods may be considered for measuring pH in nonaqueous and aqueous-organic solvent mixtures.

## Method Recommended by IUPAC

Expansion of the applicability of IUPAC Recommendation 2002, which is for dilute aqueous solutions, to nonaqueous and aqueous-organic solvent mixtures has been
discussed [15]. Primary and secondary methods of assigning pH values to primary and secondary standards have been considered. ${ }^{4)}$ But, the document seems to need some more discussion. Thus, the IUPAC recommendation now available for nonaqueous and aqueous-organic solvent mixtures is described here. It is based on Recommendation 1985 and was proposed by the IUPAC Commission on Electroanalytical Chemistry for pH measurements in organic solvents of high permittivities and in various aqueous-organic solvent mixtures [16,17].

The pH in nonaqueous solvents or solvent mixtures is notionally defined by

$$
\mathrm{pH}=-\log a_{\mathrm{H}}=-\log m_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{H}} / m^{0}
$$

where $a_{\mathrm{H}}$ is the activity, $m_{\mathrm{H}}$ is the molal concentration ( $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}$ ), $\gamma_{\mathrm{H}}$ is the activity of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$and $m^{0}$ is the standard molality.

In order to get pH values that meet this notional pH definition, the operational pH is defined by (1) and (2) below.

1. The relation among the pH values of the sample and the standard, $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{X})$ and pH $(\mathrm{S})$, and the emfs of cells (IV) and (V), $E(\mathrm{X})$ and $E(\mathrm{~S})$, is given by Eq. (6.4).

Reference electrode|salt bridge in $\mathrm{s}_{\|}^{\|}$solution $X$ in $\mathrm{s}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \mathrm{Pt}$

Reference electrode|salt bridge in $\mathrm{s}_{\|}^{\|}$solution S in $\mathrm{s}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \mathrm{Pt}$
4) Primary Standards: Harned cell is used to develop the primary method.

$$
\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \text { buffer } \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{MCl} \text { in } \mathrm{Z}|\mathrm{AgCl}| \mathrm{Ag}
$$

where $\mathrm{MCl}=\mathrm{KCl}$ or NaCl and Z is the solvent or solvent mixture under investigation. This cell is employed to determine the quantity $\mathrm{p}\left(a_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}\right)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{p}\left(a_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}\right)= & -\log \left(a_{\mathrm{H}} \gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}\right) \\
= & \left(E^{Z}-E^{0 \mathrm{Z}}\right) /(R T \ln 10 / F) \\
& +\log \left(m_{\mathrm{Cl}} / m^{0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $E^{\mathrm{Z}}$ and $E^{0 \mathrm{Z}}$ are the measured emf and the standard emf, respectively, of the cell. $E^{0 \mathrm{Z}}$ is determined by using a cell $\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}\right| \mathrm{HCl}$ in $\mathrm{Z}|\mathrm{AgCl}| \mathrm{Ag}$. The procedures for pure aqueous ( $\mathrm{Z}=$ water) and nonaqueous or mixed ( $\mathrm{Z}=\mathrm{Z}$ ) media coincide up to this point; then a substantial difference is introduced for generic solvent Z. pH (PS), where PS means primary standard, is described as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{PS})= & \lim m_{\mathrm{Cl} \rightarrow 0}\left[\left(E^{\mathrm{Z}}-E^{0 \mathrm{Z}}\right) /(R T \ln 10 / F)\right. \\
& \left.+\log \left(m_{\mathrm{Cl}} / m^{0}\right)-\mathrm{p} \gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}\right] \\
p \gamma_{\mathrm{Cl}}= & \left(A^{\mathrm{Z}} I^{1 / 2} /\left[1+1.5\left(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{W}} \rho^{\mathrm{Z}} / \varepsilon^{\mathrm{Z}} \rho^{\mathrm{W}}\right)^{1 / 2}\right.\right. \\
& \left.\left.\times\left(I / m^{0}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\varepsilon$ and $\rho$ are the relative permittivities and densities of the superscripted solvents, $A^{\mathrm{Z}}=A^{\mathrm{W}}\left(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{W}} / \varepsilon^{\mathrm{Z}}\right)^{3 / 2}$ is the relevant Debye-Hückel constant and $I$ is total ionic strength. $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{PS})$ is obtained by extrapolation to $m_{\mathrm{Cl} \rightarrow 0}$ of the straight-line plot of $\mathrm{p} a_{\mathrm{H}}$ against $m_{\mathrm{Cl}}$.

Thus, in principle, the primary standard, pH (PS), can be obtained by the same procedures as those in Recommendations 2002. But it is under various limiting conditions: for example, (i) the hydrogen electrode must work satisfactorily, (ii) the salt, MCl, should dissociate completely into ions, and for condition (ii), the value $\varepsilon^{\mathrm{Z}}$ should be larger than 35 . Even under satisfactory conditions, however, the uncertainty of the method is larger than in aqueous solutions.

Secondary Standards: The secondary standards can be obtained by comparison methods using various cells with transference (see Section 6.2.1). When a glass electrode is used, the actual slope of response in solvent Z must be used. A cell without transference, $\mathrm{Ag}|\mathrm{AgCl}|$ buffer $\mathrm{S}, \mathrm{MCl}$ in Z glass electrode, may also be used, making it unnecessary to rely on the comparative method that needs some primary pH standard.

$$
\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{X})=\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{~S})+\frac{E(\mathrm{~S})-E(\mathrm{X})}{\ln 10 \times(R T / F)}
$$

Here, s shows the solvent and the LJP at (iii) must be equal in cells (IV) and (V). ${ }^{5)}$ The hydrogen electrode in (IV) and (V) may be replaced by a glass electrode or other appropriate pH sensors.
2. $0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ potassium hydrogen phthalate (KHPh) in solvent s is used as the RVS and the reference pH values, $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{RVS})$, are assigned to it. Other buffer solutions are also used as primary pH standards and appropriate pH values are assigned to them.

This pH definition for nonaqueous and mixed solvent systems is practically the same as that for aqueous solutions (Section 6.2.1). Thus, if pH standards are available for the solvent or mixed solvent under study, the glass electrode is calibrated with them and then the pH of the sample solution is measured. The $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{RVS})$ values for $0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \mathrm{KHPh}$ have been assigned to formamide (high permittivity) and aqueous mixtures of 12 organic solvents (see footnote ${ }^{6)}$ for $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{RVS})$ at $\left.25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$. Though they are for discrete solvent compositions, the $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{RVS})$ in between those compositions can be obtained by using a multilinear regression equation [17c].

The IUPAC method is very reliable for pH measurements in water-rich solvent mixtures. However, in water-poor solvent mixtures and neat organic solvents, its applicability is restricted by the following reasons [18]. First, the solubility of KHPh is not sufficient in aqueous mixtures of MeOH and AN with water content less than $10 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{v} \%$. Second, the increase in the aprotic property of the solvent mixtures leads to the loss of the buffer capacity of the KHPh solution. This tendency is apparent from Figure 6.4, which shows the neutralization titration curves of o-phthalic acid ( $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{Ph}$ ) in various $\mathrm{AN}-\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ mixtures. In Figure 6.4, the mixtures
5) The electrolyte in the salt bridge keeps the LJP constant, but its anion should not react with hydrogen ion in the solution under study.
6) The pH values of $0.05 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ potassium hydrogen phthalate (KHPh) buffers (i.e. RVS in Recommendation 1985) in FA and in water + organic solvent mixtures at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are shown for $\mathrm{wt} \%$ of organic solvents.

Pure aqueous solution $\mathrm{pH}=4.005$; $\mathrm{MeOH}-\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(10 \% 4.243,20 \% 4.468,50 \%$
$5.125,64 \% 5.472,84.2 \% 6.232$ ) [17c];
$\mathrm{EtOH}-\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(10 \% 4.230,20 \% 4.488,40 \%$
4.973, 70\% 5.466) [17c];
$2-\mathrm{PrOH}-\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(10 \% 4.249,30 \% 4.850,50 \%$
5.210, 70\% 5.522) [17c];

1,2-Ethanediol- $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(10 \% 4.127,30 \%$
4.419, 50\% 4.790, 70\% 5.238) [17c];

2-Methoxyethanol- $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ (20\% 4.505, 50\%
5.380, $80 \%$ 6.715) [17c];
$\mathrm{AN}-\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(5 \% 4.166,15 \% 4.533,30 \%$
$5.000,50 \% 5.461,70 \% 6.194)$ [17c];

1,4-Dioxane- $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(10 \% 4.329,30 \% 5.015$, 50\% 5.782) [17c];
$\mathrm{PC}-\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(8 \% 4.177,16 \% 4.347)[17 \mathrm{e}] ;$
Ethylene carbonate- $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(8 \% 4.145,16 \%$ 4.301) [17e];

Glycerol— $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ (20\% 4.172, 40\% 4.420) [17f];

THF- $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(10 \% 4.26,20 \% 4.62,30 \% 4.93$, $40 \% 5.31,50 \% 5.56,60 \% 5.86,70 \% 6.17$ )
[17g];
FA (100\% 6.43) [17h]
When pH value of KHPh buffer is not available for the solvent composition under study, it is obtained interpolating by multilinear regression [17c, d]. For example, pH for KHPh in $\mathrm{EtOH}-\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ can be obtained by pH $(\mathrm{KHPh})=3.99865-0.46452 x^{1 / 2}+9.5545 x- 8.4053 x^{2 / 3}$, where $x$ is the mole fraction of EtOH . In Ref. [17], $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{KHPh})$ at other temperatures and some pH values for other primary pH standards are also included.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-180.jpg?height=714&width=1029&top_left_y=198&top_left_x=212)
Figure 6.4 Titration curves of 20 ml of ( 3 mM phthalic acid $\left.\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{Ph}\right)+2 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{CF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3} \mathrm{H}\right)$ in various AN- $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ mixtures with $1.0 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NOH}$ (in MeOH ). Water content in $(\mathrm{v} / \mathrm{v}) \%$ is shown on each curve. Obtained using $\mathrm{Si}_{3} \mathrm{~N}_{4}$-ISFET (Shindengen Indus. Co.) as pH sensor and titrating at $0.005 \mathrm{ml} \mathrm{min}^{-1}$ [18].

of trifluoromethanesulfonic acid $\left(\mathrm{CF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3} \mathrm{H}\right)$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{Ph}$ were titrated with a strong base, $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NOH}$ (in MeOH ), to get an entire picture of the titration curves of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{Ph}$. With the decrease in water content, the value of $\left(\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 2}-\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 1}\right)$ for $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{Ph}$ markedly increases and the slope at the $\times$-mark increases, showing a decrease in the buffer action of the $\mathrm{HPh}^{-}$solution. For $\mathrm{AN}-\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ mixtures, the buffer capacity of 0.05 m KHPh is insufficient if the water content is less than $20 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{v} \%$. For DMSO- and DMF- $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ mixtures, this insufficiency occurs if the water content is less than $\sim 35 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{v} \%$, because protophilic DMSO and DMF interact strongly with water and decrease the protic property of water.

Although the IUPAC method has some drawbacks, it is still reliable and convenient as far as the RVS or other pH standard is available for the solvent under study. Moreover, the method of determining $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{RVS})$ has been proposed by IUPAC [16, 17]. Thus, if necessary, we can provide ourselves $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{RVS})$ values for new solvent systems.

## Other Methods of pH Measurements in Nonaqueous Solutions

If the IUPAC method is not applicable, one of the following procedures can be used, although the reliability of the data decreases to considerable extent:

1. If there is an acid with a known $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ value and without any tendency to homoconjugate, the solution of the $1: 1$ mixture of the acid and its conjugate base is used as the pH standard for calibrating the glass electrode. If the acid is of the HA type, the pH of the solution is calculated by the relation $\mathrm{pH}(\mathrm{S})=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \gamma\left(A^{-}\right)$, where $\gamma\left(A^{-}\right)$is the activity coefficient of $A^{-}$calculated by the Debye-Hückel
theory (see footnote 10 in Chapter 2). If the acid is of the $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$type, pH $(\mathrm{S})=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}-\log \gamma\left(\mathrm{BH}^{+}\right)$. The pH of the sample solution is obtained by using Eq. (6.4). For example, the $1: 1$ mixture of picric acid and its $\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$or $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$salt ( $3-5 \mathrm{mM}$ each) has been used in various organic solvents as pH standard. The $1: 1$ mixture of a weak base (e.g. aniline or diphenylguanidine) and its perchlorate or trifluoromethanesulfonate is also applicable as pH standard. ${ }^{7)}$
2. If there is a strong acid that completely dissociates, we prepare a solution of the acid of a known concentration and use it to calibrate the glass electrode. The pH of the solution is calculated by estimating $\gamma\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$using the Debye-Hückel theory.

In the above, we assumed the use of a pH -sensitive glass electrode. The Nernstian response of the glass electrode has been confirmed in many polar organic solvents. ${ }^{8)}$ However, the response of the glass electrode in nonaqueous solutions is often very slow. In particular, in protophilic aprotic solvents such as DMSO and DMF, it may take over an hour to reach a steady potential. Various efforts have been made to improve the response speed of the glass electrode [19], including preconditioning of the electrode surface, the replacement of the internal solution of the glass electrode (by a nonaqueous solution or by mercury), and searching for the glass composition suitable for nonaqueous solutions. However, these attempts have not been successful. The hygroscopic gel layer existing on the surface of the glass electrode is essential for its excellent response in aqueous solutions. However, in nonaqueous solutions, the gel layer seems to retard the response speed of the glass electrode.

Recently, $\mathrm{Ta}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ - and $\mathrm{Si}_{3} \mathrm{~N}_{4}$-type pH-ISFETs have been used in nonaqueous systems, by making them solvent resistant [20]. In various polar nonaqueous solvents, they responded with Nernstian or near-Nernstian slopes and much faster than the glass electrode. The titration curves in Figure 6.5 demonstrate the fast (almost instantaneous) response of the $\mathrm{Si}_{3} \mathrm{~N}_{4}$-ISFET and the slow response of the glass electrode. Some applications of the pH -ISFETs are discussed in Section 6.3.1. ${ }^{9)}$

### 6.2.3 <br> Determination of Autoprotolysis Constants

The autoprotolysis constant, $K_{\mathrm{SH}}$, for the autoprotolysis of an amphiprotic solvent SH (Eq. (6.5)) is defined by Eq. (6.6):

$$
\mathrm{SH}+\mathrm{SH} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}+\mathrm{S}^{-}
$$

7) Chemicals such as $\mathrm{HPic}, \mathrm{HClO}_{4}, \mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NPic}$ and $\mathrm{BH}^{+} \mathrm{ClO}_{4}{ }^{-}$( $\mathrm{B}=$ amine) must be handled with enough care because they are explosive.
8) We can check the Nernstian response of the glass electrode ourselves. The method that uses a weak acid and its conjugate base is described in Section 6.3.1. Another method is to use a strong acid and to get the $\mathrm{pH}-$ potential relation of the glass electrode by varying its concentrations. Though the latter method
seems simple, it should be noted that the reaction of the strong acid with basic impurities in the solvent or with solvent itself may cause a serious error.
9) The rapid response of the pH -ISFET has also been used in an ionic liquid (IL), ethylammonium nitrate $\left[\mathrm{EtNH}_{3}\right]\left[\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right]$, to obtain the autoprotolysis constant, $K_{\mathrm{SH}}$, of the IL. See Section 13.3.1.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-182.jpg?height=654&width=1157&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=207)
Figure 6.5 Titration curves of 5 mM picric acid in AN with 1 M $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NON}$ (in MeOH ), recorded simultaneously with four pH sensors but at different titration speeds for (a)-(c). In (a), curve 1 is for $\mathrm{Si}_{3} \mathrm{~N}_{4}$-ISFET, curve 2 for $\mathrm{Ta}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$-ISFET, curve 3 for $\mathrm{IrO}_{2} \mathrm{pH}$ sensor and curve 4 for glass electrode. The pH -ISFETs were obtained from Shindengen Indus. Co. and the $\mathrm{IrO}_{2} \mathrm{pH}$ sensor from TOA Electronics Ltd. [20c].

$$
K_{\mathrm{SH}}=a\left(\mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{~S}^{-}\right) / a(\mathrm{SH})^{2}
$$

or

$$
K_{\mathrm{SH}}=a\left(\mathrm{SH}_{2}^{+}\right) a\left(\mathrm{~S}^{-}\right) \quad \text { for } a(\mathrm{SH})=1 \quad(\text { pure } \mathrm{SH})
$$

If the solvent is a mixture of water and organic solvent ZH , several equilibria may be established, more or less simultaneously, when ZH and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ have similar acid-base properties:

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{ZH}+\mathrm{ZH}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{ZH}_{2}^{+}+\mathrm{Z}^{-}, & \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{ZH} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{Z}^{-} \\
\mathrm{ZH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{ZH}_{2}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}, & \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}
\end{array}
$$

If solvent ZH is inert in acid-base property, it is not involved in the autoprotolysis but works only as a medium for the autoprotolysis of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. Even in the case of solvent mixtures, Eqs. (6.5) and (6.6) will be used to express the autoprotolysis processes.

Potentiometry is a useful method to determine $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}\left(=-\log K_{\mathrm{SH}}\right)$; in principle, we get the difference between the potentials of the hydrogen electrode at $a\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}^{+}\right)=1$ and $a\left(\mathrm{~S}^{-}\right)=1$ and then divide it by the Nernstian slope. ${ }^{\mathbf{1 0}}$ The IUPAC Commission on Electroanalytical Chemistry has proposed a method to determine $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ in organic solvents of high permittivities and in water-organic solvent mixtures [21]. In the method, the standard emf of the cell, $\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}(p=1 \mathrm{~atm})\right| \mathrm{HX}+\mathrm{MX}(\mathrm{SH})|\mathrm{AgX}| \mathrm{Ag}$

[^17]$(\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{Li}, \mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{K}, \ldots ; \mathrm{X}=\mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{Br}, \ldots)$, is obtained on the one hand and the emf of the cell, $\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{H}_{2}(p=1 \mathrm{~atm})\right| \mathrm{MS}\left(m_{\mathrm{S}}\right)+\mathrm{MX}\left(m_{\mathrm{X}}\right)(\mathrm{SH})|\mathrm{AgX}| \mathrm{Ag}$ ( $\mathrm{S}^{-}=$lyate ion), is measured on the other hand by varying $m_{\mathrm{S}}$ and $m_{\mathrm{X}}$. This method can give reliable $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ values in amphiprotic organic solvents and water-organic solvent mixtures, if MS is soluble and stable. We can replace the hydrogen electrode by a pH -sensitive glass electrode or other pH sensors. DMSO is an aprotic solvent but alkali metal salts of its lyate ion, dimsyl ion $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{SOCH}_{2}{ }^{-}$, can be obtained fairly easily. Thus, $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ in DMSO has been obtained by measuring the potentials of a hydrogen electrode in the solution of $\mathrm{HClO}_{4}$ and dimsyl ion (Courtot-Coupez, J., LeDemezet, M. (1969) Bull. Soc. Chim. Fr., 1033). In other neat aprotic solvents, however, it is usually difficult to get stable lyate ions and the IUPAC method is not applicable. One of the methods applied in such a case is to prepare the solution of a strong acid (e.g. $\mathrm{HClO}_{4}$ ) and that of a strong base (e.g. $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NOH}$ ) and measure the potentials of the pH sensor in the two solutions. Then, $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ can be obtained approximately from the potential difference between the two solutions ( $\Delta E, \mathrm{mV}$ ):
$$
\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}} \geq(\Delta E / 59.2)-\log \{c(\text { strong acid }) \times c(\text { strong base })\}
$$
where $c$ denotes the concentration. This method has been used to determine $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ in some aprotic solvents [22a]. It should be noted, however, that the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ values determined by this method have somewhat a indistinct nature (Section 3.2.2). Another method of obtaining $\mathrm{p} K_{\text {SH }}$ is an indirect method that uses the relation $K_{\mathrm{SH}}=K_{\mathrm{a}} \times K_{\mathrm{b}}$, where $K_{\mathrm{a}}$ is the dissociation constant of an acid ( HA or $\mathrm{BH}^{+}$) and $K_{\mathrm{b}}$ is that of a conjugate base ( $\mathrm{A}^{-}$or B ). $K_{\mathrm{a}}$ and $K_{\mathrm{b}}$ are determined independently by such methods as spectrophotometry, conductimetry and potentiometry.

Some $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ values in nonaqueous solvents and water-organic solvent mixtures are listed in Table 6.6 [21,22]. They are in molal scale but can easily be converted to molar scale by the relation,

$$
\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}(\text { molar })=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}(\text { molal })-2 \log \rho
$$

where $\rho$ is the density of solvent SH in $\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$.

## 6.3 <br> Applications of Potentiometry in Nonaqueous Solutions

Potentiometry and potentiometric titrations are widely used in studying various types of reactions and equilibria in nonaqueous systems (Sections 6.3.1-6.3.4). They also provide a convenient method of solvent characterization (Section 6.3.5). Moreover, if the electrode potentials in different solvents can accurately be compared, potentiometry is a powerful method of studying ion solvation (Section 6.3.6).

### 6.3.1 <br> Acid-Base Reactions in Nonaqueous Solvents

Potentiometry is often used in determining dissociation constants, $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$, and homoconjugation constants, $K^{f}\left(\mathrm{HA}_{2}{ }^{-}\right)$, for HA-type acids in aprotic solvents. Therefore, to

Table 6.6 Autoprotolysis constants ( $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ ) for pure and mixed solvents.
| Pure solvents |  | Mixed solvents |  |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Solvents ${ }^{\boldsymbol{a}}$ | $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ | wt\% | Mole fraction | $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ |
| Sulfuric acid | 3.33 | Water-methanol ${ }^{b}$ |  |  |
| 2-Aminoethanol | 5.7 | 0 | 0 | 13.997 |
| Formic acid | 6.2 | 20 | 0.1232 | 14.055 |
| $N$-Methylformamide | 10.74 | 50 | 0.3599 | 14.097 |

| Ethanol | 18.88 | 80 | 0.6100 | 15.91 |

| Ethyl acetate | 22.83 | 90 | 0.7232 | 14.37 |
| Nitromethane* | $\geq 24$ | Water-1,2-dioxane ${ }^{b}$ |  |  |
| $N$-Methyl-2-pyrrolidinone* | $\geq 24.2$ | 20 | 0.0486 | 14.62 |
| Sulfolane* | 24.45 | 45 | 0.1433 | 15.74 |

| Acetone* | $\geq 32.5$ | 40 | 0.2264 | 15.32 |
