method. The electrode oxidation of $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$has been studied in several solvents using ESR spectroscopy [42c]. From the ultimate formation of relatively stable tricyanomethylenimine radical anion, the following steps have been considered:

$$
2 \mathrm{CN}^{-} \xrightarrow{-2 \mathrm{e}^{-}} 2 \mathrm{CN} \bullet \longrightarrow(\mathrm{CN})_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CN}^{-}}(\mathrm{NC})_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{N}^{-} \xrightarrow{-\mathrm{e}^{-}, \mathrm{CN}^{-}}(\mathrm{NC})_{2} \mathrm{C}=\cdots \mathrm{N}-\mathrm{CN}^{\bullet-}
$$

### 8.2.4 <br> Electrode Reactions of Dissolved Oxygen, Dissolved Hydrogen, Carbon Dioxide and Solvated Electrons

## Dissolved Oxygen

The solubility of oxygen at 1 atm and $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ has been determined to be 1.0 mM in water, 2.1 mM in DMSO, 4.8 mM in DMF, 8.1 mM in AN, $\sim 10 \mathrm{mM}$ in hydrocarbons and $\sim 25 \mathrm{mM}$ in fluorocarbons [43a]. In aqueous solutions, the first reduction step of the dissolved oxygen is either two-electron or four-electron process, depending on the catalytic activity of the electrode material and on the solution composition.

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\text { 2-electron process : } & \mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \\
& \left(\mathrm{O}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{O}_{2}{ }^{2-} \text { in strongly alkaline solutions }\right) \\
\text { 4-electron process : } & \mathrm{O}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{array}
$$

In aprotic solvents, however, the first step is one-electron process to produce a superoxide ion $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{\bullet-}$ [44].

$$
\mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{O}_{2}^{\bullet}-
$$

If a tetraalkylammonium salt is used as supporting electrolyte, this process is either reversible or quasireversible and occurs at around -0.8 V versus aq. SCE in various aprotic solvents and with various electrode materials ( $\mathrm{Hg}, \mathrm{Pt}, \mathrm{GC}$ ). The formation of $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}$at room temperature has been confirmed by ESR spectroscopy, directly in pyridine and with the aid of spin-trapping using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide in DMSO [45]. If a Brønsted acid is added to the solution, the first step is converted to a two-electron process: $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}$produced in the first step is protonated to form $\mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{H}^{\bullet}$, which is more reducible than $\mathrm{O}_{2}$. Thus, $\mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{H}^{\bullet}$ is further reduced to $\mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{H}^{-}$at the potential of the first step. According to detailed polarographic study in DMSO- $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ mixtures, about $30 \% \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{v} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is needed to convert the polarographic wave from one-electron process to two-electron process [46]. A metal ion $\mathrm{M}^{+}$interacts with $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}$to form an ion pair $\mathrm{M}^{+}-\mathrm{O}_{2}^{\bullet-}$ (often insoluble) and shifts the half-wave potential of the first wave in a positive direction [47]. Electrogenerated superoxide $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}$can act either as a nucleophile or as an electron donor and has been used in organic syntheses [48].

In dipolar aprotic solvents, the second step of the oxygen reduction is $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}$. However, if $\mathrm{H}^{+}$is available from the solvent or from protic impurities, $\mathrm{O}_{2}{ }^{2-}$ reacts with $\mathrm{H}^{+}$to form $\mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{H}^{-}$or $\mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$. Sometimes, the $\mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{H}^{-}$or $\mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$ is further reduced at more negative potentials to give $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ as the final product. Detailed information concerning the electrochemistry of molecular oxygen can be found in Ref. [43b].

## Dissolved Hydrogen

At a platinum electrode that is slightly activated by platinization, the dissolved hydrogen in various solvents is oxidized nearly reversibly by $\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftarrows 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$. We can determine by cyclic voltammetry the standard potential of this process. If the standard potentials in various solvents are compared using a common potential scale, the Gibbs energies of transfer of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$can be obtained [49]. With electrodes other than platinum, it is difficult to observe reversible oxidation of dissolved hydrogen [49b].

## Carbon Dioxide

The electrode reduction of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ has considerably been studied in nonaqueous solvents (see review article [50]). Carbon dioxide dissolves more in nonaqueous solvents than in water, the solubilities of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ at 1 atm and $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ being $0.25_{6} \mathrm{M}$ in AN , $0.18_{6} \mathrm{M}$ in $\mathrm{DMF}, 0.15_{9} \mathrm{M}$ in $\mathrm{MeOH}, 0.14_{1} \mathrm{M}$ in PC and $0.13_{0} \mathrm{M}$ in DMSO compared to 0.033 M in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}[51]$. One-electron reduction of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ forms $\mathrm{CO}_{2}{ }^{-}$anion radical, but the radical is either adsorbed or not adsorbed at the electrode surface, depending on the electrode materials used. Then, $\mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-}$undergoes further inner-sphere processes when it is adsorbed, while $\mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-}$undergoes further outer-sphere processes when it is not adsorbed. The details of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ reduction in aqueous and nonaqueous solutions are discussed in Section 12.5, in connection with its future applicability as the carbon resources.

Here, we consider the outer-sphere processes that occur at a mercury (Hg) electrode in DMF- $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$ [52]. The cyclic voltammogram for the reduction of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is usually irreversible, though somewhat reversible behavior of the $\mathrm{CO}_{2} / \mathrm{CO}_{2}^{\bullet-}$ couple can be observed at a high voltage scan rate (e.g. $4400 \mathrm{~V} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ ) [52c], and from this, the standard potential of the $\mathrm{CO}_{2} / \mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-}$couple has been obtained to be $\sim$ -2.21 V versus aq. SCE or $\sim-2.6 \mathrm{~V}$ versus $\mathrm{Fc} / \mathrm{Fc}^{+}$couple. The number of electrons per molecule for an irreversible wave was close to 1 when $\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]>5 \mathrm{mM}$, in agreement with the formation of oxalate $\left(2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-} \rightarrow{ }^{-} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-}\right)$as well as the formation of CO and $\mathrm{CO}_{3}{ }^{2-}\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}^{\bullet-}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \rightarrow{ }^{\bullet} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CO}_{2}{ }^{-}\right.$followed by ${ }^{\bullet} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CO}_{2}{ }^{-}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{CO}_{3}{ }^{2-}$ (at electrode) or ${ }^{\bullet} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CO}_{2}{ }^{-}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}{ }^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO} \mathrm{CO}_{3}{ }^{2-}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ (in solution)]. When the DMF solution contained residual water and upon decreasing the $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ concentration, the number of electrons per molecule approached 2, indicating that the formation of formate, $\mathrm{HCOO}^{-}$, occurred by $\mathrm{CO}_{2}^{\bullet-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{HCO}_{2}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$followed by $\mathrm{HCO}_{2}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{HCOO}^{-}$(at electrode) or $\mathrm{HCO}_{2}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \mathrm{HCOO}^{-}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ (in solution). The product distribution obtained after the preparative-scale electrolysis of $\sim 100 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{CO}_{2}$ at an Hg electrode in DMF- $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$ showed a decrease in the percentage of oxalate and an increase in the percentage of CO with the decrease in temperature: i.e. the percentages of oxalate and CO were 80 and $20 \%$, respectively, at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ but 20 and $80 \%$, respectively, at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The percentages of oxalate and CO also depended on the $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ concentration: at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, they were 90 and $10 \%$, respectively, at $\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]=20$ mM but 23 and $77 \%$, respectively, at $\left[\mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]=217 \mathrm{mM}$. Interestingly, the electrolysis catalyzed by radical anions of aromatic esters and nitriles exclusively produced oxalate in the DMF medium.

## Solvated Electrons

In strongly basic solvents such as HMPA, amines and liquid ammonia, solvated electrons are relatively stable. In these solvents, if the supporting electrolyte is the salt of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$or $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$, blue solvated electrons, $\mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{SH}^{-}}$, are generated from the surface of the platinum electrode polarized at a very negative potential.

$$
\mathrm{e}^{-}(\text {cathode })+\mathrm{SH} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{SH}}^{-}
$$

This process was shown by voltammetry to be reversible, with the standard potentials equal to -3.44 V versus $\mathrm{Ag} / 0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{AgClO}_{4}$ in HMPA ( $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) and -2.90 V versus $\mathrm{Ag} / 0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{AgClO}_{4}$ in methylamine ( $-50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) [ $53 \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ ]. The stability of solvated electrons in these solvents has been attributed to the capture of electrons into the cavity of the solvent. For the details of electrochemistry of solvated electrons, see Ref. [53c, d].

## 8.3 <br> Polarography and Voltammetry of Organic Compounds

Polarographic and voltammetric studies of organic compounds are often carried out in nonaqueous solutions for the following reasons: (1) Many organic compounds that are insoluble in water dissolve in appropriate nonaqueous solvents. (2) Polarography and voltammetry in aprotic solvents are useful to study the electronic properties of organic species, including extremely unstable intermediates. This is because, in such solvents, the first steps for the reduction and oxidation of organic compounds are usually one-electron processes that form radical anions and cations, respectively, and the potentials of these processes reflect the LUMO and HOMO of the compounds. Several excellent books on organic electrochemistry are available. Among them, Organic Electrochemistry (fourth edition, edited by Lund and Hammerich, 2001) [54] is the most authoritative, covering all aspects of organic electrochemistry. References [55, 56] also cover electrochemistry of organic compounds. The book by Mann and Barnes [57], though somewhat old, deals with electrode reactions of a variety of organic and inorganic species in nonaqueous solutions. Meites and Zuman [58] and Bard [59] have compiled many data of organic electrochemistry. References [4, 5] show how to get electrochemical data and mechanistic information of organic reactions, mainly from cyclic voltammetry. In addition to these books, many useful reviews that discuss topical problems are available (e.g. [60-63]). In this section, some characteristics of the electrode reactions of organic species in nonaqueous solutions are outlined.

### 8.3.1 <br> Reductions of Organic Compounds

As described in Section 4.1.2, electrophilic organic compounds are reducible at the electrode. Some reducible organic compounds are listed in Table 8.5 with the potentials of the first reduction step in dipolar aprotic solvents. As described in

Ref. [54], organic compounds undergo various complicated electrode reductions. Here, however, only simple but typical cases are considered; they are reductions by the outer-sphere electron-transfer reactions and the dissociative electron-transfer reactions.

## Outer-Sphere Electron-Transfer Reactions

In reductions of this type, the first step is usually one-electron process that produces a radical anion, $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ :

$$
\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}
$$

$\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ is generally reactive, but its lifetime varies over a wide range, depending on its intrinsic reactivity and on the reactivity of the environment. If $Q^{\bullet-}$ has a delocalized negative charge, it is more or less stable unless the environment is reactive. Here, an environment of low reactivity (low Lewis acidity) is realized in aprotic solvents using tetraalkylammonium salts as supporting electrolyte. If $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ is stable enough, we can generate it by controlled-potential electrolysis and study its characteristics by measuring its UV/vis absorption spectra or ESR signals (Sections 9.2.1 and 9.2.2). However, study by cyclic voltammetry is by far the most popular; by employing high-voltage scan rates and low-temperature techniques, we can study the behavior of the radical anion even when its lifetime is very short ( $10 \mu$ s or less, Section 8.4). Because Q and $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ have similar structures and sizes, the first reduction step of organic compounds in aprotic solvents is usually highly reversible. Some data on the standard rate constants ( $k_{\mathrm{s}}$ ) are provided in Table 8.6. They were obtained by the AC

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-254.jpg?height=443&width=704&top_left_y=196&top_left_x=212)
Figure 8.13 Influence of a weak proton donor (e.g. water) on the DC polarogram of the two-step reduction of an organic compound in an aprotic solvent. Curve a: without proton donor; curves b-e: with increasing amounts of proton donor.

impedance method and the experimental results were corrected for the double-layer effect. Some of them approach $\sim 30 \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. Recently, cyclic voltammetry, which uses ultramicroelectrodes and fast scan rates, has often been employed in studying the kinetics of highly reversible electrode reactions, as discussed in Section 8.4.

Many organic compounds in aprotic solvents are reduced in two steps, as shown by curve a in Figure 8.13. The second step is, in principle, the formation of a dianion, $\mathrm{Q}^{2-}$.

$$
\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Q}^{2-}
$$

As described in Section 4.1.2, for aromatic hydrocarbons $(\mathrm{AH})$ in the gas phase, the standard potential of the second step is expected to be by $4-5 \mathrm{~V}$ more negative than that of the first step. In solutions, however, the experimental standard potential of the second step $\left(E_{2}^{0}\right)$ is about $0.5-0.8 \mathrm{~V}$ more negative than that of the first step $\left(E_{1}^{0}\right)$ [64]. This difference in the gas phase and in solutions is caused by the fact that the solvation energy of ions depends approximately on the square of the charge number; the solvation energy of the dianion $Q^{2-}$ is four times that of the anion radical $Q^{\bullet}{ }^{-}$, while the solvation energy of the neutral $Q^{0}$ is nearly negligible. This phenomenon also occurs for other kinds of compounds. When the solvation of the dianion $\left(\mathrm{Q}^{2-}\right)$ is extraordinarily strong by such reasons as the decreased size of dianion, the order of $E_{1}^{0}$ and $E_{2}^{0}$ is inverted and $E_{2}^{0}$ becomes more positive than $E_{1}^{0}$. In such a case, the reduction of Q becomes one-step two-electron process and it occurs at a potential equal to $\left(E_{1}^{0}+E_{2}^{0}\right) / 2$. Such a potential inversion occurs, for example, with 3,6 -dinitrodurene (3,6-dinitro-1,2,4,5-tetramethyl-benzene) with $\left(E_{1}^{0}-E_{2}^{0}\right)=-280 \mathrm{mV}$; it is considered that for its dianion, the benzene ring is distorted into the boat form [60, 65].

The $\mathrm{Q}^{2-}$-dianions with delocalized charges can remain somewhat stable and give a well-defined reversible CV curve for the second step. However, because $Q^{2-}$ is a much stronger base than $Q^{\bullet-}$, it easily reacts with protons that are originated from the protic impurity such as $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ or from the solvent itself.

$$
\mathrm{Q}^{2-}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{QH}^{-}, \quad \mathrm{QH}^{-}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{QH}_{2}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-255.jpg?height=636&width=767&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=345)
Figure 8.14 Reduction of fullerene, $\mathrm{C}_{60}$, in AN-toluene ( $1: 5$ ) with $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NPF}_{6}$ as supporting electrolyte at $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, recorded using (a) cyclic voltammetry at $100 \mathrm{mV} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ and (b) differential pulse voltammetry at $25 \mathrm{mV} \mathrm{s}^{-1} . E_{1 / 2}:-0.98,-1.37,-1.87,-2.35$, -2.85 and -3.26 V versus $\mathrm{Fc} / \mathrm{Fc}^{+}$[62].

If this occurs, the second step becomes less reversible or shifts to a more positive potential (see Figure 8.13). Occasionally, $\mathrm{QH}_{2}$ is further reduced at more negative potentials. Moreover, the second step that appears at negative potentials is easily influenced by the cation of the supporting electrolyte (see footnote 12).

In contrast to the two one-electron steps in aprotic solvents, many organic compounds in water and other protic solvents give one two-electron reduction wave as shown in Figure 8.13, curve e. ${ }^{7)}$

$$
\mathrm{Q}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{QH}_{2}
$$

The transition from the mechanism in aprotic solvent to that in protic solvent is important and is discussed later.

Some organic compounds undergo multistep one-electron reversible reductions. As shown in Figure 8.14, Buckminsterfullerene ( $\mathrm{C}_{60}$ ) in $\mathrm{AN} /$ toluene at $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, for example, gives six one-electron reversible steps in cyclic voltammetry $[62,66] . \mathrm{C}_{60}$ can accept up to six electrons to form diamagnetic $\mathrm{C}_{60}{ }^{6-}$ because its LUMO is triply degenerated. ${ }^{8)}$
7) Some organic compounds ( Q ) undergo twostep one-electron reduction $\left(\mathrm{Q} \rightarrow \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-} \rightarrow\right. \mathrm{Q}^{2-}$ ) even in aqueous solutions. It occurs when $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ (semiquinone) is stable and its disproportionation ( $2 \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{Q}^{2-}$ ) is difficult (see also footnote 10).
8) All of the six anions are stable on the voltammetric time scale but, if they are generated
by controlled-potential electrolysis, only $\mathrm{C}_{60}{ }^{-}$ to $\mathrm{C}_{60}{ }^{4-}$ are stable [62a]. Neutral $\mathrm{C}_{60}$ is almost insoluble in such solvents as DMF, AN and THF, but its anions dissolve easily. Thus, these anions can be generated from a suspension of $\mathrm{C}_{60}$.

Various factors give influences on the reductions of organic compounds in aprotic solvents. They are discussed below.

1. Relation between the LUMO and the half-wave potential of the first reduction wave When an organic compound, Q , is reduced, it accepts an electron from the electrode to its lowest unoccupied molecular orbital. Here, the energy of the LUMO of Q corresponds to its electron affinity (EA). If the energies of LUMO $\left(\varepsilon_{\text {lu }}\right)$ for a series of analogous compounds are obtained by the molecular orbital method, there should be a relationship:

$$
E_{1 / 2}(1)=E_{\mathrm{ref}}(\mathrm{abs})-\varepsilon_{\mathrm{lu}}-\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Q} / \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}\right)
$$

where $E_{1 / 2}(1)$ is the half-wave potential of the first wave, $E_{\text {ref }}(\mathrm{abs})$ is the absolute potential of the reference electrode and $\Delta G_{\mathrm{SV}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Q} / \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}\right)=G_{\mathrm{SV}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}\right)-G_{\mathrm{SV}}^{\circ}(\mathrm{Q})$, i.e. the difference between the solvation energies of Q and $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$.

Two classical results are shown in Figures 8.15 and 8.16. In Figure 8.15, Hoijink obtained near-linear relations between $E_{1 / 2}(1)$ and $\varepsilon_{\text {lu }}$ for aromatic hydrocarbons (AHs). Similar near-linear relations have been observed for many organic compounds (quinones, nitorobenzenes, ketones and others). Figure 8.16 shows Peover's results [63]. He measured the spectra of the charge-transfer complexes of hexamethylbenzene (electron donor D ) and various aromatic compounds (electron acceptors A) in dichloromethane and obtained the charge-transfer transition energies ( $h \nu_{\mathrm{CT}}$ ):

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-256.jpg?height=728&width=688&top_left_y=1162&top_left_x=212)
Figure 8.15 Half-wave potentials of the first wave of various conjugated hydrocarbons in $96 \%$ dioxane-water and the calculated values of the energy of LUMO obtained by (a) Hückel's and (b) Wheland's approximation. Plotted from the data in Hoijink, G.J. (1955) Rec. Trav. Chim., 74, 1525.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-257.jpg?height=654&width=700&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=345)
Figure 8.16 The relation between $v_{C T}$ of the spectra of chargetransfer complexes and the half-wave potentials of electron acceptors [63]. Electron acceptor: derivatives of phthalic anhydride, quinone and nitrobenzene and tetracyanobenzene and tetracyanoethylene.

$$
\mathrm{D}+\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{h v_{\mathrm{CT}}} \mathrm{D}^{+} \cdot \mathrm{A}^{-}
$$

$h \nu_{\mathrm{CT}}$ is correlated with the ionization potential (IP) of D and the electron affinity of A by

$$
h \nu_{\mathrm{CT}}=\mathrm{IP}-\mathrm{EA}+C
$$

where $C$ is a constant.
In Figure 8.16, the values of $h \nu_{\text {CT }}$ are plotted against the half-wave potentials of the acceptors measured in acetonitrile. A near-linear relation is observed. Peover also obtained, by an electron capture method, the values of EA of aromatic hydrocarbons in the gas phase and confirmed a near-linear relation with the reduction potentials in the solution phase.

It is certain that, in the first reduction step in aprotic solvents, an electron is accepted by the LUMO of the organic compound. However, it was fortunate that this conclusion was deduced from studies that either ignored the influence of solvation energies or used the results in different solvents. Shalev and Evans [67] estimated the values of $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Q} / \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet}\right)$ for 22 substituted nitrobenzenes and 9 quinones from the half-wave potentials measured by cyclic voltammetry. For quinones and some substituted nitrobenzenes, the values of $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Q} / \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}\right)$ in a given solvent were nearly independent of the EA values. Similar results had been observed for alternant aromatic hydrocarbons (AAH) in AN (Section 8.3.2) [68]. If $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Q} / \mathrm{Q}^{\ominus-}\right)$ does not vary with EA, there should be a linear relation of unit slope between $E_{1 / 2}$ and EA. Shalev and Evans [67], moreover, got a near-linear
relation between $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Q} / \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}\right)$ and EA for some other substituted nitrobenzenes. Here again, the $E_{1 / 2}-\mathrm{EA}$ relation should be linear, though the slope deviates from unity. ${ }^{9)}$
2. Effect of dynamic solvent property on the reduction kinetics As described in Section 4.1.3, the outer-sphere reduction of large organic compounds is suitable to examine the role of dynamic solvent properties in determining the standard rate constant, $k_{\mathrm{s}}$. Curve 2 in Figure 8.9 is for the reduction of benzophenone in various solvents; a near-linear relation of unit slope is observed between $\log k_{\mathrm{s}}$ and $\log \tau_{\mathrm{L}}{ }^{-1}$, where $\tau_{\mathrm{L}}$ is the longitudinal solvent relaxation time of the subpico- to picosecond region [69a]. For the one-electron reduction of $p$-dicyanobenzene, the relation between $\left\{\ln \left[k_{\mathrm{s}} /\left(\Delta G_{\text {os }}^{*}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]+\Delta G_{\text {os }}^{*} / R T\right\}$ and $\ln \tau_{\mathrm{L}}$ (see Eq. (4.11)) has been studied, as in the case of nickelocene in Section 8.2.2, and a roughly linear relation of unit slope has been obtained [35d].
3. Effect of Lewis acidity of aprotic solvents on half-wave potentials For an organic compound ( Q ) in dipolar aprotic solvents, the half-wave potential ( $E_{1 / 2}$ ) of the first reduction step tends to shift to positive direction with an increase in solvent Lewis acidity (i.e. acceptor number) [69]. It is because, for the redox couple $\mathrm{Q} / \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$, the reduced form $\left(\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}\right)$ is energetically more stabilized than the oxidized form (Q) with increasing solvent acidity. The positive shift in $E_{1 / 2}$ with solvent acceptor number has been observed with quinones [69c], benzophenone [69a,d] and anthracene [69d]. With fullerene ( $\mathrm{C}_{60}$ ), the positive shift in $E_{1 / 2}$ with solvent acidity parameter, $E_{\mathrm{T}}$, has been observed for the reductions of $\mathrm{C}_{60}$ to $\mathrm{C}_{60}^{-}, \mathrm{C}_{60}^{-}$to $\mathrm{C}_{60}{ }^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{60}{ }^{2-}$ to $\mathrm{C}_{60}{ }^{3-}[66 \mathrm{c}]$. However, the positive shift in $E_{1 / 2}$ is not apparent if the charge in $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ is highly delocalized, as in the case of perylene and fluoren-9one [69d].
4. Effect of Brønsted acids on the reduction mechanisms Brønsted acids (proton donors, HA) have significant influences on the reduction of organic compounds in aprotic solvents. If a weak Brønsted acid such as water is added step-wise to the electrolytic solution, the height of the first polarographic wave increases at the expense of that of the second wave (Figure 8.13). By the addition of a weak acid, following reactions occur at or near the electrode:

$$
\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}+\mathrm{HA} \xrightarrow{k} \mathrm{QH}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{A}^{-} ; \quad \mathrm{Q} \mathrm{H}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{QH}^{-} ; \quad \mathrm{QH}^{-}+\mathrm{HA} \longrightarrow \mathrm{QH}_{2}+\mathrm{A}^{-}
$$

The QH r radical formed by protonation is usually easier to reduce than Q and the first wave approaches a two-electron process. By theoretical analysis of the increase in the first-wave height, we can determine the rate constant $(k)$ for the
9) Another interesting result obtained by Shalev and Evans [67] is that the rate constant for the homogeneous self-exchange electron transfer, $\log k_{\mathrm{ex}}$, between various Q and $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ in DMF decreases linearly with the increase in $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Q} / \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}\right)$. This is because $\log k_{\mathrm{ex}}$ is mainly governed by the solvent reorganization
energy from the configuration around $Q$ to that around $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$, which is expected to increase with the increase in $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Q} / \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}\right)$. Here, $\log k_{\text {ex }}$ can be determined from the broadening of the ESR signal of $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ in the presence of Q (Section 9.2.2).
protonation reaction [70]. However, the regeneration of Q by such reactions as $\mathrm{QH}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{QH}^{-}+\mathrm{Q}$ and $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}+\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Q}^{2-}+\mathrm{Q}$ may also contribute to the exaltation of the first wave.

If the Brønsted acid is strong enough, the proton addition to $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ rapidly occurs and a new wave appears before the first wave because the ratio $\left[\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}\right] /[\mathrm{Q}]$ is kept very small. In some cases, $\mathrm{QH}^{+}$formed by the reaction of Q and $\mathrm{H}^{+}$gives a new wave at much more positive potential than the first wave. Inversely, if the Brønsted acid is very weak, it does not influence the first wave but shifts the second wave to positive direction, because the dianion $\mathrm{Q}^{2-}$ is a stronger base than $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ (Figure 8.13). Possible mechanisms for the reduction of an organic compound in the presence of a Brønsted acid are often represented by the 'square-scheme'. The mechanism actually occurring depends on the strength of the Brønsted acid and the base strength of $\mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ and $\mathrm{Q}^{2-}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-259.jpg?height=287&width=617&top_left_y=775&top_left_x=618)

By using Brønsted acids of known $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values and by examining their influence on the reduction of Q , we can get some qualitative information about the base strengths of $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ and $\mathrm{Q}^{2-}$. Recently, some quantitative studies have been carried out on this problem. For example, Niyazymbetov et al. [66f] studied the $p K_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of dihydrofullerene, $\mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}_{2}$ and related species by combining cyclic voltammetry, controlled-potential electrolysis and acid-base titrations. They got (or estimated) in DMSO a $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}}$ of $\sim 8.9$ for $\mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}^{\bullet} ; \mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 1}$ and $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 2}$ of 4.7 and 16 for $\mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}_{2} ; \mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 1}$, $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 2}$ and $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 3}$ of $9, \sim 9$ and $\sim 25$ for $\mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}_{3}^{\bullet}$; and $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 2}, \mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 3}$ and $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 4}$ of $16, \sim 16$ and $\sim 37$ for $\mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}_{4}$. To obtain these estimates, they used a relation as in Eq. (8.4):

$$
\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 1, \mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}^{\bullet}}=\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 2, \mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}_{2}}+\frac{E_{2, \mathrm{C}_{60}}^{0}-E_{1, \mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}^{\bullet}}^{0}}{0.059}
$$

where $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 1, \mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}}$ and $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a} 2, \mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}_{2}}$ are for $\mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}^{\bullet} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{C}_{60}^{\bullet-}+\mathrm{H}^{+}$and $\mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{C}_{60}{ }^{2-} \mathrm{H}^{+}$, respectively, and $E_{1, \mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}^{\bullet}}^{0}$ and $E_{2, \mathrm{C}_{60}}^{0}$ are for $\mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}^{-}$and $\mathrm{C}_{60}^{\bullet-}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{C}_{60}{ }^{2-}$, respectively. On the other hand, Cliffel and Bard [66g] used voltammetry and near-IR spectrophotometric titrations to obtain $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{a}} \sim 3.4$ for $\mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}^{\bullet}$ in o-dichlorobenzene.

If water is added to the solution of anthracene in DMF, the radical anion of anthracene is protonated as described above and the first wave in DC polarography is gradually converted to a two-electron process as in Figure 8.13. The influence of water on the reduction of anthraquinone is somewhat different from the case of anthracene [70]. By the addition of a large amount of water, the height of the first wave increases, while the second wave decreases in height and shifts to the more
positive side; this is similar to the case of anthracene. However, in AC polarography, the two waves for anthraquinone are reversible and their reversibilities do not decrease by the addition of water; this shows that both $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ and $\mathrm{Q}^{2-}$ do not react with the proton from water. Moreover, according to the ESR measurement, $Q^{\bullet-}$ in the bulk of the solution does not disappear by the addition of water. If the exaltation of the first wave is due to the proton addition, $Q^{\bullet-}$ must disappear more rapidly. In order to explain these phenomena, the following disproportionation mechanism was proposed:

$$
2 \mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{Q}^{2-}
$$

In anhydrous DMF, the equilibrium is almost completely to the left and the first wave is a one-electron process. ${ }^{\mathbf{1 0}}$ ) However, if water is added, the equilibrium slightly shifts to the right, as is apparent from the fact that the second wave approaches the first wave. This is because $Q^{2-}$ interacts strongly with water by hydrogen bonding. As a result, some part of $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ formed at the first wave returns to Q and is reduced again at the electrode to exalt the first wave.

In some cases, the intramolecular hydrogen bonding stabilizes $Q^{\bullet-}$ and shifts the first wave to positive side. For example, the first wave of $o$-nitrophenol ( $E_{1 / 2}=-0.33 \mathrm{~V}$ ) is much more positive than those of $m$ - and $p$-nitrophenols ( $E_{1 / 2}=-0.58 \mathrm{~V}$ and -0.62 V ) [70]. This is due to the intramolecular hydrogen bonding of the radical anion of $o$-nitrophenol.
5. Effect of cations A metal ion (Lewis acid) has an influence on the reductions of organic compounds in aprotic solvents. Namely, the metal ion, $\mathrm{M}^{+}$, forms ion pairs with $Q^{\bullet-}$ and $Q^{2-}$ and shifts the first and second waves, to a greater or lesser extent, to positive potentials [71]. This influence is pronounced in protophobic aprotic solvents (AN, PC, etc.), in which $\mathrm{M}^{+}$is solvated only weakly. The influence increases in the order $\mathrm{K}^{+}<\mathrm{Na}^{+}<\mathrm{Li}^{+}$and it also depends on the Lewis basicity of $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$ and $\mathrm{Q}^{2-}$. The example in Figure 8.17 is for 1,2-naphthoquinone in AN [71b]. Because the radical anion of 1,2-naphthoquinone interacts strongly with metal ions forming a chelate-like structure, the positive shifts of the first and second waves are marked and much larger than the case of 1,4naphthoquinone. ${ }^{11)}$
10) If the first and the second waves are well separated, the difference in their half-wave potentials, $\Delta E_{1 / 2}$, and the disproportionation constant, $K=[\mathrm{Q}]\left[\mathrm{Q}^{2-}\right] /\left[\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet}\right]^{2}$, are related by $\Delta E_{1 / 2}=-(R T / F) \ln K$.
11) If the ion pair $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}-\mathrm{M}^{+}$is soluble, its formation constant, $K_{\mathrm{A}}=\left[\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}-\mathrm{M}^{+}\right] /$ ( $\left[\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}\right]\left[\mathrm{M}^{+}\right]$), is related to the positive shift of
the half-wave potential $\Delta E_{1 / 2}$ by $\Delta E_{1 / 2}=(R T / F)\left(\ln K+\ln \left[\mathrm{M}^{+}\right]\right)$, where $\left[\mathrm{M}^{+}\right]$is the concentration of free metal ion. The relation is used to determine the $K_{\mathrm{A}}$ value. However, it should be noted that the relation is not applicable if the ion pair is insoluble, as is often the case.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-261.jpg?height=473&width=762&top_left_y=196&top_left_x=350)
Figure 8.17 Influence of metal ions on the DC polarographic reduction wave of 1,2-naphthoquinone ( 0.5 mM ) in $\mathrm{AN}-0.05 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$ [71b]. Metal ions (5 mM): curve 1, none; $2, \mathrm{~K}^{+} ; 3, \mathrm{Na}^{+}$; $4, \mathrm{Li}^{+}$and $5, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$.

The difference in the size of $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$ions also has considerable influences:
(i) $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$ions, which do not form stable ion pairs with $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$, scarcely influence the potential of the first reduction wave. However, they influence the potential of the second wave; small $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$ions such as $\mathrm{Me}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$tend to shift the second wave to positive direction forming ion pairs with $Q^{2-}$, while larger $R_{4} N^{+}$ ions such as $\mathrm{Hex}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$tend to shift the second wave to negative direction by the double-layer effect. ${ }^{\mathbf{1 2})}$
(ii) The influence of $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$on the kinetics of the one-electron reduction of organic compounds is worth noting [72]. Petersen and Evans [72a], for example, determined the standard rate constants ( $k_{\mathrm{s}}$ ) for the reductions of many organic compounds at a mercury electrode in AN , using $\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{Hep}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$ as supporting electrolytes. As shown in Figure 8.18, for the reductions at negative potentials, the $k_{\mathrm{s}}$ value was larger with $\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$than with $\mathrm{Hep}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$. Generally, the $k_{\mathrm{s}}$ value for the reduction at negative potentials decreases with increasing size of $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$. Interestingly, however, the standard potential and the rate of homogeneous self-exchange electron-transfer reaction are not influenced by $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$. Thus, the influence on $\log k_{\mathrm{s}}$ has been attributed to the blocking effect of the compact layer of adsorbed $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$ions, whose thickness
12) The potential of the second wave of organic compounds and dissolved oxygen, $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Q}^{2-}$, are often influenced significantly by $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$of the supporting electrolyte. Small $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$ions, electrostatically attracted to the negatively charged electrode surface, seem to form ion pairs with $\mathrm{Q}^{2-}$ rather than with $\mathrm{Q}^{\bullet-}$, causing a positive shift of the second wave. In contrast, with the increase in the size of $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$, the distance of OHP from the
electrode increases and makes the potential at the closest approach of $Q^{\bullet-}$ more negative, resulting in the more difficult formation of $\mathrm{Q}^{2-}$ and in a negative shift of the second wave. For the double-layer effect that considers the cationic size of the supporting electrolyte, see, for example, Fawcett, R.W. and Lasia, A. (1990) J. Electroanal. Chem., 279, 243.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-262.jpg?height=515&width=824&top_left_y=198&top_left_x=221)
Figure 8.18 Relationship between the half-wave nitromesitylene; 13, dicyanobenzene; 14, potential and the standard rate constant for the nitromethane; 15 , nitroethane; 16 , nitropropane; first reduction wave of various organic 18, cyclooctatetraene; 20, 4-cyanopyridine; 21, compounds in AN. The influence of $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$of the benzophenone; $22,4,4^{\prime}$ supporting electrolyte [72a]. Compounds: 1, dimethoxybenzophenone; 25, trans-stilbene; hexafluorobiacetyl; 2 , $p$-benzoquinone; names are abbreviated for $4,7,12,17,19,23,24$ 3, 1,4-naphthoquinone; 5, oxygen; 6, 9, 10- and 26. The upper limit of determination of $k_{s}$ anthraquinone; $8, p$-nitrotoluene; $9,4,4^{\prime}$ - $\quad$ was $1 \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ (i.e. $\log k_{s} \sim 0$ ). methoxybenzyl; 10, 2,3-butanedione; 11,

increases with alkyl chain length. The electron transfer occurs by tunneling through the layer. ${ }^{13)}$ It has been reported that when large $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$ions such as $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}, \mathrm{Hex}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Oct}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$are adsorbed onto the electrode surface, the three alkyl chains facing the electrode are bent and the charge centers of $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$are kept at $\sim 0.37 \mathrm{~nm}$ from the surface, irrespective of the size of R [72c].

## Dissociative Electron-Transfer Reactions

The reductions of halogenated organic compounds (RX) involve the cleavage of carbon-halogen bonds [73]. Depending on the solvent, supporting electrolyte, electrode material and potential, it is possible to electrogenerate either alkyl radicals ( $\mathrm{R}^{\bullet}$ ) or carbanions ( $\mathrm{R}^{-}$), which then can lead to the formation of dimers ( $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{R}$ ), alkanes ( RH ) and olefins [ $\mathrm{R}(-\mathrm{H})$ ]:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{RX}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{RX}^{\bullet-}, \quad \mathrm{RX}{ }^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k} \mathrm{R}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{X}^{-}, \quad \mathrm{R}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}^{-} \\
& 2 \mathrm{R}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{R}(\text { dimerization }) \text { or }[\mathrm{RH}+\mathrm{R}(-\mathrm{H})] \text { (disproportionation) } \\
& \left(\mathrm{R}^{\bullet} \text { or } \mathrm{R}^{-}\right)+\mathrm{SH} \rightarrow \mathrm{RH}+\left(\mathrm{S}^{\bullet} \text { or } \mathrm{S}^{-}\right) \quad(\mathrm{SH}: \text { solvent })
\end{aligned}
$$

13) It was shown in Ref. [72d] that the standard electron transfer rate constants ( $\log k_{\mathrm{s}}$ ) for each nitroalkane, $\mathrm{R}^{\prime} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$, are linearly related to the hydrodynamic diameter of $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$ ( $d_{\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}}$). Moreover, it was shown that the log $k_{\mathrm{s}}$ data for all nitroalkanes are in a single linear relation against ( $d_{\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}}+\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{R}^{\prime}}$ ), where
$\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{R}^{\prime}}$ is roughly the radius of $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}$
(Figure 8.19). The distance, ( $d_{\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}}+r_{\mathrm{R}^{\prime}}$ ), may have some relation to the tunneling of electrons from the electrode to the nitro group, although the smallest distance from the electrode to the nitro group is largely independent of the size of $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-263.jpg?height=603&width=778&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=355)
Figure 8.19 Standard electron transfer rate constants for four nitroalkanes, $\mathrm{R}^{\prime} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$, obtained in $\mathrm{AN} / 0.10 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$ at a HMDE [72d]. Data are plotted versus the sum of the hydrodynamic diameter of $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}\left(d_{\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}}\right)$and the radius of $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}\left(r_{\mathrm{R}^{\prime}}\right)$.

If a mercury electrode is used, $\mathrm{R}^{\bullet}$ reacts with mercury to form organomercury compounds, whereas $\mathrm{R}^{-}$tends to form such species as RH and $\mathrm{R}(-\mathrm{H})$.

In aprotic solvents, the radical anion $\mathrm{RX}^{\bullet-}$ for aryl halides has been detected as an intermediate. In cyclic voltammetry of aryl halides, though an irreversible twoelectron reduction occurs at low scan rate, a reversible one-electron reduction occurs at high scan rate. Thus, it is possible to get the values of the standard potential ( $E^{0}$ ) for the $\mathrm{RX} / \mathrm{RX}^{\bullet-}$ couple and the rate constant $(k)$ for $\mathrm{RX}^{\bullet-} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}^{\bullet}$ (therefore, the lifetime of $\mathrm{RX}^{\bullet-}$ ). In Figure 8.20, the relation between $E^{0}$ and $\log k$ for aryl bromides in DMF is linear with a slope of $\sim 0.5[61 \mathrm{f}]$. It is apparent that the lifetime of $\mathrm{RX}^{\bullet-}$, obtained by $1 / k$, increases with the positive shift of $E^{0}$. In contrast, the existence of $\mathrm{RX}^{\bullet}$ - for alkyl monohalides has never been confirmed. With these compounds, it is difficult to say whether the two processes, i.e. electron transfer and bond cleavage, are step-wise or concerted ( $\mathrm{RX}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{X}^{-}$). According to Savéant [61f], the smaller the bond dissociation energy, the larger the tendency for the concerted mechanism to prevail over the step-wise mechanism.

Studying the electrochemical reduction of halogenated organic compounds has practical importance, especially for organic syntheses [73]. Moreover, the reductive cleavage of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond can be applied to convert hazardous chlorinated compounds; for example, polychlorinated biphenyl (PCB) is converted to biphenyl by the reduction in DMF at -2.8 V versus SCE [74].

potentials of the first oxidation step in nonaqueous solvents. By using a solvent of weak basicity and a supporting electrolyte that is difficult to oxidize, we can expand the potential window on the positive side and can measure oxidations of difficult-tooxidize substances such as benzene. Anodic oxidations of organic compounds usually occur via complicated mechanisms but many of them are of practical importance. Various books and review articles on the electrolytic oxidation of organic

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-265.jpg?height=462&width=823&top_left_y=198&top_left_x=352)
Figure 8.20 Cleavage rate constant $k$ of aryl bromides as a function of their standard potential $E^{0}$ in DMF [ 61 g ]. 1, 2-isopropyl-4-nitrophenyl; 2, 4-nitrophenyl; 3, 2-methyl-3nitrophenyl; 4, 2-methyl-4-nitrophenyl; 5, 3-fluorenyl; 6, 1-fluorenyl; 7, 2,6-dimethyl-4-nitrophenyl; 8, 3-benzoylphenyl; 9, 4-benzoylphenyl; 10, 9-anthracenyl; 11, 3-acetylphenyl; 12, 4-acetylphenyl; 13, 1-naphthyl.

compounds are available [54-57, 63]. Here, however, we focus our discussion only on the oxidation of $\mathrm{C}_{60}$ and AHs .

A reversible one-electron oxidation CV peak for $\mathrm{C}_{60}$ has been obtained at room temperature and at $100 \mathrm{mV} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ in 1,1,2,2-tetrachloroethane (TCE) containing $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NPF}_{6}$ as supporting electrolyte. In other solvents such as AN and DCE , the oxidation was irreversible and contained multielectrons. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap for $\mathrm{C}_{60}$ in TCE was 2.32 V [62a].

In solvents of weak basicity (e.g. AN), aromatic hydrocarbons are oxidized, at least in principle, in two steps. In the first step, the compound gives an electron in its highest occupied molecular orbital to the electrode to form a radical cation $\left(\mathrm{AH}^{\bullet+}\right),{ }^{14)}$ whereas, in the second step, it gives another electron to the electrode to form dication $\mathrm{AH}^{2+}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{AH} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{AH}^{\bullet+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \\
& \mathrm{AH}^{\bullet+} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{AH}^{2+}+\mathrm{e}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$

The two-step oxidation really occurs, for example, with 9,10 -diphenylanthracene. As shown by the CV curve in Figure 8.21, the two waves are reversible or nearly reversible. The radical cation of 9,10-diphenylanthracene is fairly stable and, as in the case of radical anions, its ESR signals can be measured.

On the contrary, the radical cation of anthracene is unstable. Under normal voltammetric conditions, the radical cation $\mathrm{AH}^{\bullet+}$ formed at the potential
14) The potential for the first oxidation step is linearly related to the donor number of the solvents [29a].

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-266.jpg?height=614&width=734&top_left_y=196&top_left_x=212)
Figure 8.21 Cyclic voltammogram for the oxidation of 9,10diphenylanthracene ( 1 mM ) at a platinum microelectrode in 0.5 M $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}-\mathrm{AN}$. Scan rate: $1000 \mathrm{~V} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ [73].

of the first oxidation step, undergoes a series of reactions (chemical $\rightarrow$ electrochemical $\rightarrow$ chemical $\rightarrow \cdots$ ) to form polymerized species. This occurs because the dimer, trimer, etc. formed from $\mathrm{AH}^{\bullet+}$, are easier to oxidize than AH . As a result, the first oxidation wave of anthracene is irreversible and its voltammetric peak current corresponds to that of a process of several electrons (Figure 8.22a). However, if fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) at an ultramicroelectrode (UME) is used, the effect of the follow-up reactions is removed and a reversible one-electron CV curve can be obtained (Figure 8.22b) [75]. By this method, the half-life of the radical cation of anthracene has been determined to be $\sim 90 \mu \mathrm{~s} .{ }^{15)}$ Okazaki et al. [76a] used FSCV of up to $30000 \mathrm{~V} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at a UME to determine the oxidation and reduction potentials of 9-substituted anthracenes and correlated them with the results of the calculated energy levels of the HOMO and LUMO. Nozaki et al. [76b] used FSCV of up to $300000 \mathrm{Vs}^{-1}$ at a UME to study the kinetics of the dimerization of 9-methoxy-anthracene cation radicals in nitroethane.

The radical cation of 9,10-diphenylanthracene is much more stable than that of anthracene because, with 9,10 -diphenylanthracene, the 9 - and 10 -positions, which are reactive because of the high unpaired electron densities, are masked by phenyl groups and the unpaired electrons are delocalized. The stabilization of the radical
15) Useful methods for detecting short-lived cationic radicals are the fast-scan cyclic voltammetry at a UME (Section 8.4.2) and cyclic voltammetry at low temperatures (Section 8.4.3). It is preferable to prepare
electrolytic solutions using solvents of low basicity (AN, DME, $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$, etc.) and to remove water almost completely with the aid of active alumina (Sections 8.1.2 and 10.2) [68].

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-267.jpg?height=942&width=774&top_left_y=980&top_left_x=345)
Figure 8.22 Effect of scan rate on the cyclic voltammogram for the oxidation of anthracene ( 2.4 mM ) at a platinum ultramicroelectrode in $0.6 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}-\mathrm{AN}$. Electrode radius: $5 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ [73].

cation of anthracene also occurs by introducing other substituents such as $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ and $-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$ at positions 9 and 10.

If the solution contains a nucleophile ( $\mathrm{Nu}:^{-}$) or a base (B) or consists of a basic solvent, the radical cation ( $\mathrm{AH}^{\bullet}+$ ) formed at the first oxidation step reacts as follows [54]:

$$
\mathrm{AH}^{\bullet+}+\mathrm{Nu}:^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{A}^{\bullet}-\mathrm{Nu}, \quad \mathrm{AH}^{\bullet+}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{~A}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{BH}^{+}
$$

The products of the above two reactions, $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{A}^{\bullet}-\mathrm{Nu}$ and $\mathrm{A}^{\bullet}$, are easier to oxidize than AH and are oxidized to form $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{A}^{+}-\mathrm{Nu}$ and $\mathrm{A}^{+}$, which chemically react with $\mathrm{Nu}:^{-}$and B to form electrically neutral final products. Thus, in the presence of a nucleophile or a base, aromatic hydrocarbons give a one-step two-electron oxidation wave. ${ }^{\mathbf{1 6})}$

Parker [68] obtained in acetonitrile the oxidation and reduction potentials ( $E_{\mathrm{ox}}$ and $\left.E_{\text {red }}\right)$ of alternant aromatic hydrocarbons by cyclic voltammetry and examined how those potentials are related with the ionization potential and the electron affinity of the compounds (Table 8.8). As expected, he found linear relations of unit slopes between $E_{\mathrm{ox}}$ and IP and between $E_{\text {red }}$ and EA . Moreover, he found that $E_{\mathrm{ox}}$ and $E_{\text {red }}$ of each AAH were symmetrical with respect to a common potential $M_{\text {AAH }}(-0.31 \mathrm{~V}$ versus SCE). The values of ( $E_{\mathrm{ox}}-M_{\mathrm{AAH}}$ ) and ( $E_{\mathrm{red}}-M_{\mathrm{AAH}}$ ) are correlated with the values of IP and EA, obtained in the vacuum, by $E_{\mathrm{OX}}-M_{\mathrm{AAH}}=\mathrm{IP}-\phi+\Delta G_{\mathrm{SV}+}^{\circ}$ and $E_{\text {red }}-M_{\text {AAH }}=\mathrm{EA}-\phi-\Delta G_{\text {SV- }}^{\circ}$, respectively (Figure 8.23). Here, $\phi$ is the work function of graphite and is equal to 4.34 eV , and $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sV}+}^{\circ}$ and $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sV}-}^{\circ}$ are the differences in solvation energies for the $0 /+1$ and $0 /-1$ couples of AAH. Experimentally, $\Delta G_{\text {sv }+}^{\circ}$ and $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}-}^{\circ}$ were almost equal, not depending on the species of AAH , and were equal to -1.94 eV in AN.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-268.jpg?height=285&width=727&top_left_y=1319&top_left_x=212)
Figure 8.23 Relationship between the oxidation potential ( $E_{\mathrm{Ox}}$ ), the reduction potential ( $E_{\text {Red }}$ ), the ionization potential (IP), the electron affinity (EA) and the solvation energies ( $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}+}^{\circ}, \Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}-}^{\circ}$ ) for alternant aromatic hydrocarbons [68].

16) The anodic partial fluorination of organic compounds occurs in weak Lewis base solvents (AN, $\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$, DME) containing fluoride ions ( $\mathrm{QH} \xrightarrow{-\mathrm{e}^{-}} \mathrm{QH}^{\bullet+} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{F}^{-}]{-\mathrm{e}^{-}-\mathrm{H}^{+}} \mathrm{Q}-\mathrm{F}$ or $\left.\mathrm{QH} \xrightarrow{-2 \mathrm{e}^{-}-\mathrm{H}^{+}} \mathrm{Q}^{+} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{F}^{-}} \mathrm{Q}-\mathrm{F}\right)$. This occurs because $\mathrm{F}^{-}$in aprotic solvents is extremely nucleophilic (Table 2.7). In contrast to
chemical fluorination that uses hazardous reagents and to anodic fluorination in anhydrous HF, this method can be carried out under mild conditions and without using hazardous reagents. For details, see Ref. [54a], Chapter 25.

There is a relation $\Delta E \equiv E_{\text {red }}-E_{\mathrm{ox}}=\mathrm{EA}-\mathrm{IP}-2 \Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}$. Here, we imagine an AAH with $\mathrm{IP}=6.26 \mathrm{eV}$ and $\mathrm{EA}=2.38 \mathrm{eV}$. Then, we get $\Delta E=0$, and $50 \%$ of the AAH dissolved in AN is expected to ionize by disproportionation $\left(2 \mathrm{AAH} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{AAH}^{+}+\right. \mathrm{AAH}^{-}$). This ionization is motivated by the ion solvation energies, in contrast to the fact that in the solid state, electrical conductivity is obtained only with graphite, for which $\mathrm{IP}=\mathrm{EA}=4.34 \mathrm{eV}$.

## 8.4 <br> Cyclic Voltammetry for Electrochemical Studies in Nonaqueous Solutions

Cyclic voltammetry is one of the most useful techniques for studying chemistry in nonaqueous solutions. It is especially useful in studying electrode reactions that involve an unstable intermediate or product. By analyzing cyclic voltammograms, we can elucidate the reaction mechanisms and can know the thermodynamic and kinetic properties of the unstable species. Some applications were described in previous sections. Much literature is available on cyclic voltammetry dealing with the theories and practical methods of measurements and data analyses [3-5, 77]. In this section, three useful cyclic voltammetry techniques are outlined.

### 8.4.1 <br> Digital Simulation in Cyclic Voltammetry

For many inorganic and organic substances, it is rare that the electrode reaction is simply an electron transfer at the electrode surface. In most cases, the electron transfer process is accompanied by preceding and/or following reactions, which are either chemical or electrochemical. For example, for the electrode reduction of substance A, mechanisms described here can be considered.

```
E mechanism: $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$
EE mechanism: $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}$
EEE mechanism: $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{D}, \ldots$
EC mechanism: $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}$ (or $2 \mathrm{~B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}$, etc.)
ECE mechanism: $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}$ (or $2 \mathrm{~B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}$, etc.), $\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{D}$
EEdisp mechanism: $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}, 2 \mathrm{~B} \rightarrow \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{C}$
( $\mathrm{E}=$ electrochemical reaction, $\mathrm{C}=$ chemical reaction and disp $=$ disproportiona-
tion)
```

Moreover, each of the chemical and electrochemical reactions can have different reaction rates and reversibilities. All of them are reflected in cyclic voltammograms $[3-5]$. If we measure cyclic voltammograms of an electrode reaction, changing parameters such as potential range, voltage scan rate, temperature, electrode material
and solution composition and analyze the voltammograms appropriately, we can get various information about the electrode reaction. However, except the cases where the electrode process is very simple, it is not an easy matter to analyze the cyclic voltammograms appropriately.

Digital simulation software, which is now commercially available, is useful in analyzing cyclic voltammograms of complicated electrode reactions [78]. If we assume a possible reaction mechanism and can get simulated CV curves that fit the experimental CV curves, we can confirm the reaction mechanism and obtain thermodynamic and kinetic parameters concerning the electron transfer and chemical processes. By the development of simulation softwares, cyclic voltammetry has become a very powerful technique. On the contrary, without a simulation software, cyclic voltammetry is not as convenient. ${ }^{17)}$

### 8.4.2 <br> Ultramicroelectrodes in Cyclic Voltammetry

The UME described in Section 5.54 displays its ability when used in nonaqueous solutions [79].

1. Because the current that flows at a UME is extremely small ( $10 \mathrm{pA}-100 \mathrm{nA}$ ), we can keep the effect of the $i R$-drop very small and can measure voltammograms even in solutions of high resistance, i.e. in solutions without supporting electrolyte [80] and in solutions of nonpolar solvents such as benzene, toluene and hexane [81]. UMEs have also been used for voltammetry in the gaseous phase [82] and in supercritical fluids [83].
2. Because the current that flows at a UME is small, we can scan the electrode potential at extremely high rates, sometimes reaching $1000000 \mathrm{Vs}^{-1}$ or even more [84]. By using cyclic voltammetry at such fast scan rates, we can detect intermediates and products with very short lifetimes and study their thermodynamic and kinetic properties [61e, 77c]. We can also determine the rate constants of very rapid electrode reactions, as shown in Table 8.9.

UMEs of $\sim 10 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ in diameter and voltammetric instruments for use with such UMEs are commercially available. Electrodes of smaller dimensions can be prepared
17) Determination of homogeneous electron transfer rate constants by cyclic voltammetry: Evans and Gilicinski [72b] used cyclic voltammetry assisted by a simulation method to determine the rate constants for a homogeneous electron-transfer reaction $\mathrm{Ox}_{1}+\operatorname{Red}_{2} \underset{k_{b}}{\stackrel{k_{f}}{\rightleftarrows}} \operatorname{Red}_{1}+\mathrm{Ox}_{2}$. They measured cyclic voltammograms for the mixtures of $\mathrm{Ox}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{Ox}_{2}$. If the above reaction does not occur, the
reduction-reoxidation peaks for $\mathrm{Ox}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{Ox}_{2}$ should be obtained. However, if the above reaction occurs in the solution near the electrode, the cyclic voltammograms are distorted. Under appropriate conditions, it is possible to determine the homogeneous electron-transfer rate constants, $k_{\mathrm{f}}$ and $k_{\mathrm{b}}$, by simulating the cyclic voltammograms. They employed this method for $\mathrm{Ox}_{1}=\mathrm{RNO}_{2}(\mathrm{R}:=$ Me, Et, $i$-Pr and $t$-Bu) and $\mathrm{Ox}_{2}=$ terephthalonitrile.

in the laboratory, though it needs considerable skills [85]. In order to use UMEs successfully for high-speed voltammetry in highly resistive solutions, care must be taken concerning the effects of the ohmic drop and the capacitance of the cell system [76b, 85, 86]. Moreover, two types of voltammograms, i.e. curves (a) and (b) in Figure 5.23, should be used appropriately according to the objective of the measurements. For practical experimental techniques, see Refs [76, 78-84]. Some recent topics related to UMEs are described in footnotes. ${ }^{\mathbf{1 8}, \mathbf{1 9}, \mathbf{2 0}}$

### 8.4.3 <br> Low-Temperature Electrochemistry and Cyclic Voltammetry [87, 88]

The low-temperature electrochemistry technique is useful in studying electrode reactions involving unstable products or intermediates. Lowering the temperature by $30-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ decreases the reaction rate of the unstable species to one-tenth of the original value. It is equivalent to a 10-fold increase in the voltage scan rate. Figure 8.24 shows the effect of temperature on the cyclic voltammogram for the oxidation of

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-272.jpg?height=437&width=781&top_left_y=961&top_left_x=214)
Figure 8.24 Effect of temperature on the cyclic voltammogram for the oxidation of $1,2,3,6,7,8$-hexahydropyrene ( 2.5 mM ) at a platinum electrode in $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$-butyronitrile. Scan rate: $50 \mathrm{mV} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ [87a].

18) In AN without supporting electrolyte, the positive end of the potential window reaches +6 V versus $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{Ag}^{+}$, and the electrolytic oxidations of the substances with high ionization potentials, including methane, butane, pentane, heptane, rare gases ( $\mathrm{Ar}, \mathrm{Kr}, \mathrm{Xe}$ ) and oxygen, can be observed [80a]. In $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ at $-70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and without supporting electrolyte, $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}, \mathrm{Rb}^{+}, \mathrm{K}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$were oxidized at a platinum UME ( $10 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ diameter) [80d].
19) The infinite dilution half-wave potential of 7,7,8,8-tetracyanoquinodimethane (TCNQ) versus $\mathrm{Fc} / \mathrm{Fc}^{+}$was obtained by measuring
steady-state UME voltammograms (Figure 5.23b) in AN with $1-70 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{Me}_{4} \mathrm{NPF}_{6}$ and by extrapolating the half-wave potential to zero ionic strength [80c].
20) Ultramicroelectrodes of extremely small radius $(1-2 \mathrm{~nm}$, called nanodes or nanoelectrodes) [89] are suitable for determining the rate constants of very fast electrode reactions. For example, the standard rate constant, $k_{\mathrm{s}}$, has been determined to be $220 \pm 120 \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ for the ferrocenium/ferrocene couple in $\mathrm{AN}-0.3 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$.

1,2,3,6,7,8-hexahydropyrene. At ambient temperatures, it does not give a re-reduction peak. However, at $-60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, reversible oxidation and re-reduction waves are observed. Low-temperature electrochemistry techniques are often used for studying electrode reactions of organic compounds and metal complexes. Examples of the solvent-supporting electrolyte systems for low-temperature electrochemistry are listed in Table 8.10. Some specific situations occur for voltammetry at low temperatures: increase in solution resistance, decrease in the rate of diffusion of electroactive species, decrease in the Nernstian slope, etc. Appropriate care must be taken for these. The use of a UME is advantageous in overcoming the influence of solution resistance. The reference electrode is usually kept at a constant temperature (e.g. $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), while the temperature of the indicator electrode is varied at much lower temperature ranges. Fortunately, the thermal potential developed between the solution of the reference electrode and that containing the indicator electrode is negligibly small. For the practical aspects of electrochemistry at reduced temperatures, the review by Evans and Lerke [88] is useful.

## 8.5 <br> Voltammetry of Isolated Nanoparticle Solutions (Nanoelectrochemistry)

Voltammetry in solutions of metal nanoparticles of various sizes is the subject of research and has been reviewed in detail [90]. Here, we consider a typical case of Au nanoparticles stabilized by organothiolate ligand monolayers: this kind of nanoparticle is called monolayer-protected clusters (MPCs).

The MPCs are synthesized as follows: Chloroaurate is phase-transferred, using transfer reagent $\mathrm{Oct}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+} \mathrm{Br}^{-}$, from water to toluene, where organothiol is added and reduces $\mathrm{Au}^{\text {III }}$ to a colorless $\mathrm{Au}^{\text {I }}$ thiolate complex that is not isolated.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& {\left[\mathrm{Oct}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}+\mathrm{AuCl}_{4}^{-}\right]_{\text {water }}} \\
& \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Oct}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}+\mathrm{AuCl}_{4}^{-}\right]_{\text {toluene }} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{RSH}}\left[\mathrm{Oct}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}+\mathrm{Au}^{\mathrm{I}} \mathrm{SR}+\mathrm{RSSR}_{\text {toluene }}\right.
\end{aligned}
$$

A reducing agent, $\mathrm{BH}_{4}{ }^{-}$, is then added and Au nanoparticles are formed. By selecting the thiol: Au ratio, reaction temperature, etc. we can get MPCs of sizes from 1 to 5 nm , though the raw product is their mixture (polydispersity). The nature of organothiolate dominates the MPC solubilities: those based on alkanethiolate or arylthiolate are soluble in moderately polar to nonpolar media but not in water. The MPC is stable and the raw product can be fractionated into monodisperse samples. Here, partial replacement of thiolate by other functional ligand is also possible.

Three types of voltammetry exist by the size of MPCs: they are bulk continuum voltammetry, quantized double-layer charging voltammetry and voltammetry of moleculelike nanoparticles [90]. This metal-to-molecule transition of voltammetry is summarized in Figure 8.25.

Bulk continuum voltammetry is for large nanoparticles of $>3-4 \mathrm{~nm}$ sizes. The relation that distinguishes such nanoparticles from even smaller ones is $\Delta V=z e / C_{\text {CLU }}$, where $\Delta V$ is the change in the electrochemical potential of a nanoparticle with a double-layer capacitance $C_{\text {CLU }}$, that is incurred upon transfer of $z$ electrons to/from the nanoparticle. The subscript 'CLU' means an individual metal cluster. If $\Delta V \leq k_{\mathrm{B}} T_{25}(=25.7 \mathrm{meV})$, successive electron transfer to/from the nanoparticle will result in a continuum - as opposed to step-wise - change in the nanoparticle's potential. The bulk continuum behavior or voltammetry of smooth current-potential relation is expected for $C_{\text {CLU }}$ larger than about 6 aF .

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-274.jpg?height=607&width=1035&top_left_y=1287&top_left_x=209)
Figure 8.25 Summary of electrochemical behavior by MPC core size, showing estimated energy gaps (HOMO-LUMO gaps) and electrochemical energy gaps (which is the spacing between the first oxidation peak and the first reduction peak for the native nanoparticle) [90].

In quantized double-layer charging voltammetry, the MPCs with smaller sizes (diameter: $\sim 2.0 \mathrm{~nm}$ for $\mathrm{Au}_{225}$ and 1.6 nm for $\mathrm{Au}_{140}$ ) behave as quantum capacitors. Figure 8.26 (upper) is an example of this type of voltammogram [91]: differential pulse voltammogram (DVP) was measured for hexanethiolate-coated $\mathrm{Au}_{147}$ MPCs ( $177 \mu \mathrm{M}$ ) dissolved in 10 mM bis(triphenylphosphoranylidene) ammonium tetrakis (pentafluorophenyl)borate (BTPPATPBF ${ }_{20}$ )-1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) solution. A two-electrode arrangement, a Pt microelectrode as working electrode and a silver wire as both counter and quasireference electrode, was used. Evenly spaced 15 peaks ( 0.25 V in average space, corresponding to $C_{\text {CLU }}=0.64 \mathrm{aF}$ ) were observed.

By further decrease in nanoparticle size (e.g. $\sim 1 \mathrm{~nm}$ for $\mathrm{Au}_{25}$ ), a molecule-like phenomenon appears and an energy gap is observed in the voltammogram. Figure 8.26 (lower) is a voltammogram for $170 \mu \mathrm{M}$ hexanethiolate-coated $\mathrm{Au}_{38}$ in the same solution as in Figure 8.26 (upper). If the charging energy ( 0.3 V ; estimated as the difference between the two oxidation peaks) is corrected for from 1.2 V , which is the gap in peak potentials between the first oxidation $(1 / 0)$ and the first reduction $(0 /-1)$, the HOMO-LUMO gap of 0.9 V is obtained. Though under limited conditions, the monolayer of thiolate ligand of the MPCs works only to stabilize the Au core against aggregation. For the voltammetric behavior at the functionalized MPCs, see Ref. [90].

Nanoelectrochemistry is a branch of electrochemistry that deals with electrochemical phenomena of materials at nanometer size levels. This includes, in addition to voltammetry of solutions of isolated nanoparticle, electrochemistry at nanoscopic electrodes (nanoelectrode), electrochemistry of films of nanoparticles
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-275.jpg?height=610&width=926&top_left_y=1201&top_left_x=345)

Figure 8.26 Differential pulse voltammetric responses for MPC solutions measured at a Pt microelectrode; as-prepared $177 \mu \mathrm{M}$ hexanethiolate(C6S)-coated $\mathrm{Au}_{147}$ (upper) showing 15 high-resolution quantized doublelayer (QDL) peaks and $170 \mu \mathrm{M}$ C6S-Au 38 (lower) showing a HOMO-LUMO gap [91]. It can be
seen that the as-prepared solution contains a residual fraction of $\mathrm{Au}_{38}$ that smears out the charging response at potentials where QDL peaks overlap. The electrode potential was scanned from negative to positive. For the solution and electrodes, see text.
and electrochemistry of single nanopores in membranes [90]. Nanoelectrodes are the electrodes with submicrometer (nanometer) dimensions and useful to investigate the transition from micro-scale to molecular- or atomic-scale of various electrochemical phenomena, including electrodeposition and corrosion. The nanoelectrodes dealt with in footnote 20 of this chapter and in Figure 9.11, which is for single-molecule electrochemistry, are the examples of such electrodes. For the electrochemistry of films of nanoparticles and that of single nanopores in membranes, see Ref. [90].
