| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carbon dioxide | 304 (31) | 7.4 |
| Water | 647 (374) | 221 |
| Ammonia | 405 (132) | 114 |
| Sulfur dioxide | 431 (158) | 77.8 |
| Methanol | 513 (240) | 81.0 |
| Ethanol | 514 (241) | 61.5 |
| Acetonitrile | 546 (273) | 48.3 |
| Difluoromethane (HFC32) | 351 (78) | 5.8 |
| 1,1,1,2-Tetrafluoroethane (HFC134a) | 374 (101) | 4.1 |


1) In Figure 13.2, the value of autoprotolysis constant of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\left(\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{W}}\right)$ at 25 MPa is also shown as the function of temperature. In high-temperature water $\left(200-300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right), \mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{W}}$ is 12 or less. However, the $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{W}}$ suddenly increases at temperatures over critical point.
2) The Kamlet-Taft parameters ( $\alpha$ for hydrogen bond donor (HBD) acidity, $\beta$ for hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) basicity and $\pi^{*}$ for dipolarity/polarizability) or related parameters have been determined in some supercritical fluids. For $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ [5], $\alpha$ value at $318^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ varied with density, from 0 at above $\sim 0.4 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$ to 0.19 at $0.25 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$. The value
0.19 is a little less than that of acetonitrile (0.23). The $\beta$ value was at most of the order of hexane. The $\pi^{*}$ value varied from negative value at lower densities to zero at higher densities, showing that the dipolarity/ polarizability is also very weak. For scHFC 32 and 134a [6], the $\alpha, \beta$ and $\pi^{*}$ values show that they have moderate HBD acidity, HBA basicity and dipolarity/polarizability. For scW , only $\pi^{*}$ value has been reported. Though the water near critical point ( $360^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) had $\pi^{*}$ value near 0.5 , that for scW was near to zero or minus, showing the low polarity of scW .

Table 13.2 Comparison of properties of supercritical fluids and those of gases and liquids.
| State | Condition | Density, $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{cm}^{-3}$ | Viscosity, $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{cm}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ | Diffusion coefficient, $\mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gas | $1 \mathrm{~atm}, 25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ | $0.6-2 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1-3 \times 10^{-4}$ | $1-4 \times 10^{-1}$ |
| Liquid | $1 \mathrm{~atm}, 25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ | 0.6-2 | $0.2-3 \times 10^{-2}$ | $0.2-2 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| Supercritical | $T_{c}, P_{c}$ | 0.2-0.5 | $1-3 \times 10^{-4}$ | $0.5-4 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| fluid | $T_{\mathrm{c}}, 4 P_{\mathrm{c}}$ | 0.4-0.9 | $3-9 \times 10^{-4}$ | $0.5-4 \times 10^{-3}$ |


SCFs have been used for many years in a variety of ways. Some SCFs serve as 'green solvents' to replace organic solvents that are hazardous to human health and the environment. Especially, scW and $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ are important as such SCFs.

In the case of scW, as described above, nonpolar organic substances can dissolve in high concentrations owing to the low dielectric constant. This fact and the high critical temperature ( $T_{\mathrm{c}}=374.2^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) make possible the supercritical water oxidation that decomposes the toxic organic substances and hazardous wastes and makes them innocuous. If an oxidizing agent $\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right.$ or $\left.\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}\right)$ coexists, considerable parts of PCB and dioxins are decomposed to $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and HCl (Figure 13.3). ScW is also useful for chemical recycling of various waste plastics; the plastics are decomposed to raw materials and fuels. In these processes, subcritical water also works more or less in the same way. Supercritical and subcritical waters are useful to decompose biomass wastes and get resources from them: the hydrolysis in subcritical water gives useful substances that are contained in the biomasses, the hydrothermal reactions in subcritical and supercritical waters give fuel gas (hydrogen and methane), and the supercritical water oxidation gives $\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and heat that can be used as energy sources.

The $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ is widely applied because it is supercritical near room temperatures, does not flame, does not decompose substances and yet dissolves much organic substances. Examples of the applications of $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ are as follows:

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-352.jpg?height=492&width=714&top_left_y=1467&top_left_x=350)
Figure 13.2 Temperature dependence of relative permittivity ( $\varepsilon_{r}$ ) and autoprotolysis constant $\left(\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{W}}\right)$ of water at a pressure of 25 MPa (Tester, J.W. et al. (1993) ACS Symp. Ser., 518, 35).

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-353.jpg?height=898&width=739&top_left_y=198&top_left_x=214)
Figure 13.3 Decomposition of dioxins by scW plus oxidizing agents at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 30 \mathrm{MPa}$ and reaction time of 30 min [4d].

1. For separation or extraction $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ is used for the decaffeination of green coffee beans, the extraction of hops for beer production and the production of essential oils and pharmaceutical products from plants. It is also used to remove dioxins from soils and to separate and recover uranium and plutonium from nuclear fuels. $\mathrm{ScCO}_{2}$ is also an extractant of organic substances from ionic liquids [8].
2. For chemical reactions and syntheses $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ is used to produce various chemicals and polymers with high yield and high-energy efficiency and under benign conditions. Examples are the synthesis of polyacrylic acid in $\mathrm{ScCO}_{2}$ and the synthesis of carbonate using $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ as raw materials.
3. For manufacturing and processing of micro- and nanomaterials $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ is suitable for preparing micro- and nanoparticles and fine-porosity materials (e.g. silicaaerogel).
4. For cleaning $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ is used for dry cleaning, replacing perchloroethylene and other undesirable solvents. It is also used to clean semiconductors and important parts of precision tools.

## 5. For the supercritical fluid chromatography (SFC)

The critical points of alcohols are between $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and water and, thus, supercritical alcohols can decompose substances by the extent in between $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ and scW .

For example, PET bottles are decomposed by supercritical methanol to dimethyl terephthalate and ethylene glycol; by using ethylene glycol and terephthalic acid, obtained by further hydrolysis of dimethyl terephthalate, a resin is reproduced to get new PET bottles.

### 13.2.2 <br> Electrochemical Aspects of Supercritical Fluids

In 1981, Silvestri et al. [9] used supercritical HCl and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ to study the anodic dissolution of Fe and Ag. Since then, electrochemical studies in SCFs have been carried out to a considerable extent.

Bard and coworkers [10] carried out voltammetric works in supercritical and nearcritical water. High temperature water is very corrosive, attacks most metals and dissolves quartz and Pyrex glass, leading to equipment failure and solution contamination. So they got an improved electrochemical cell as follows [10d]. They replaced the alumina tube reactor by an oxidized titanium tube to produce a more rugged reactor. All metal fittings were replaced by titanium ones to decrease corrosion problems. The working electrodes were Ptdisk UMEs (radius: $14 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ ) fabricated by sealing Pt-Ir wires in a high PbO -containing glass. This glass had the advantage over borosilicate glass in showing lower conductivity at temperatures above $230^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, better corrosion resistance and a coefficient of thermal expansion well matched to Pt. They used this improved electrochemical cell to get well-defined steady-state cyclic voltammograms for the oxidation of iodide ion and hydroquinone in $0.2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaHSO}_{4}$ solutions over a temperature range $23-385^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. For both processes, diffusion coefficients, determined from the limiting currents, agreed well with those calculated from the Stokes-Einstein equation and viscosity values for water at the relevant temperatures and pressures (Figure 13.4). The facts that no special change was observed before and after the critical point show that no large change occurred in the size or shape of these species and that the changes in water for diffusion can simply be treated in terms of bulk viscosity changes. Electrochemical studies are useful to understand various phenomena (e.g. supercritical water oxidation) occurring in supercritical and near-critical water. Moreover, because near-critical water and scW can dissolve many nonpolar organic compounds, electrochemical syntheses in high-temperature water may become a promising procedure. Bard and coworkers also studied electrochemistry in nearcritical and supercritical ammonia [11], sulfur dioxide [12] and acetonitrile [13].
$\mathrm{ScCO}_{2}$ is important in that it is nontoxic, nonflammable, environmentally benign, commercially available in high quality and has easily attainable critical parameters. However, it is practically nonpolar ( $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \sim 1.6$ ), and polar solutes and electrolytes are difficult to dissolve in it. Abbott and Harper [14] tried voltammetric measurement of bis(tetradodecylammonium) nickel maleonitrile by dissolving a hydrophobic electrolyte, $\mathrm{Decyl}_{4} \mathrm{NBF}_{4}$, in $\mathrm{ScCO}_{2}$, but they did not get a well-defined wave. Voltammetric measurements in $\mathrm{ScCO}_{2}$ are usually carried out by adding some polar modifier (e.g. water, acetonitrile) to increase the polarity. Wightman and coworkers [15] studied, using UMEs, the voltammetric oxidation of ferrocene in $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ containing $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ as a modifier and $\mathrm{Hex}_{4} \mathrm{NPF}_{6}$ as a supporting electrolyte. When $\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right] \sim 55 \mathrm{mM}$,

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-355.jpg?height=900&width=808&top_left_y=198&top_left_x=221)
Figure 13.4 Diffusion coefficient of iodide and hydroquinone as a function of temperature in water [10d].

ferrocene gave a well-defined oxidation voltammogram, though its oxidation was impossible if $\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]<28 \mathrm{mM}$. When the concentration of $\mathrm{Hex}_{4} \mathrm{NPF}_{6}$ was increased, a phase separation occurred between the $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Hex}_{4} \mathrm{NPF}_{6}$, which complicated the electrode process. Li and Prentice [16] used a high-pressure, near-critical $\mathrm{CO}_{2}+ \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{EtOH}$ solution, with LiCl as electrolyte and a copper cathode, to synthesize methanol by direct electrochemical reduction of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.

$$
\mathrm{CO}_{2}+6 \mathrm{H}^{+}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

Methanol was produced at $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ with a $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ pressure of 68 bar. The best current efficiency of $40 \%$ is too low to be commercially feasible, but this synthesis uses an environment friendly dense-phase $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ for the solvent system, and the $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is also the raw material of synthesis. Conductive polymers have been synthesized in $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ by several workers [17]. The nanostructured nickel with particle sizes of sub-100 nm was electroplated in emulsion of $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ and electrolyte solution by Yoshida et al. [18]. Platinum/ruthenium nanoparticles were decorated on carbon nanotubes in $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$, and the PtRu/carbon nanotube nanocomposite was shown to have high activity for the electrooxidation of MeOH in $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{MeOH}$ [19]. The results proved the feasibility of processing bimetallic catalysts in $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ for fuel cell applications.

Supercritical difluoromethane ( HFC 32 ), trifluoromethane ( HFC 23 ) and 1,1,1,2tetrafluoroethane (HFC134a) are somewhat more polar than $\mathrm{scCO}_{2}$ and have readily attainable critical parameters. They are used as a voltammetric solvent alone or by mixing with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$. Olsen and Tallman [20] measured in supercritical chlorodifluoromethane and HFC23 the oxidation wave of ferrocene ( $\mathrm{Fc}^{0} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fc}^{+}$) and the reduction wave of cobaltocenium ion ( $\mathrm{Cc}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cc}^{0}$ ). The difference between their half-wave potentials was 1.28 V , in good agreement with 1.31 V obtained in various nonaqueous solutions (Section 8.2.2). Abbott and coworkers carried out a series of studies [21-25]: (1) By using HFC134a and HFC32 in liquid and supercritical states, oxidation waves of $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$and Xe were obtained [21]. The potential window had an unprecedented width of 9.4 V in $\mathrm{HFC} 134 \mathrm{a} / 0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$, which was liquid at $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 10 bar. (2) The effect of electrolyte concentration on the viscosity of HFC32 was measured by using a modified quartz resonator [22]. Ion solvation caused a significant structuring of the solvent and an appreciable increase in solution viscosity. Voltammetry was used to measure the changes of viscosity. (3) The dissociation constants of ferrocene carboxylic acid and its $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{~N}$ salt were studied in scHFC32, and the changes in the redox potentials of ferrecene derivatives were related to the dissociation constants of the electroactive species [23]. (4) The capacitance of a $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NBF}_{4} / \mathrm{scHFC} 32$ electrolyte at a Pt electrode was shown to depend strongly on fluid density [24]. The capacitance-potential plots at high pressures exhibited a double-layer response similar to that in the liquid state, but, when the pressure was lowered toward the critical value, the diffuse layer collapsed and a pure Helmholtz response was observed. (5) The reduction of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ in a mixed SCF $\left(\mathrm{CO}_{2} / \mathrm{HFC} 134 \mathrm{a}, x_{\mathrm{HFC} 134 \mathrm{a}}=0.3\right)$ was studied using Pt and Pb electrodes [25]. At $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 260 bar, the faradaic efficiencies (\%) of $(\mathrm{COOH})_{2}, \mathrm{CO}$ and HCOOH formations were $41.6,14.6$ and 0 , respectively, at Pt and $17.5,42.0$ and 0 , respectively, at Pb . The highly efficient $(\mathrm{COOH})_{2}$ formation at Pt , compared to $<5 \%$ in aprotic solutions, was interpreted in terms of the adsorption of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}{ }^{\bullet-}$ onto the Pt electrode (Section 12.5).

The use of SCFs in electrochemical technologies is still scarce but will become more popular, considering their wide applications in many fields. Electrochemical in situ monitoring of solutes in SCFs will be another type of applications.

## 13.3 <br> Ionic Liquids

Ionic liquid or room-temperature ionic liquids (RTILs) are the salts that are liquids near room temperatures, although often defined as the salts melting below $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Many of them are nonvolatile, nonflammable, less toxic, chemically and thermally stable and good solvents for both organic and inorganic materials. From electrochemical point of view, some of them have good electrical conductivity and wide potential windows. Hydrophobic ILs form interfaces with water (W) and are applicable to chemical separations, extractions and electrochemistry at the IL/W interfaces, just like the cases between two immiscible electrolyte solutions (ITIES).

The electrochemistry at the IL/W interfaces will be discussed in the next chapter. Some books [26] and review articles [27] are available on the electrochemical use of ILs.

ILs can be divided into two groups: chloroaluminate (or more generally haloaluminate) ILs and nonchloroaluminate ILs. Chloroaluminate ILs are the mixtures of $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ and organic chloride ( $\mathrm{R}^{+} \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$). $\mathrm{R}^{+}$include 1,3-dimethylimidazolium ( $[\mathrm{MMIm}]^{+}$), 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium ( $[\mathrm{EMIm}]^{+}$), 1-propyl-3-methylimidazolium ([PMIm] ${ }^{+}$), 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium ([BMIm] ${ }^{+}$), 1-propyl-2,3-dimethylimidazolium ( $[\mathrm{PMMIm}]^{+}$), $N$-butylpyridinium ( $[\mathrm{BP}]^{+}$), etc. (see Scheme 13.2 for the structure of skeletons). The reaction $2 \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{AlCl}_{4}{ }^{-}+\mathrm{AlCl}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{7}{ }^{-}$occurs between $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$. If we express the mole fraction of $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ by $N \left(=\left[\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}\right] /\left(\left[\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}\right]+[\mathrm{RCl}]\right)\right)$, the ILs of $N=0.5$ are neutral with an anion $\mathrm{AlCl}_{4}{ }^{-}$, the ILs of $N>0.5$ are acidic with anions $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{7}{ }^{-}$and $\mathrm{AlCl}_{4}{ }^{-}$and the ILs of $N<0.5$ are basic with anions $\mathrm{AlCl}_{4}{ }^{-}$and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$. Chloroaluminate ILs are usually hygroscopic.

Nonchloroaluminate ILs are the salt between a cation $\mathrm{R}^{+}$and an anion $\mathrm{A}^{-}$, where the examples of $\mathrm{R}^{+}$for aprotic ILs are $[\mathrm{MMIm}]^{+},[\mathrm{EMIm}]^{+},[\mathrm{PMIm}]^{+},[\mathrm{BMIm}]^{+}$, 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium ( $[\mathrm{HMIm}]^{+}$), $[\mathrm{BP}]^{+}$, 1-butyl-3-methylpyridinium ([BMP]*), 1,1-dimethylpyrrolidinium ([MMPyr] ${ }^{+}$), trimethylethylammonium ( $\left[\mathrm{Me}_{3} \mathrm{EtN}\right]^{+}$), trimethylpropylammonium ( $\left[\mathrm{Me}_{3} \mathrm{PrN}\right]^{+}$), tetraethylammonium ( $\left[\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{~N}\right]^{+}$), tetrabutylammonium ( $\left[\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{~N}\right]^{+}$), tetrabutylphosphonium ( $\left[\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{P}\right]^{+}$) and trimethylsulfonium ( $\left[\mathrm{Me}_{3} \mathrm{~S}\right]^{+}$), and those of $\mathrm{A}^{-}$are $\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right]^{-},\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]^{-},\left[\mathrm{CF}_{3} \mathrm{CO}_{2}\right]^{-}$, $\left[\mathrm{CF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]^{-}\left(\mathrm{TfO}^{-}\right),\left[\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]^{-},\left[\left(\mathrm{CF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{2}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~N}\right]^{-}\left(\mathrm{NTf}_{2}{ }^{-}\right)$and $\left[\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{HF})_{2.3}\right]^{-}$. Some are very hydrophobic and immiscible with water as discussed in the next chapter. There are protic ILs too, the cations $\mathrm{R}^{+}$of which being primary, secondary and tertiary ammonium cations, 1-alkylimidazolium cations, 1-alkyl-2-alkylimidazolium cations, etc. [28].

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-357.jpg?height=603&width=1101&top_left_y=1432&top_left_x=205)
Scheme 13.2 Skeleton of cations of ILs.

### 13.3.1 <br> Physical, Chemical and Electrochemical Properties of ILs

Dielectric constants [29] Static dielectric constants have been determined for some ILs. They are 15.2 for $[\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\mathrm{CF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}\right], 12.8$ for $[\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right], 11.7$ for $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right], 11.4$ for $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]$ and 8.9 for $[\mathrm{HMIm}]\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]$ compared to 20.56 for acetone, 12.9 for pyridine, 10.37 for 1,2-dichloroethane and 8.93 for dichloromethane.

Viscosity [30] Viscosity is a measure of resistance of fluid to flow. Viscosities of ILs $(\eta)$ at room temperature are from about 10 cP to over 500 cP . These values are much higher than 0.34 cP of $\mathrm{AN}, 0.89 \mathrm{cP}$ of W and 2.53 cP of PC , and are comparable to 16.1 cP of ethylene glycol and 934 cP of glycerol. Some of the data are shown in Table 13.3. The viscosities are much influenced by temperature. However, the large dispersions of some data in Table 13.3 mainly result from impurities; especially, $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$-ion and water that are easily contained in ILs much influence the viscosities. Chloride concentration between 1.5 and $6 \mathrm{wt} \%$ raised the viscosity between 30 and $600 \%$. Two wt\% of water reduced the viscosity of $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right]$ by more than $50 \%$. For nonchloroaluminate ILs of the same cation, the viscosity increases with the anion in the order $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}{ }^{-}<\mathrm{BF}_{4}{ }^{-}<\mathrm{CF}_{3} \mathrm{CO}_{2}{ }^{-}< \mathrm{CF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}{ }^{-}<\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{3}{ }^{-}<\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{5} \mathrm{CO}_{2}{ }^{-}<\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}_{2}{ }^{-}<\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}{ }^{-}<\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{~F}_{9} \mathrm{SO}_{3}{ }^{-}$. For ILs between substituted imidazolium cations and $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}{ }^{-}$, the viscosity increases in the order $[\mathrm{EMIm}]^{+}<[\mathrm{EEIm}]^{+}<[\mathrm{BEIm}]^{+}<[\mathrm{BMIm}]^{+}<[\mathrm{PMMIm}]^{+}$. For chloroaluminate $[\mathrm{EMIm}] \mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ at 303 K , the viscosity is nearly constant (14-18) for $[\mathrm{EMIm}] \mathrm{Cl}$ below $50 \mathrm{~mol} \%$, but it increases for $[\mathrm{EMIm}] \mathrm{Cl}$ above $50 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ and becomes over 190 cP at $67 \mathrm{~mol} \%$. This increase is explained to be due to the hydrogen bonding between $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$and the imidazolium ring. For chloroaluminate ILs with the same anion, the viscosity increases with the size of the cation but tends to decrease if the cation is highly asymmetrically substituted. In general, the addition of small amount of cosolvent (e.g. acetonitrile) decreases the viscosity of ILs significantly.

Conductivity [31] The conductivity depends on the number of available charge carriers and their mobility. Very high conductivities are expected for ILs because they are composed entirely of ions. But the actual conductivities of common ILs are comparable to the highest nonaqueous solvent/electrolyte systems ( $\sim 10 \mathrm{mS} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ ), and they are much smaller than those of concentrated aqueous electrolyte solutions. The smaller than expected conductivities of ILs can be attributed to ion pairing or ion aggregations, in addition to the large sizes of constituent ions and the high viscosities of ILs. Some of the conductivity $(\kappa)$ and molar conductivity $(\Lambda)$ data are also shown in Table 13.3. Here, the conductivity of ILs with F $(\mathrm{HF})_{2.3}{ }^{-}$-anion is exceptionally high and is $\sim 100 \mathrm{mS} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}$. The conductivity of ILs is influenced significantly by the impurities, especially by $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$-ions and water. Small amount of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$-ions tends to decrease the conductivity while small amount of water increases it. The relation between the molar conductivity and the

viscosity nearly follows the Walden's rule, i.e. $\Lambda \eta \approx$ constant. Thus, for ILs of high viscosity, the molar conductivity is small. With ILs of the same anions, the value of $\Lambda \eta$ tends to decrease with the increase of cationic size while with ILs of the same cation, no clear relations can be found between the anionic size.

Acid-base properties and polarity Acceptor numbers (ANs) have been determined for some aprotic ILs, by using a linear relation between ANs of various molecular solvents and Raman spectrum of diphenylcyclopropenone [32]. ANs for ILs are 27.7 for $\left[\mathrm{BMIm}^{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right], 26.9$ for $\left[\mathrm{BMIm}^{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right]$ and $[\mathrm{BMIm}] \mathrm{Cl}, 25.2$ for $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right], 27.5$ for $[\mathrm{BMMIm}]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right], 22.2$ for $\left[\mathrm{PrMe}_{3} \mathrm{~N}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right], 23.9$ for $\left[\mathrm{Et}_{2} \mathrm{Me}\right. (\mathrm{MeOEt}) \mathrm{N}]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right]$ and 20.3 for $\left[\mathrm{Et}_{2} \mathrm{Me}(\mathrm{MeOEt}) \mathrm{N}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$. These values compare with 27.1 for $t-\mathrm{BuOH}, 22.2$ for $t-\mathrm{PeOH}$ and 20.5 for nitromethane, and these are somewhat larger than those for conventional aprotic solvents ( $A N \sim 20$ or smaller). The Kamlet-Taft parameters ( $\alpha, \beta$ and $\pi^{*}$ ) for nonchloroaluminate aprotic ILs have also been determined, as shown in Table 13.4 [33]. Here, $\alpha$ is a measure of hydrogen bond acidity, $\beta$ a measure of hydrogen bond basicity and $\pi^{*}$ a measure of dipolarity/polarizability. The values in Table 13.4 were obtained from the UV/vis spectra of a single set of dyes (Reichardt's dye, $N, N$-diethyl-4nitroaniline and 4-nitroaniline) ${ }^{3)}$ though they are usually obtained by averaging the values for several sets of dyes. For aprotic ILs, the $\alpha$ values are largely determined by the nature of the cation though there is also a smaller anion effect. The $\alpha$ values for [BMIm] and [HMIm] ILs are close to the value of $t$-BuOH and a little higher than those for ILs of other cations. The [BMMIm] IL has lowest $\alpha$ values, reflecting the loss of the proton on the 2 -position of the ring. Usually, the hydrogen bond acidity of ILs ranges from aprotic to a little higher than aprotic. The $\beta$ values of ILs are sensitive to anions, but generally the values are smaller than the value of acetone and close to or even smaller than that of acetonitrile, which is a very weak base. All of the $\pi^{*}$ values for ILs are higher than those for nonaqueous molecular solvents but lower than that for water, showing that the polarities of ILs are moderately high. About the Kamlet-Taft parameters of ILs, the temperature effects on them [33e] and the values in IL/organic solvent mixtures [33d] have been studied. Kamlet-Taft parameters have also been determined for the protic ILs [28] and some of them are shown in Table 13.4. Except for [ $\mathrm{H}^{*}$ MIm][TfO] ( $\mathrm{H}^{*}$ : hydrogen), $\alpha$ values for protic ILs are higher than those for aprotic ILs. The autoprotolysis constant $\left(K_{\mathrm{SH}}\right)$ of $\left[\mathrm{EtH}_{3} \mathrm{~N}\right]\left[\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right]$, a protic IL, has been obtained using a rapidly responding pH-ISFET to be $10^{-10.0} \mathrm{~mol}^{2} \mathrm{dm}^{-6}$, indicating that the neutral $\left[\mathrm{EtH}_{3} \mathrm{~N}\right]\left[\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right]$ involves $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{EtNH}_{2}$ molecules as much as $1.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{M}$ or pH is 5.0 at $298^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ [34a]. A method utilizing the rapidly responding pH -ISFET has been developed for the characterization of ILs and patented [34b]. The rapidity of pH ISFET response in molecular solvents has been dealt with in Chapter 6.
3) $E_{\mathrm{T}}(30)$ (in kcal mol ${ }^{-1}$ ) is obtained by $25891 / \lambda_{\text {max }}(\mathrm{nm})$, where $\lambda_{\text {max }}$ is the wavelength of the maximum of the charge transfer absorption band of the Reichardt's dye. $\pi^{*}$ is obtained by measuring the wavelength of maximum absorbance, $v_{\text {max }}$ in kK (kilokeyser, $10^{-3} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ ), of the dye $N, N$-diethyl-4-nitroaniline from the relation $\pi^{*}=\left(v_{\text {max }}-v_{0}\right) / s$, where $v_{0} (27.52 \mathrm{kK})$ is the regression value for a reference solvent system and $s(-3.182)$ is the
susceptibility of intensity of spectral absorption due to changing solvent dipolarity/polarizability. $\alpha$ is then obtained by $\alpha=\left\{E_{\mathrm{T}}(30)\right. \left.-14.6\left(\pi^{*}-0.23\right)-30.31\right\} / 16.5 . \beta$ is obtained by measuring the relative difference of solvatochromism between 4-nitroaniline (1) and $N, N$-diethyl-4-nitroaniline (2) by $\beta=\{1.035 v \left.(2)_{\text {max }}-v(1)_{\text {max }}+2.64\right\} / 2.8$, where $v(2)_{\text {max }}$ and $v(1)_{\text {max }}$ are the wavelengths of maximum absorbance.

${ }^{b}$ By definition.
Solvation of ions Recent study by Raman spectroscopy in [BMIm][NTf ${ }_{2}$ ] [35] showed that the numbers of $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}{ }^{-}$bound to $\mathrm{Li}^{+}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{K}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$are 1.95, 2.88, 3.2 and 3.9, respectively. Because $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}{ }^{-}$is a bidentate ligand, this means that the coordination numbers of metal ions are $4,6,6$ and 8 , respectively, for
$\mathrm{Li}^{+}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{K}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$. It was also shown that the ion-ion interaction between the alkali metal ions and $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}{ }^{-}$is mainly electrostatic and that $c i s-\mathrm{NTf}_{2}{ }^{-}$is favored in the first solvation sphere of the small $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ion, although this tendency diminishes with an increase in the radii of metal ions. Review articles are available on structural studies of ILs by EXAFS [36a] and of alkylimidazoliumbased ILs with fluoroanions [36b].

Electrochemical potential windows [37] Potential windows have been measured using GC, Pt, tungsten (W), etc. as working electrodes. The reference electrodes used are $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{7}{ }^{-}, \mathrm{AlCl}_{4}{ }^{-} \mid \mathrm{Al}$ (wire), $\mathrm{I}_{3}{ }^{-}, \mathrm{I}^{-} \mid \mathrm{Pt}$ (wire) and $\mathrm{AgBF}_{4}$ (satd) or $\mathrm{AgTaF}_{6}$ (satd) $\mid \mathrm{Ag}$ (wire), the reactions of which are $4 \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{7}^{-}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Al}+ 7 \mathrm{AlCl}_{4}^{-}, \quad \mathrm{I}_{3}^{-}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows 3 \mathrm{I}^{-}$and $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Ag}$, respectively. A quasireference electrode (Ptor Agwire) and $\mathrm{Li}^{+} \mid$Li reference electrode are also frequently used. The potentials of some reference electrodes against the $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ system (solvent-independent reference) are shown in Table 13.5.
Some of the potential windows for chloroaluminate ILs are shown in Figure 13.5 using the potential of $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ system as reference. In the acidic chloroaluminate ILs ( $N>0.5$ ), the cathodic limit is the reduction of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{7}{ }^{-}$and the anodic limit is the oxidation of $\mathrm{AlCl}_{4}{ }^{-}$. In the basic chloroaluminate ILs ( $N<0.5$ ), the cathodic limit is the reduction of the cation $\mathrm{R}^{+}$and the anodic limit is the oxidation of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$. In neutral chloroaluminate ILs ( $N=0.5$ ), the cathodic limit is the reduction of $\mathrm{R}^{+}$and the anodic limit is the oxidation of $\mathrm{AlCl}_{4}{ }^{-}$, and the width is about 4.4 V for $[\mathrm{EMIm}] \mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ and 3.6 V for [BP] $\mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$.
The potential windows for nonchloroaluminate ILs are shown in Figure 13.6 against $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ reference system. Generally, the cathodic limit is the reduction of the cation while the anodic limit is the oxidation of the anion. However, when the anion is easily reduced, the cathodic limit may be the reduction of the anion. Contrarily, when the cation is easily oxidized, the anodic limit may be the oxidation of the cation. The former case occurs with [ $\mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{~N}$ ][TSAC] (TSAC $=$ 2,2,2-trifluoro- $N$-(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)acetamide) and the latter case with EMIm and $p$-dimethylamino- $N$-butylpyridinium (DMABP) systems. The widest potential windows are obtained with ILs of aliphatic quaternary ammonium ions and fluoroanions: the widths of nearly 6.0 V are comparable

### 13.3.2

## Voltammetry in ILs

Voltammetric measurements in room-temperature ionic liquids can be performed in similar ways as those in nonaqueous solvents, though reference electrodes should be selected appropriately and, in the case of chloroaluminate ILs, the effect of moisture should be avoided.

1. Reduction of dissolved oxygen Dissolved oxygen in ILs is reduced to superoxide $\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}{ }^{\bullet-}\right)$, just as in aprotic solvents: $\mathrm{O}_{2}{ }^{\bullet-}$ generated in [ Im ]Cl- $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ (GC electrode) [38a] is not stable but that in $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]$ (GC electrode) [38b] is stable enough to give a cyclic voltammogram for reverse reaction $\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}{ }^{\bullet-} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}+\right. \mathrm{e}^{-}$). Buzzeo et al. [38c] used a microdisk gold electrode of about $10 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ in diameter and studied the reduction of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ in $[\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{HeMe}_{3} \mathrm{~N}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ by cyclic voltammetry. For $[\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{HeMe}_{3} \mathrm{~N}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ with the viscosities of 32.1 and 220 cP , respectively, the solubilities of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ were 3.9 and 11.6 mM and the diffusion coefficients of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ were $8.3 \times 10^{-6}$ and $1.5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The diffusion coefficients of $\mathrm{O}_{2}{ }^{\bullet-}$, determined by simulation using these values, were $2.7 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ in $[\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ and $4.7 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ in $\left[\mathrm{HeMe}_{3} \mathrm{~N}\right] \left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$. The fact that the diffusion coefficients for both $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}{ }^{\bullet-}$ are much different between the two ILs can be attributed to the big difference in viscosity. Superoxide ion, $\mathrm{O}_{2}{ }^{\bullet-}$, generated from IL, e.g. $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]$, is a safe and mild oxidizing agent and has several potential applications in green chemistry [40]. For example, it can destroy hexachlorobenzene (HCB), which have a serious environmental problem.
2. Voltammetry of metallocene derivatives In nonaqueous molecular solvents, ferrocenium ion/ferrocene ( $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ ) system is used as a reference for electrode potentials because its potential is nearly solvent independent. Such a use is made also in ILs. The oxidation of ferrocene has been studied voltammetrically by various investigators [41]. In $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]$ at $22^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, ferrocene was found to be poorly soluble and need solid-state voltammetric experiment, involving mechan-
4) The structure at the IL-electrified metal interface has been studied for imidazoliumbased ILs by sum frequency generation (SFG), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and vibrational Stark shift [39]. The vibrational Stark shift and EIS results showed that ions organize in a Helmholtz-like layer at the interface, where the potential drop occurs over the range of $3-5 \AA$ from the metal surface into the liquid. Further, the SFG results im-
plied that the 'double-layer' structure is potential dependent; at potentials positive of the potential of zero charge (PZC), anions are adsorbed to the surface and the imidazolium ring is repelled to orient more along the surface normal, compared to the potentials negative of the PZC, at which the cation is oriented more parallel to the surface plane and the anions are repelled from the surface.
ical attachment of finely grinded solid to the electrode surface [41a]. The reaction can be expressed as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left.\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{Cp})_{2}\right]_{(\text {solid })}+[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]_{(\text {solvent })} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{Cp})_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]_{(\text {solid })} \\
& \quad+\mathrm{e}^{-}+\left[\mathrm{BMIm}_{(\text {solvent })}^{+}\right.
\end{aligned}
$$

From the solid-state voltammogram, the formal potential for Fc at the Au electrode was determined to be +1.332 V versus $\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{Cp})_{2}\right]^{+/ 0}$, the value that compares with +1.335 V versus $\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{Cp})_{2}\right]^{+/ 0}$ in AN. In this IL, voltammetric studies have also been carried out about ferrocene derivatives and cobaltocenium ion $\left(\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{Cp})_{2}\right]^{+}\right) ;\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{Cp})_{2}\right]^{+}$is reduced at GC and Au electrodes in two reversible steps, $\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{Cp})_{2}\right]^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{Cp})_{2}\right]^{0}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{Cp})_{2}\right]^{0}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{Cp})_{2}\right]^{-}$. The potential difference between the two steps was $\sim 800 \mathrm{mV}$. Recently, the behaviors of Fc and $\mathrm{Cc}^{+}$were studied by double potential step chronoamperometry in several nonchloroaluminate ILs, and the diffusion coefficients of $\mathrm{Fc}, \mathrm{Fc}^{+}, \mathrm{Cc}$ and $\mathrm{Cc}^{+}$were obtained [41b]. Some of the data for Fc and $\mathrm{Fc}^{+}$are shown in Table 13.6. The solubility of Fc in $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]$ was reported to be poor in Ref. [41a], but the solubility in this study ( 38.2 mM ) was found to be not poor but moderate. Care was taken concerning the water content of ILs in [41a], but the product as received was used in [41b]. The discrepancy of the solubility may have come from the care taken of the IL purity. The rate constants for the oxidation of a series of ferrocene derivatives in $[\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ have been determined using the high-speed channel electrode method at a Pt electrode and at $23^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ [41c]. For ferrocene, the diffusion coefficient is $D=3.35 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and the rate constant is $k_{\mathrm{s}}=0.21 \pm 0.06 \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$. In $\mathrm{AN}, k_{\mathrm{s}}=0.99 \pm 0.22 \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and $D$ was about two orders of magnitude larger. However, a paper reporting the concentration dependence of the diffusion coefficient of ferrocene in $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right]$ has been published [40d]: $D_{\mathrm{Fc}}\left(\times 10^{-7}\right. \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ ) determined by cyclic voltammetry is 1.3 at $4.3 \mathrm{mM}, 1.8$ at $8.6 \mathrm{mM}, 2.1$ at $12 \mathrm{mM}, 3.3$ at $17 \mathrm{mM}, 4.5$ at $22 \mathrm{mM}, 5.7$ at 26 mM and 6.8 at 32 mM though the exact reason for this is not clarified yet.

| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $[\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ | 34 | 5.34 | 3.90 | 1.37 | 62.3 |
| $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ | 52 | 3.77 | 2.65 | 1.43 | 77.4 |

[^28]3. Reduction of metal ions The reductions of alkali metal ions have been studied at mercury electrodes [42]. Boxall and Osteryoung [42a] determined formal potentials and diffusion coefficients of lithium, sodium and potassium ions in [BMIm] $\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]$, using a hanging mercury drop electrode. The diffusion coefficients measured by chronoamperometry were $1.9 \times 10^{-8}, 1.4 \times 10^{-8}$ and $4.5 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ for $\mathrm{Li}^{+}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{K}^{+}$, respectively. The formal potentials (versus $\mathrm{Fc} / \mathrm{Fc}^{+}$) of -2.96 V for $\mathrm{Na}^{+} / \mathrm{Na}$ and -3.35 V for $\mathrm{K}^{+} / \mathrm{K}$ were obtained from the data by normal pulse voltammetry (NPV), but that of -2.45 V for $\mathrm{Li}^{+} / \mathrm{Li}$ from the chronopotentiometric data because current maximum was observed in NPV. Since $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]$ is reduced at about -2.5 V versus $\mathrm{Fc} / \mathrm{Fc}^{+}$, only $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$could be reduced before IL reduction at nonmercury electrodes. The electrochemistry of cesium was investigated in $\left[\mathrm{Bu}_{3} \mathrm{MeN}_{]}\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]\right.$ by using cyclic staircase voltammetry, rotating disk voltammetry and chronoamperometry. The reduction of $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$at mercury was quasireversible with $k_{\mathrm{s}}=9.8 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}, \alpha=0.36$ and the diffusion coefficient $D=1.04 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Bulk deposition/stripping experiments conducted at a rotating mercury film electrode gave an average recovery of $97 \%$ of the electrodeposited Cs.

The voltammetry and kinetics of the $\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}$ system (commonly used as a reference electrode in both molecular solvents and ILs) were studied in [BMPyr] $\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$, using a $10 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ diameter Pt electrode [43]. For $\mathrm{AgOTf}, \mathrm{AgNTf}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$, voltammogram characteristics for 'deposition/stripping' process were obtained. The diffusion coefficients of $\mathrm{AgOTf}, \mathrm{AgNTf}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$, obtained by potential step chronoamperometry, were $1.05,1.17$ and $5.00 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, respectively. The standard electrochemical rate constants $\left(k_{\mathrm{s}}\right)$ for the three were $2.0,1.5$ and $0.19 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, respectively, which are too small for ideal reference electrode, although these $\mathrm{Ag}^{+} / \mathrm{Ag}$ electrodes can serve as a reference electrode if the areas of the Ag electrodes are large enough, using a potentiostat through which only small current flows.

Thorium compound, $\left[\mathrm{Th}\left(\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right)\left(\mathrm{HNTf}_{2}\right)\right] \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, where both $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}{ }^{-}$and $\mathrm{HNTf}_{2}$ are coordinated to the metal center, has been studied voltammetrically in [Bu$\left.\mathrm{Me}_{3} \mathrm{~N}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$, and the result showed that $\mathrm{Th}(\mathrm{IV})$ is reduced to $\mathrm{Th}(0)$ in a single step [44]. Cyclic voltammograms showed that an insoluble product is formed at the electrode surface, which was attributed to the formation of $\mathrm{ThO}_{2}$ by reaction with water. The formal potential of the reduction of $\mathrm{Th}(\mathrm{IV})$ to $\mathrm{Th}(0)$ was -2.20 V versus $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$, which showed that the $\mathrm{Th}(\mathrm{IV})$ reduction in $\left[\mathrm{BuMe}_{3} \mathrm{~N}\right] \left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ is easier than that in $\mathrm{LiCl}-\mathrm{KCl}$ eutectic ( $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), water and nonaqueous solvents.
4. Reduction of organic substances Many organic substances in ILs are reduced to anion radicals by $1 \mathrm{e}^{-}$processes. A typical example is the reduction of benzaldehyde in [BMPyr][NTf ${ }_{2}$ ] [45]: at a $10 \mu \mathrm{~m} \phi$ Pt microdisk electrode and at $1000 \mathrm{~V} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, two reversible CV waves are obtained, the first of which at -1.73 V versus $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ is $1 \mathrm{e}^{-}$reduction to anion radical and the second at -2.33 V versus $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ is another $1 \mathrm{e}^{-}$reduction to dianion. Two waves are also observed in $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$; however, they are quasireversible and the first wave shifts to -0.75 V versus
$\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ and the second to -1.85 V versus $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$. This positive shift in potential is because of the effect of imidazolium cation: $\pi-\pi$ interactions or hydrogen bonds of imidazolium cation to the anion radical and dianion stabilize them.

In the reduction of nitrobenzene in $[\mathrm{BMMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$, a $1 \mathrm{e}^{-}$-reversible wave appears at -1.2 V (versus Ag quasireference electrode) and a $2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$-irreversible wave at -1.85 V (versus Ag ) [46]. The diffusion coefficient is $1.95 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. A series of aromatic nitro compounds (4-nitrotoluene, 2-nitro- $m$-xylene, 2-nitromesitylene, nitropentamethylbenzene, 2,4,6-tritertbutylnitrobenzene) also undergo $1 \mathrm{e}^{-}$reduction at GC and Au electrodes in $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{BuEt}_{3} \mathrm{~N}\right] \left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$, although the rates of electron transfers are slower than that in AN [47]. It is noted that the formal potentials of these compounds in $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$, measured versus $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$ reference, are usually more positive by $0.15-0.2 \mathrm{~V}$ than those in $\left[\mathrm{BuEt}_{3} \mathrm{~N}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$. This is also the effect of the acidity of the imidazolium cation. 1,2- and 1,4-Dinitrobenzene exhibit two $1 \mathrm{e}^{-}$-waves in AN but a single $2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$wave in $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right]$. This phenomenon was originally interpreted to be caused by a strong ion pairing between the imidazolium cation and the dinitrobenzene dianion [48], but now the stabilization of the dianion is considered to be due to the $\pi-\pi$ interaction or hydrogen bonding of imidazolium cation to the dianion.
5. Oxidation of organic substances In ILs, just as in molecular solvents of weak acidity (e.g. AN), typical oxidation of organic compounds proceeds in two $1 e^{-}$-steps, the first being the formation of cation radical and the second the formation of dication. This occurs, for example, with $N, N, N^{\prime}, N^{\prime}$-tetraalkyl-para-phenylenediamines (TAPD; alkyl(A) = butyl(B) or methyl(M)) in [ $\mathrm{C}_{n} \mathrm{MIm}$ ] [NTf ${ }_{2}$ ] ( $n=2,4,8$ and 10 ) and $\left[\mathrm{HexMe}_{3} \mathrm{~N}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ [49].

$$
\mathrm{TAPD} \rightarrow \mathrm{TAPD}^{\bullet+}+\mathrm{e}^{-}, \quad \mathrm{TAPD}^{\bullet+} \rightarrow \mathrm{TAPD}^{2+}+\mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

Voltammograms, obtained with an Au microdisk electrode ( $12.5 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ in diameter) and at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ gave the first wave at around +0.1 V and the second at around +0.7 V (versus Ag wire). The diffusion coefficients, $10^{7} D_{\text {TMPD }}$ and $10^{7} D_{\text {TBPD }} \left(\mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\right)$, were 2.62 and 1.23 in $[\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right](\eta=32.1 \mathrm{cP}), 1.87$ and 0.80 in $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right](\eta=57.6 \mathrm{cP}), 1.12$ and 0.40 in $\left[\mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{MIM}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right](\eta=119.3 \mathrm{cP}), 1.13$ and 0.52 in $\left[\mathrm{C}_{10} \mathrm{MIm}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right](\eta=152.8 \mathrm{cP})$ and 0.70 and 0.24 in $\left[\mathrm{HexMe}_{3} \mathrm{~N}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ ( $\eta=220.0 \mathrm{cP}$ ), compared to 218 and 127 in AN- $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NClO}_{4}$.

The radical cation of anthracene in $[\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right],[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ and $[\mathrm{BMIm}] \left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]$ was unstable and an irreversible oxidation wave was observed at a scan rate of $0.1-100 \mathrm{~V} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$; because the water content in the ILs was of the order of 1500 ppm , a reaction of water with the radical cation occurred and gave a $2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$-oxidation wave. A reversible cyclic voltammogram was obtained at a scan rate above $3000-5000 \mathrm{~V} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. From the reversible behavior at $E^{0}$ of about 0.9 V versus $\mathrm{Fc}^{+} / \mathrm{Fc}$, the values of $10^{7} D$ ( $\mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ ) and $k_{\mathrm{s}}\left(\mathrm{cm} \mathrm{s}^{-1}\right)$ were obtained to be 2.8 and $0.1-0.2$ in [BMIm][ $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}$ ], 5.3 and $0.6-0.7$ in $\left[\mathrm{EMIm}^{-1}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ and 5.6 and $0.2-0.3$ in $\left[\mathrm{BMIm}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right]$, compared to 250 and $3-5 \mathrm{in}\left(\mathrm{AN} / \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NPF}_{6}\right)[50]$.

### 13.3.3 <br> Applications of ILs in Electrochemical Technologies

1. Electrodeposition of metals and semiconductors Ref. [26c] has the title 'Electrodeposition from Ionic Liquids' and Chapters 4-6 deal with electrodeposition of metals, alloys and semiconductors, respectively [51]. Some other books [52] and review articles [53] also deal with this topic.

The electrodepositions of alkali and alkaline earth metals have been studied in neutral chloroaluminate ILs, to use the ILs in rechargeable batteries. However, the reductions of these metals occurred at more negative potential than the decomposition of imidazolium cations. The depositions of Na and Li metals and $\mathrm{Na}-\mathrm{Li}$ alloys (dendrite free) were possible by using chloroaluminate ILs of quaternary ammonium cations in the presence of such additives as HCl and $\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$ because these extended the cathodic limit and produced passivation films on the deposited alkali metals [54]. Electrodepositions of such metals as $\mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Al}, \mathrm{Bi}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Cr}, \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Fe}$, $\mathrm{Ga}, \mathrm{Hg}, \mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Pb}, \mathrm{Pd}, \mathrm{Pt}, \mathrm{Sb}, \mathrm{Sn}$ and Zn and such alloys as $\mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Cr}$, $\mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Nb}, \mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Pt}$ and $\mathrm{Al}-\mathrm{Ti}$ have been carried out from acidic chloroaluminate ILs, while electrodepositions of such metals as $\mathrm{Au}, \mathrm{Cd}, \mathrm{Hg}, \mathrm{In}, \mathrm{Pd}$, $\mathrm{Sb}, \mathrm{Sn}, \mathrm{Te}, \mathrm{Tl}$ and Zn have been carried out from basic or neutral chloroaluminate ILs. However, the chloroaluminate ILs are very reactive with moistures, and this lessens the practical importance of the electrodeposition from these ILs.

The electrodepositions from nonchloroaluminate ILs can be divided in two groups, one from chlorozincate and the other from the usual nonchloroaluminate ILs. Chlorozincate IL is a mixture of $[\mathrm{EMIm}] \mathrm{Cl}$ and $\mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}[53 \mathrm{~b}]$. It is basic if the concentration of $\mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}$ is less than $33.3 \mathrm{~mol} \%$, while it is acidic if the concentration of $\mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}$ is more than $33.3 \mathrm{~mol} \%$. The predominant ionic species in basic ILs are $[\mathrm{EMIm}]^{+}, \mathrm{ZnCl}_{4}{ }^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$, but those in acidic ILs are $[\mathrm{EMIm}]^{+}$, $\mathrm{ZnCl}_{4}{ }^{2-}, \mathrm{ZnCl}_{3}{ }^{-}, \mathrm{Zn}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{5}{ }^{-}$and $\mathrm{Zn}_{3} \mathrm{Cl}_{7}{ }^{-}$. Metallic Zn is deposited only from acidic ILs. The metals and alloys deposited from the chlorozincate ILs are $\mathrm{Cd}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Fe}$, Te; Zn-Cd, Zn-Co, Zn-Co-Dy, Zn-Cu, Zn-Fe, Zn-Ni and Zn-Te(semiconductor). As the examples of depositions from the usual nonchloroaluminate ILs are $\mathrm{Ge}($ semiconductor $)$ from $\left[\mathrm{BMIm}^{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{PF}_{6}\right], \mathrm{Cd}, \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Sb}$ and $\mathrm{In}-\mathrm{Sb}$ from $[\mathrm{EMIm}] \mathrm{Cl}- [\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right]$ and $\mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Li}, \mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Pd}[55], \mathrm{Si}$ (semiconductor) and Zn from $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}$ salts. Lithium can be electrodeposited from $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}$ salts of aliphatic and alicyclic ammonium cations. Magnesium can virtually be electrodeposited from 1: 2 mixture of Grignard reagent ( 2 M phenyl-magnesium chloride in THF) and 1-butyl-1-methylpyrrolidium $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\left([\mathrm{BMPyr}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]\right) \mathrm{IL}$ at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}[56]$.

Here, it is interesting to note that nanomaterials of particle sizes or film thicknesses of 1-100 nm can easily be electrodeposited for various metals, alloys and semiconductors from chloroaluminate and nonchloroaluminate ILs though the electrodeposition of such materials is difficult from aqueous solutions. For example, nanocrystalline Cu and Al can be electrodeposited from green [BMPyr] $[\mathrm{TfO}]$ and $[\mathrm{BMPyr}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$, respectively, on conventional solid electrodes [57]. Generally, the obtained Cu and Al deposits are shiny, dense and adherent with
very fine crystallites of average sizes of $30-40 \mathrm{~nm}$. The [BMPyr] ${ }^{+}$cation might act as a grain refiner, leading to nanosized deposits. For the reviews of electrodeposition of nanoscale materials from ILs, see Ref. [58].

## 2. Electrochemical organic synthesis

(a) Electrochemical polymerization [59] Electrochemical synthesis of electrically conductive polymers such as polyarene, polypyrrole, polythiophene and polyaniline has been performed in chloroaluminate ILs. However, the polymer films are decomposed rapidly by the corrosive products, e.g. HCl , generated by the hydrolysis of these moisture-sensitive ILs. Electropolymerization has also been carried out in nonchloroaluminate ILs. For example, Fuchigami et al. employed [EMIm][OTf] ( $\eta=42.7 \mathrm{cP}$ ) for electropolymerization of pyrrole. The polymerization proceeded much faster in this IL than in the conventional media such as aqueous and acetonitrile solutions. In the viscous IL, since the diffusion is slow, radical-radical coupling, further oxidation of oligomers and polymer deposition favorably occur because the reaction products are accumulated in the vicinity of electrode surface and the polymerization rate is increased. Electropolymerization of thiophene is also accelerated in this IL, although that of aniline is decelerated. The polypyrrole film formed in this IL was very smooth and no grains were observed. Moreover, the polypyrrole and polythiophene films prepared in this IL showed much higher electrochemical capacities and electroconductivities. This can be attributed to the extremely high concentration of dopant anions, which results in a much higher doping level. The polymers thus prepared are suitable for high-performance electrochemical capacitors and other electrochemical devices.

## (b) Other examples of electrochemical organic synthesis

(i) Electrosynthesis of organic carbonates from $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and alcohols has been established in $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$-saturated $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right]$, followed by the addition of an alkylating agent [60].

$$
\mathrm{ROH}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \xrightarrow{+\mathrm{e}^{-},+R^{\prime} \mathrm{I}} \mathrm{ROCO}_{2} R^{\prime}
$$

The synthesis was carried out under mild ( 1 atm CO 2 and $55^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) and safe conditions, the use of volatile and toxic solvents and catalysts being avoided. The IL used for the reaction was recyclable. The primary and secondary alcohols were converted in good yields, whereas tertiary alcohol and phenol were not reactive.
(ii) The preparative electrocatalytic homocoupling of PhBr and $\mathrm{PhCH}_{2} \mathrm{Br}$, yielding in biphenyl and 1,2-diphenylethane, respectively, was performed using $\mathrm{NiCl}_{2}$ (bipy) complex in reusable media, $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right][61]$.

$$
2 \mathrm{Ph}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2}\right)_{x} \mathrm{Br} \xrightarrow{+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}, \quad \text { cat. } \mathrm{NiCl}_{2} \text { (bpy) }} \mathrm{Ph}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2}\right)_{x} \mathrm{Ph}
$$

(iii) Partial fluorination has been carried out conveniently in ILs [62]. Momota used liquid fluoride salts, $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NF} \cdot x \mathrm{HF}(\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{Me}$, Et and $n-\mathrm{Pr}, x>3.5)$, as

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-370.jpg?height=224&width=779&top_left_y=196&top_left_x=345)
Scheme 13.3

nonviscous, highly conductive and anodically stable ILs and used for anodic partial fluorination of arenes such as benzene, mono-, di- and trifluorobenzene, chlorobenzene, bromobenzene, toluene and quinolines (see Scheme 13.3). For various examples of partial fluorination, see Fujigami's review [63].
3. Use in electrochemical devices ILs have been used to various electrochemical devices, e.g. double-layer capacitors, rechargeable lithium batteries and electrochromic windows, mainly because of their safe nonvolatile and nonflammable properties.
(a) Double-layer capacitors Desirable properties for the electrolytes of double-layer capacitors are high double-layer capacitance, electrical conductivity and decomposition voltage, wide operational temperature range and good safety. For commercial double-layer capacitors using activated carbon electrodes, nonaqueous media such as $0.5-1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Et}_{4} \mathrm{NBF}_{4}-\mathrm{PC}$ or aqueous solutions such as $3.7-4.5 \mathrm{M}(30-35 \mathrm{wt} \%) \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ have been used. Nonaqueous solvents have advantages of high decomposition voltage, wide operational temperature range and noncorrosive property but suffer from disadvantages of low electric conductivity, air-tight packaging to avoid moisture, high environmental impact and high cost. Ue [64] used such ILs as $\left[\mathrm{EMIm}^{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right],\left[\mathrm{EMIm}^{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{TaF}_{6}\right],[\mathrm{EMIm}] [\mathrm{TfO}],[\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right],[\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~N}\right]$ and $[\mathrm{EMImM}]\left[\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{HF})_{2.3}\right]$ and compared their performances with those of nonaqueous $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Et}_{3} \mathrm{MeNBF}_{4} / \mathrm{PC}$ and aqueous $4.5 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} / \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. Some ILs could perform comparably to $\mathrm{Et}_{3}$. $\mathrm{MeNBF}_{4} / \mathrm{PC}$ system but only at higher temperatures; the performance at temperatures below $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ was much inferior. Ue [64] used new IL, [EMIm] $\left[\mathrm{BF}_{3} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{5}\right]\left(\mathrm{mp}=-1^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$, and found that the electric conductivity was comparable to $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Et}_{3} \mathrm{MeNBF}_{4} / \mathrm{PC}$ down to $-35^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and the capacitor with this IL worked well at low temperatures. Searching for new ILs that show excellent low-temperature characteristics will be the best way to succeed in using ILs in the double-layer capacitors. There are two other ways to get excellent lowtemperature characteristics. One is to use polymer ILs, which immobilizes ILs by polymers to overcome the disadvantage of liquid electrolytes. The other is, as often carried out, to mix organic solvents and ILs.
(b) Rechargeable lithium batteries [65] ILs for rechargeable lithium batteries must be liquid in a wide temperature range, wide in potential window, high in conductivity, thermally stable, nonvolatile and nonflammable and chemically
stable. To prepare Li battery with chloroaluminate $\mathrm{ILs}, \mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$ should be added to make Li metal deposition possible. An example is $\mathrm{Li} /[\mathrm{EMIm}] \mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}-\mathrm{LiCl}- \mathrm{SOCl}_{2} /$ graphite, but this battery does not work well. For Li batteries with nonchloroaluminate ILs of alkylimidazolium cation, Li electrode should be replaced by, for example, $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ electrode that has more positive potential because imidazolium cation is decomposed at more positive potentials than $\mathrm{Li}^{+} / \mathrm{Li}$ electrode. With the battery, $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12} /[\mathrm{EMIm}]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right]-\mathrm{Li}\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right] / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$, however, cell voltage was reduced to about 2 V , which was too low for Li battery. If IL of nonimidazolium cation, such as 1-ethyl-2-methylpyrazolium ([EMPy] ${ }^{+}$) and $N$-methyl- $N$-propylpiperidinium ( $[\text { PMPip }]^{+}$), are used, the Li electrode can be used. The batteries with these cations, $\mathrm{Li} /\left[\mathrm{EMPy}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{6}\right]-\mathrm{Li}\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right] / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{Li} /[\mathrm{PMPip}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]-\mathrm{Li}\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right] / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$, can give emfs of nearly 3.9 V and fairly good charge-discharge characteristics. However, the Li batteries using ILs are still inferior in performance to those using nonaqueous solvents.
(c) Other electrochemical devices Lu et al. [66] used [BMIm][BF4] and [BMIm][PF6] to construct electrochemical mechanical actuators, electrochromic windows and numeric displays made from three types of $\pi$-conjugated polymers: polyaniline, polypyrrole and polythiophene. The $\pi$-conjugated polymers in ILs were electrochemically cycled with enhanced lifetimes (up to 1 million cycles) without failure and with fast cycle switching speeds ( 100 ms ). Brazier et al. [67] studied electrochromic devices by using [BMPyr][ $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}$ ] to make two Li-ion conducting elements and by interposing them between transparent film electrodes such as $\mathrm{WO}_{3}$ and Li-charged $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$. The Li-ion conducting elements were [BMPyr][ $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}$ ] - Li[ $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}$ ] (liquid) and [BMPyr] [ $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}$ ]-P(EO)- $\mathrm{Li}\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ (solid). During $\mathrm{WO}_{3}$ coloration, these showed higher optical contrast than a conventional nonaqueous electrolyte such as PC-Li[NTf ${ }_{2}$ ].

### 13.3.4 <br> Risks of ILs and Their Decomposition

ILs are considered to be more green substitutes of conventional volatile organic solvents. This is mainly because ILs are believed to be nontoxic and nonflammable due to their nonvolatile properties. Certainly, air pollution is reduced by using ILs, but recently, the risks of ILs to the aquatic systems and soils have been pointed out. Many reports are now being published every year on this subject, and two reviews have appeared in 2007 [68].

To test the effect of ILs in aquatic ecosystems, Daphnia magna are used because they are the most popular live food for aquarium fish. The acute and chronic toxicity of imidazolium cation-based ILs was first examined by Bernot and coworkers [69]: the median lethal concentration (LC50) was used as an indicator for the acute toxicity. As shown in Table 13.7, the toxicities of imidazolium ILs (anions: $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{Br}^{-}, \mathrm{PF}_{6}{ }^{-}$and $\mathrm{BF}_{4}{ }^{-}$) are analogous to ammonia and phenol, which are fairly toxic. These ILs, if leak out, may be more damaging to aquatic ecosystems than conventional volatile organic solvents. Daphnia magna were also used to know the effects of ILs based on

${ }^{a}$ From Ref. [69].
imidazolium, pyridinium, phosphonium and ammonium cations, and the results have been shown using EC50 (medium effective concentration) values, as also summarized in Table 13.7 [70]. All tested ILs had EC50 values below $100 \mathrm{mgl}^{-1}$, imidazolium cations being most toxic and, irrespective of the cationic type, the toxicities of ( $\mathrm{C}_{8}-\mathrm{C}_{18}$ )-substituted salts are much severer than those of ( $\mathrm{C}_{1}-\mathrm{C}_{4}$ )substituted salts. There are also studies on microorganism toxicology, cytotoxicology and animal tests. In all these cases, more or less toxic effects are observed; the increase in lipophilicity of ILs by elongation of alkyl side chains increases the toxicities [68b].

ILs, more or less, can be distilled and decomposed at $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ under reduced pressure [71]. ILs can easily be disposed of by incineration at temperatures above $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, at which even the toughest IL will give up [72]. But, because of the high stability of ILs, their disposal or leakage may cause their accumulation in the environment. Although IL cations are generally not subjected to abiotic hydrolysis, IL anions may undergo hydrolysis. $\mathrm{AlCl}_{4}{ }^{-}$of chloroaluminate ILs are hydrolyzed and give HCl that is corrosive. Of nonchloroaluminate $\mathrm{ILs}, \mathrm{PF}_{6}{ }^{-}$ion is hydrolyzed in the presence of acid and gives toxic HF [73]. Other fluorinated anions may, more or less, be hydrolyzed; even the most stable $\mathrm{NTf}_{2}{ }^{-}$has some tendency to hydrolyze, as detected by pH change [74].

If ILs are accumulated in the environment, it is desirable to biologically degrade them. The biodegradation of 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium, $N$-alkylpyridinium and $N$-alkyl-4-(dimethylamino)-pyridinium halides (mainly $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$salts) has recently been studied [75]. It was found that significant primary biodegradation occurred for (eco)toxicologically unfavorable compounds carrying long alkyl side chain (C6 and

C8). In contrast, for (eco)toxicologically more recommendable imidazolium ILs with short alkyl (<C6) and short functionalized side chain, no biological degradation was found. The introduction of different functional groups into the side chain moiety did not improve biological degradation. After an incubation period of 24 days for the 1-methyl-3-octylimidazolium cation, different biological transformation products carrying hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups were identified. Furthermore, shortened side chain moieties were identified indicating the degradation of the octyl side chain via $\beta$-oxidation. In the report [75], an electrochemical wastewater treatment was proposed for nonbiodegradable ILs, as part of an alternative disposal strategy: 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium cation was completely destroyed within 4 h using an electrolysis double cell equipped with electrodes made of iridium oxide (anode), stainless steel (cathode) and a boron-doped diamond-coated bipolar electrode. The products formed electrochemically were easily accessible to biological degradation. The electrochemical decomposition of ILs has also been reported in [76]: when large voltages were applied, $[\mathrm{BMPyr}]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ formed decomposition products such as methylpyrrolidine, octanes, octenes, 2-butanol, dibutylmethylamine and butylpyrrolidine. On the other hand, the electrochemical breakdown of $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\mathrm{BF}_{4}\right]$ formed 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium radicals that react with each other mainly to disproportionate.
