## 14

## Electrochemistry at the Liquid-Liquid Interfaces

Electrochemistry at the liquid-liquid interfaces is considered by dividing them into two parts: interfaces between two immiscible electrolyte solutions (Section 14.1) and interfaces between ionic liquids (IL) and water (W) (Section 14.2).

## 14.1 <br> Interfaces Between Two Immiscible Electrolyte Solutions (ITIES)

In conventional polarography and voltammetry, the indicator electrodes used are electronic conductors such as metals, carbons or semiconductors. In an electrode reaction, an electron transfer (ET) occurs at the electrode/solution interface. Recently, however, it became possible to measure an ion transfer (IT), an electron transfer or a couple of ion-electron transfers at the interface between two immiscible electrolyte solutions (ITIES) by means of voltammetry or polarography. Many books [1] and review articles [2] have been published on this subject. Here, only an outline is provided.

### 14.1.1 <br> Fundamentals of Electrochemistry at the ITIES

Typical examples of the immiscible liquid-liquid interfaces are water (W)/nitrobenzene (NB), W/1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) and W/o-nitrophenoloctylether (o-NPOE). Here, the solubilities (in $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}, 298 \mathrm{~K}$ ) of W in NB, DCE and $o$-NPOE are $0.2,0.11$ and $4.61 \times 10^{-2}$, respectively, while those of NB, DCE and $o$-NPOE in W are $1.5 \times 10^{-2}, 8.5 \times 10^{-2}$ and $2.01 \times 10^{-6}$, respectively [2d]. Thus, in reality, W/o-NPOE is almost immiscible but W/NB and W/DCE are slightly miscible. Here, W is saturated with organic solvent $(\mathrm{O})$ and O with W , but in this section, they are simply shown by W and O , respectively. Figure 14.1 shows the density profile that was obtained by molecular dynamics of water/DCE interface [3]. The interface is very sharp with a thickness of less than $10 \AA$, though it loses its sharpness to a considerable extent when an external electric field is applied to the interface.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-378.jpg?height=483&width=850&top_left_y=198&top_left_x=355)
Figure 14.1 Density profile at the water/DCE interface calculated by molecular dynamics [3].

Two examples of the cell used in measurements are shown in Figure 14.2. The indicator electrode is a flat electrode for (a), while it is a dropping electrolyte electrode for (b). In both cases, the cells are usually equipped with four other electrodes, two of which are counter electrodes for supporting the current flow, while the other two are reference electrodes for measuring the potential of the indicator electrode. In practice, various other types of indicator electrodes are used: among them are a gelled electrolyte electrode, a polymer membrane electrode, a micro liquid-liquid interface electrode and an array of these electrodes.

Curve 1 in Figure 14.3 is the current-potential relations at a dropping electrolyte electrode with a cell construction, $\mathrm{Ag}|\mathrm{AgCl}| \mathrm{SCl}(\mathrm{W})|\mathrm{SY}(\mathrm{O})|\left(\mathrm{LiCl}+\mathrm{MgSO}_{4}\right)(\mathrm{W})|\mathrm{AgCl}|$ Ag , where O is NB and SY is tetrabutylammonium tetraphenylborate ( $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}$ ). The interface $\mathrm{SCl}(\mathrm{W}) \mid \mathrm{SY}(\mathrm{O})$ is depolarized because $\mathrm{S}^{+}$is contained in both solvents.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-378.jpg?height=513&width=1050&top_left_y=1347&top_left_x=341)
Figure 14.2 Cells for ITIES measurements: (a) cell with a stationary interface for voltammetry and (b) cell with a dropping electrolyte electrode for polarography. (1) aqueous electrolyte phase; ( $1^{\prime}$ ) aqueous electrolyte drop; (2) organic phase; (3) Teflon capillary; (4) reservoir of aqueous electrolyte. CE and RE show the counter and reference electrodes.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-379.jpg?height=516&width=846&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=209)
Figure 14.3 ITIES polarogram obtained with a dropping electrolyte electrode. Curve 1: in $0.05 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{LiCl}+1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{MgSO}_{4}$ (water) and $0.05 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NBPh}_{4}$ (NB); curve 2: in $1+0.5 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{Me}_{4} \mathrm{NCl}$ (water). Dashed curve: curve 2 - curve 1. The potential $E$ is referred to the Galvani potential difference between 0.05 M $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NCl}$ (water) and $0.05 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NBPh}_{4}$ (NB).

However, the interface $\mathrm{SY}(\mathrm{O}) \mid\left(\mathrm{LiCl}+\mathrm{MgSO}_{4}\right)(\mathrm{W})$ is polarized: $\mathrm{LiCl}(0.05 \mathrm{M})$ and $\mathrm{MgSO}_{4}(1 \mathrm{M})$ are almost insoluble in NB , while $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}(0.05 \mathrm{M})$ is almost insoluble in W. Thus, $\mathrm{LiCl}, \mathrm{MgSO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}$ work as supporting electrolytes and only a small current (residual current) flows within the potential windows.

The transfer of ion $i^{z}$ occurs near the standard ion transfer potential (standard Galvani potential difference), $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}(i)$, which is related to the standard Gibbs energy of transfer, $\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(i, \mathrm{O} \rightarrow W)$, and the standard chemical potentials of $i^{z}$ in W and O , $\mu_{i, \mathrm{~W}}^{0}$ and $\mu_{i, \mathrm{O}}^{0}$, by

$$
\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}(i)=-\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(i, \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{~W}) / z F=\left(\mu_{i, \mathrm{O}}^{0}-\mu_{i, \mathrm{~W}}^{0}\right) / z F
$$

The individual values of these quantities are not accessible thermodynamically. But if an extrathermodynamic assumption is introduced, these quantities can be obtained by the measurements of solubility, partition, potentiometry or voltammetry (see Chapter 2). A popular extrathermodynamic assumption is $\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{As}^{+}, \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\right)= \Delta G_{t}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{~B}^{-}, \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\right)$ or $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{t}^{0}\left(\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{As}^{+}\right)=-\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{t}^{0}\left(\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{~B}^{-}\right)$. Voltammetrically, this corresponds to using $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{AsPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}$ as the supporting electrolyte in O and taking the center of the current-potential curves for the transfers of $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{As}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{~B}^{-}$as potential zero [4]. The $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}(i)$ values from NB, DCE and $o$-NPOE to W are shown in Figure 14.4 [5]. The hydrophobicity of the cation increases by going to lower part of the figure, while that of the anion increases by going to its upper part. From this, it is apparent that, for curve 1 in Figure 14.3, the left limit corresponds to the transfer of $\mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$from NB to W and the right limit to the transfer of $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{~B}^{-}$from NB to W.

The width of the potential window depends considerably on solvents and the electrolytes. Figure 14.5a shows cyclic voltammograms at the 0.1 M tetraoctylam-

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-380.jpg?height=1650&width=1219&top_left_y=205&top_left_x=244)
Figure $14.4 \Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}(i)$ values from NB, DCE and o-NPOE to $W$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C} . \mathrm{M}$ and Me , methyl; $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ and Et , ethyl; Pr , propyl; $\mathrm{C}_{4}$ and Bu , butyl; Pe, pentyl; $\mathrm{C}_{6}$ and Hex, hexyl; $\mathrm{C}_{8}$, octyl; $\mathrm{C}_{10}$, decyl; $\mathrm{C}_{12}$, dodecyl; Ph, phenyl; Bz, benzyl; Im, imidazolium; AcCh, acetylcholine; CV, crystal violet.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-381.jpg?height=1087&width=656&top_left_y=189&top_left_x=207)
Figure 14.5 (a) Cyclic voltammograms of the 0.1 M tetraoctylammonium tetrakis(4-chlorophenyl)borate (DCH, DCB, or DCE)| $0.05 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{~W})$ interfaces [6a] and (b) those of 0.1 M $\mathrm{LiCl}(\mathrm{W}) \mid 0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}$ (curve 1) or tetrapentylammonium tetrakis[3,5-bis (trifluoromethyl)pentyl]borate (curve 2) (o-NPOE) interfaces [6b]. The potentials are referred to the $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{AsPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}$ assumption.

monium tetrakis(4-chlorophenyl)borate(DCH, DCB or DCE) $\mid 0.05 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ (W) interfaces, where DCH is 1,6 -dichlorohexane, DCB is 1,4-dichlorobutane and DCE is 1,2-dichloroethane [6a]. The width of the potential window increases in the order $\mathrm{DCE}<\mathrm{DCB}<\mathrm{DCH}$. Figure 14.5b shows cyclic voltammograms for 0.1 M LiCl in W and $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Bu}_{4} \mathrm{NPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}$ (curve 1) or tetrapentylammonium tetrakis[3,5-bis(trifluoromethyl)pentyl]borate (curve 2 ) in o-NPOE [6b]. The potential window of curve 2 is much wider than that of curve 1.

## Ion Transfer at the W/O Interface

When the two solutions contain ion $i^{z}$ of activities $a_{i, \mathrm{O}}$ and $a_{i, \mathrm{~W}}$ (concentrations $C_{i, \mathrm{O}}$ and $C_{i, \mathrm{~W}}$ ), the ion transfer potential $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}(i)$ is expressed by

$$
\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}(i)=\Delta_{O}^{W} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}(i)+\frac{R T}{z F} \ln \frac{a_{i, \mathrm{O}}}{a_{i, \mathrm{~W}}}=\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0^{\prime}}(i)+\frac{R T}{z F} \ln \frac{C_{i, \mathrm{O}}}{C_{i, \mathrm{~W}}}
$$

Here $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0^{\prime}}(i)$ is the formal ion transfer potential. If the value of $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0^{\prime}}(i)$ is within the potential window, a current-potential curve for ion transfer is obtained. If the IT process is diffusion controlled and the diffusion occurs through the diffusion layers of $\delta_{i, \mathrm{O}}$ and $\delta_{i, \mathrm{~W}}$ thickness, the current-potential curve due to the transfer of $i^{z}$ can be expressed as follows:

$$
E=E_{1 / 2}+\frac{R T}{z F} \ln \frac{i_{1, \mathrm{p}}-i}{i-i_{1, \mathrm{n}}} \quad E_{1 / 2}=\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0^{\prime}}(i)+\frac{R T}{z F} \ln \frac{D_{i, \mathrm{~W}} \delta_{i, \mathrm{O}}}{D_{i, \mathrm{O}} \delta_{i, \mathrm{~W}}}+\text { const }
$$

where $E$ is the potential of W -side against O -side, $i_{1 \mathrm{p}}$ and $i_{1 \mathrm{n}}$ are the limiting currents at the positive and negative sides of the wave, $D_{i, \mathrm{~W}}$ and $D_{i, \mathrm{O}}$ the diffusion coefficients of $i^{z}$ in W and O , and 'const' depends on the potential of the reference electrode, $\mathrm{Ag}|\mathrm{AgCl}| \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{W})$, and the liquid junction potential at $\mathrm{SCl}(\mathrm{W}) \mid \mathrm{SY}(\mathrm{O}) . i_{1 \mathrm{p}}$ and $i_{1 \mathrm{n}}$ can be expressed by the following:

$$
i_{i, \mathrm{p}}=z F D_{i, \mathrm{~W}} C_{i, \mathrm{~W}} / \delta_{i, \mathrm{w}} \quad i_{i, \mathrm{n}}=z F D_{i, \mathrm{O}} C_{i, \mathrm{O}} / \delta_{i, \mathrm{O}}
$$

The shapes of the current-potential curves are similar to those in Figure 5.6. Such curves can nearly be obtained with a dropping electrolyte electrode and with an ultramicroelectrode. In Figure 14.3, curve 2 was obtained with a dropping electrolyte electrode for the IT of $0.5 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{Me}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$in W to NB. The limiting current is expressed by the Ilkovič equation and is proportional to the concentration. With a flat electrode, a single-sweep voltammetry and a cyclic voltammetry give current-potential curves as shown in Figures 5.19 and 5.21, respectively.

Here, if ion $i^{z}$ in W forms at the interface a complex with neutral ligand Lin O or an ion pair with ion $j^{z^{\prime}}$ in O , then the ITprocess will be facilitated. In case if the formation constants are appropriate, the IT process that originally occurs outside the potential window will occur within the potential window. For example, Figure 14.6 shows cyclic voltammograms of base electrolytes ( $1,1^{\prime}$ ) and 1 mM 2 -phenylethylammonium ion ( $2,2^{\prime}$ ) in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 0.01 M dibenzo-18-crown-6 in NB [7]. Base electrolytes are 0.01 M HCl in W and 0.01 M tetraphenylarsonium $3,3^{\prime}$-como-bis (undecahydro-1,2-dicarba-3-cobalta-closo-dodecaborate) in NB. These processes are not only useful to get the information about the complexation or ion pair formation but also conveniently applicable to increase the target ions for chemical analyses.

## Electron Transfer at the W/O Interface

We consider an electron transfer reaction between a hydrophilic redox couple ( $\mathrm{Ox}_{1}$ / $\left.\operatorname{Red}_{1}\right)$ in W and a hydrophobic redox couple $\left(\mathrm{Ox}_{2} / \operatorname{Red}_{2}\right)$ in O .

$$
\operatorname{Red}_{2}(\mathrm{O})+\mathrm{Ox}_{1}(\mathrm{~W}) \rightleftarrows \operatorname{Red}_{1}(\mathrm{~W})+\mathrm{Ox}_{2}(\mathrm{O})
$$

Here, the number of electrons is equal to $n$ in both cases, namely, $\mathrm{Ox}_{1}(\mathrm{~W})+ n \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \operatorname{Red}_{1}(\mathrm{~W})$ and $\mathrm{Ox}_{2}(\mathrm{O})+n \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftarrows \operatorname{Red}_{2}(\mathrm{O})$. If this reaction system is in equilibrium, the Galvani potential difference ( $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{ET}}$ ) at the $\mathrm{O} / \mathrm{W}$ interface is given by the following equation:

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-383.jpg?height=779&width=758&top_left_y=193&top_left_x=214)
Figure 14.6 Cyclic voltammograms of base electrolyte ( $1,1^{\prime}$ ) and 1 mM 2-phenylethylammonium ion $\left(2,2^{\prime}\right)$ in W in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 10 mM dibenzo-18-crown-6 in NB. Sweep rate $0.02 \mathrm{~V} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$.

$$
\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{ET}}=\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{ET}}^{0}+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{a_{\mathrm{Red}_{1}, \mathrm{~W}} a_{\mathrm{Ox}_{2}, \mathrm{O}}}{a_{\mathrm{Red}_{2}, \mathrm{O}} a_{\mathrm{Ox}_{1}, \mathrm{~W}}}=\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{ET}}^{0^{\prime}}+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{C_{\mathrm{Red}_{1}, \mathrm{~W}} C_{\mathrm{Ox}_{2}, \mathrm{O}}}{C_{\mathrm{Red}_{2}, \mathrm{O}} C_{\mathrm{Ox}_{1}, \mathrm{~W}}}
$$

where $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{ET}}^{0}$ and $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{ET}}^{0^{\prime}}$ are the standard and formal Galvani potential differences, respectively, and $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{ET}}^{0^{\prime}}=E_{\mathrm{O}_{2} / \operatorname{Red} 2}^{0^{\prime}}-E_{\mathrm{O}_{1} / \operatorname{Red} 1}^{0^{\prime}}$ ( $E_{\mathrm{Ox} / \operatorname{Red}}^{0^{\prime}}$ : formal redox potential). When $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{ET}}^{0^{\prime}}$ is within the potential window, a wave due to the ET reaction of Eq. (14.4) can be observed. The general equation for the wave can be expressed as

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E=A+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{\left(i_{1, \mathrm{Ox}_{2}}-i\right)\left(i_{1, \operatorname{Red}_{1}}-i\right)}{\left(i_{1, \mathrm{Ox}_{1}}-i\right)\left(i_{1, \operatorname{Red}_{2}}-i\right)} \\
& A=E^{0^{\prime}}+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{\left(D_{\mathrm{Ox}_{1}, \mathrm{~W}} / \delta_{\mathrm{Ox}_{1}, \mathrm{~W}}\right)\left(D_{\operatorname{Red}_{2}, \mathrm{O}} / \delta_{\operatorname{Red}_{2}, \mathrm{O}}\right)}{\left(D_{\mathrm{Ox}_{2}, \mathrm{O}} / \delta_{\mathrm{Ox}_{2}, \mathrm{O}}\right)\left(D_{\operatorname{Red}_{1}, \mathrm{~W}} / \delta_{\operatorname{Red}_{1}, \mathrm{~W}}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$E^{0^{\prime}}$ is the formal potential of the reaction (14.4) versus the reference electrode in Wside and $i_{1, i}$ is the limiting current proportional to the concentration of species $i$. When $\operatorname{Red}_{2}$ in O and $\mathrm{Ox}_{1} / \operatorname{Red}_{1}$ couple in W are present and the concentrations of $\mathrm{Ox}_{1} / \operatorname{Red}_{1}$ are much larger than that of $\operatorname{Red}_{2}$, the above equation reduces to

$$
E=A+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{i_{1, \operatorname{Red}_{1}}}{i_{1, \mathrm{Ox}_{1}}}+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{i}{i_{1, \operatorname{Red}_{2}}-i}
$$

This means that the same wave form is obtained as that of the IT process.

The truly heterogeneous ET process can be obtained when a sufficiently hydrophobic redox species is used in the O-phase. Lutetium biphthalocyanine ( $\mathrm{LuPc}_{2}$ ) is considered to be such a redox species.

## Couples of Ion/Electron Transfers at the W/O Interphasial Region

The system of Fc in NB and $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}{ }^{3-}$ in W was first considered to be an example of the electron transfer at the W/O interface. But the mechanism of the system has been shown to be as follows [8]:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Fc}(\mathrm{NB}) \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Fc}(\mathrm{~W}) \\
& \mathrm{Fc}(\mathrm{~W})+\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}^{3-}(\mathrm{W}) \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Fc}^{+}(\mathrm{W})+\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}^{4-}(\mathrm{W}) \\
& \mathrm{Fc}^{+}(\mathrm{W}) \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Fc}^{+}(\mathrm{NB})
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, the ET reaction (14.9) occurs homogeneously in the W-side and the electric current flows by the IT reaction (14.10). The partition of Fc into W, Eq. (14.8), is not favored, but the depth of the reaction field for the homogeneous ET reaction in W (a few hundred micrometers) is much larger than that of the heterogeneous ETreaction at the W/O interface (several hundred picometers). Thus, the IT mechanism, Eq. (14.10), is the major process for the current flow. Various phenomena, which were first considered to be the ET processes, are now recognized to be the couples of ion/electron transfers.

### 14.1.2 <br> Practical Applications of Electrochemistry at the ITIES

Voltammetry including single-sweep, cyclic and differential pulse methods, polarography at a dropping electrolyte electrode and amperometry for the IT processes are applicable to the analyses of various ionic species; especially, the applications to redox-inactive biological and pharmaceutical species are promising. They are also useful to get fundamental data of the IT processes, which can be used in the separation chemistry, in the elucidation of biological membrane processes and in the fabrication of ion-selective electrodes. Extraction of ions from aqueous phase 1 (W1) to aqueous phase 2 (W2) by the application of potential difference across the W1/LM/W2 (LM: liquid membrane) interfaces is also useful [9]. The facilitation of the ITprocesses by complexations and ion pair formations increases the ionic species that can be analyzed or extracted.

As an analytical application, an electrochemical flow cell has been constructed for rapid and coulometric IT at the W/O interface (Figure 14.7) [10]. The cell was composed of a porous Teflon tube ( 1 mm in inner diameter), an $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}$ wire ( 0.8 mm in diameter) inserted into the tube, a Pt wire placed outside the tube, Ophase in which the tube was immersed and a tetraphenylborate ISE (connected to an $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}$ reference electrode) in O-phase. The W-phase containing a species of interest was flowed through the narrow gap between the tube and the $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}$ wire. The potential was applied by using the $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}$ wire and the ISE, and the current due

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-385.jpg?height=663&width=1300&top_left_y=191&top_left_x=207)
Figure 14.7 (a) Electrochemical flow cell for rapid, coulometric IT at the W/O interface [10]. (b) A porous Teflon tube ( 1 mm in inner diameter) and an $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}$ wire ( 0.8 mm in diameter) inserted into the tube.

to the IT was measured by using the $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}$ and Pt wire electrodes. The IT process was complete within the time of flow of the sample through the cell (about 40 s ). The cell was used for flow coulometric analyses (p. 151) of $\mathrm{K}^{+}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ with excellent results.

Charge transfer processes that occur, including an intermediate or a product adsorbed at the interfacial region, are of practical interest:

1. Formation of a silicate membrane by the sol-gel process at the liquid-liquid interface is possible by this process [11]. The W-phase contains a precursor $\left(\mathrm{Si}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{8}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}{ }^{4-}\right.$ ) and the O-phase the template ion (e.g. trimethyloctadecylammonium ion, [TODA] ${ }^{+}$). The template ion, being transferred to the interface and adsorbed there, reacts with the precursor to form a solid.

$$
4[\mathrm{TODA}]^{+}(\mathrm{O})+\mathrm{Si}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{8}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}^{4-}(\mathrm{W}) \rightleftarrows\left[\mathrm{TODA}_{4} \mathrm{Si}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{8}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}(\text { solid })\right.
$$

2. Electropolymerization at the W/O interface has been used to prepare the polymer films of polypyrrole, polythiophene, etc. [12]. The monomer, which is in DCE with $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{AsBPh}_{4}$, is adsorbed at the interface and reacts with an oxidizing agent ( $\mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$, $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$, etc.) in W to form a polymer film.
3. Electrodepositions of noble metal particles at the interface are also useful. Since Au particles were deposited by the ET reaction between $\mathrm{AuCl}_{4}{ }^{-}$in DCE and Fe $(\mathrm{CN})_{6}{ }^{4-}$ in W [13], electrodeposited particles of various noble metals such as Pd, $\mathrm{Pt}, \mathrm{Pd} / \mathrm{Pt}$ and Ag have been prepared on macro-, micro- or nanoscopic ITIES and membrane-supported ITIES [14]. As a reducing agent, $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}{ }^{4-}$ in W is used when noble metal complex is in DCE and a ferrocene derivative (dimethylferro-
cene, butylferrocene, etc.) in DCE is used when noble metal complex is in W. The W/O interface is smooth and free of defects that act as a preferential nucleation sites; thus, nanoscale metal particles are usually formed at the interface.

Phase transfer catalysis has been used in organic synthesis to perform reactions in O when some of the reactants are present in W: an example is the substitution reaction involving alkylchloride ( RCl ), $\mathrm{RCl}(\mathrm{O})+\mathrm{NaCN}(\mathrm{W})=\mathrm{RCN}(\mathrm{O})+\mathrm{NaCl}$ (W) [15]. This reaction proceeds only after the addition of tetraalkylammonium chloride, $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NCl}$, as a catalyst. It is considered that $\mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{NCl}$ is partitioned between the two phases and a distribution potential is set up that drives $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$or electron to the target substance. Polarization studies at the ITIES are useful for the systematic search of suitable catalysts.

### 14.1.3 <br> Three-Phase Electrochemistry

Three-phase electrochemistry involves three phases in contact: one is the working (indicator) electrode (graphite, Pt or Au ) and the other two are liquids that are immiscible (usually O and W ). During the electrode process, ions move across the boundary between O and W . Two types of cells exist (Figure 14.8): one uses a flat working electrode and the other a cylindrical working electrode. In the first, a semidroplet of O is attached to the flat graphite disk working electrode surface, which, in turn, is immersed into the W-phase (Figure 14.8a) [16]. In the second, a cylindrical microelectrode (Pt or Au ) is immersed vertically across the $\mathrm{O} / \mathrm{W}$ interface (Figure 14.8b) [17a] or inserted horizontally into a droplet of O-phase in the W-phase

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-386.jpg?height=576&width=1355&top_left_y=1284&top_left_x=117)
Figure 14.8 Cells for three-phase electrochemistry: (a) a drop of O attached to the flat graphite disk electrode surface is immersed into the W-phase, (b) a cylindrical microelectrode ( Pt or Au ) is immersed vertically across the $\mathrm{O} / \mathrm{W}$ interface and ( $\mathrm{b}^{\prime}$ ) into a drop of O-phase in the W-phase, a cylindrical Pt microelectrode is inserted.

(Figure 14.8b') [17b]. In all cases, two electrodes (reference and auxiliary) are used besides the working electrode, and usually an electroactive (redox) substance is added to O while a supporting electrolyte is added to W . This is in contrast to the conventional electrochemistry at the ITIES, in which four electrodes (two references and two auxiliaries (counters)) are used besides the electrode of W/O interface and the supporting electrolytes are added to both W and O . The case that employs a semidroplet O-phase has been reviewed in detail [16]. In the case that employs the cylindrical microelectrode, the ETprocess occurs in the O-phase and simultaneously the IT process occurs from W-phase to O-phase.

$$
\operatorname{Red}(\mathrm{O})+n A^{-}(\mathrm{W}) \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Ox}^{n+}(\mathrm{O})+n \mathrm{~A}^{-}(\mathrm{O})+n \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

The formal potential of the process can be expressed by

$$
E_{\mathrm{f}}=E_{\mathrm{Ox} / \operatorname{Red}(\mathrm{O})}^{0^{\prime}}+\Delta_{\mathrm{W}}^{\mathrm{O}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}\right)-\frac{R T}{F} \ln a_{\mathrm{A}^{-}(\mathrm{W})}+\frac{R T}{F} \ln \left(\frac{a_{\operatorname{Red}(\mathrm{O})}}{2}\right)
$$

where $E_{\mathrm{Ox} / \operatorname{Red}(\mathrm{O})}^{0^{\prime}}$ is the formal potential of $\operatorname{Red}(\mathrm{O}) / \mathrm{Ox}^{n+}(\mathrm{O})$ couple, $\Delta_{\mathrm{W}}^{\mathrm{O}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}\right)$is the standard Galvani potential difference for transfer of $\mathrm{A}^{-}$from W to O and $a$ is the activity. Thus, from the measurements of the formal potential, the values of $\Delta_{\mathrm{W}}^{\mathrm{O}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}\right)$can be obtained. The ET process occurs in the O-side, but the width of the reaction layer from the interface depends on the $\Delta_{\mathrm{W}}^{\mathrm{O}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}\right)$values. With anion $\mathrm{A}^{-}$ of high hydrophlicity, the reaction layer is very thin.

## 14.2 <br> Interfaces Between Ionic Liquids and Water

The electrochemistry at the interface between hydrophobic ionic liquid and water is a new research field and various phenomena, which are similar to or different from the electrochemistry at the ITIES, can be observed. A useful review article is available on this topic [18].

### 14.2.1 <br> Fundamentals

The mutual solubility of IL and W plays an important role in determining the electrochemical behavior at the IL/W interface. But, as shown in Table 14.1, it varies considerably depending on the ILs. If the ions of the IL dissolved in W do not form an ion pair, the solubility of the IL in W is determined by the solubility product $K_{\mathrm{sp}}^{\mathrm{W}}$ of the IL . Here, if the IL is composed of cation $\mathrm{C}^{+}$and anion $\mathrm{A}^{-}$, it is expressed by

$$
\ln K_{\mathrm{sp}}^{\mathrm{W}}=-\frac{\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{C}^{+}, \mathrm{IL} \rightarrow \mathrm{~W}\right)+\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}, \mathrm{IL} \rightarrow \mathrm{~W}\right)}{R T}
$$

where $\Delta G_{t}\left(\mathrm{C}^{+}, \mathrm{IL} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\right)$ and $\Delta G_{t}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}, \mathrm{IL} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\right)$ are the Gibbs energies of transfer of $\mathrm{C}^{+}$and $\mathrm{A}^{-}$and are the measures of the hydrophobicity of ions. But they cannot have a universal significance because each of them somewhat depends on the counter ion

Table 14.1 Mutual solubilities of water with hydrophobic ionic liquids (IL) and organic solvents (O). ${ }^{a}$
| IL or O | Solubility ofW in IL or O |  | Solubility of IL or O in W |  | $\Delta_{\text {IL }}^{\text {W }} \boldsymbol{\phi}^{\boldsymbol{\prime}} \boldsymbol{,} \boldsymbol{\mathrm { V }}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
|  | wt\% | $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}$ | wt\% | $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}$ |  |
| Moderately hydrophobic ILs |  |  |  |  |  |

| $[\mathrm{BMIm}]\left[\left(\mathrm{CF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{2}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~N}\right]$ | 1.48 | $8.20 \times 10^{-1}$ | 0.716 | $1.72 \times 10^{-2}$ | 0.02 |
| $[\mathrm{HMIm}]\left[\left(\mathrm{CF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{2}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~N}\right]$ | 1.05 | $5.83 \times 10^{-1}$ | 0.237 | $5.30 \times 10^{-3}$ |  |
| $[\mathrm{OMIm}]\left[\left(\mathrm{CF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{2}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~N}\right]$ | 0.87 | $4.83 \times 10^{-1}$ | $8.87 \times 10^{-2}$ | $1.87 \times 10^{-3}$ | -0.04 |
| $[\mathrm{OMIm}]\left[\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~N}\right]$ | 0.6 | $3 \times 10^{-1}$ |  | $1.1 \times 10^{-3}$ | -0.01 |

${ }^{a}$ From [17b]. Temperature: $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. $\left[\mathrm{C}_{18} \mathrm{Iq}\right]=N$-octadecylisoquinolinium, $[\mathrm{TOMA}]=$ trioctylmethylammonium and $[\mathrm{TFPB}]=$ tetrakis $[3,5$-bis(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]borate. For $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi^{\prime}$, see text.

composing the IL. However, the polarities of ILs of different types are very similar to each other and comparable to those of lower aliphatic alcohols. Thus, it seems reasonable to consider that the hydrophobicities of $\mathrm{C}^{+}$and $\mathrm{A}^{-}$of the IL resemble the hydrophobicities of $\mathrm{C}^{+}$and $\mathrm{A}^{-}$in organic solvent of similar polarities. Here, we select $N B$ as such organic solvent $(O)$ : the standard ion transfer potential, $\Delta_{N B}^{W} \phi_{t}^{o}(i)$, which is equal to $-\Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}(i, \mathrm{NB} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}) /(z F)$ ( $z$ is the charge of $i^{z}$ ), is considered instead of the standard ion transfer potential $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}(i)$. The values of $\Delta_{\mathrm{NB}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}(i)$ shown in Figure 14.4 are referred to the $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{AsPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}$ assumption, i.e. $\mathrm{Ph}_{4} \mathrm{AsPh}_{4} \mathrm{~B}$ is used as the supporting electrolyte and the center of symmetry of the current-potential curve was considered $\Delta_{\mathrm{NB}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi=0$.

When the absolute values of $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}(i)$ for $\mathrm{C}^{+}$and $\mathrm{A}^{-}$are different, the difference in the solubilities of $\mathrm{C}^{+}$and $\mathrm{A}^{-}$causes the distribution potential difference across the IL/W interface, $\Delta_{\text {IL }}^{\text {W }} \phi$, to compensate for the difference in solubilities. Here, $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi=\left(\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}\left(\mathrm{C}^{+}\right)+\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{-}\right)\right) / 2$. Some $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi^{\prime}$ values, estimated using $\Delta_{\mathrm{NB}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}(i)$ instead of $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{t}}^{0}(i)$, are also shown in Table 14.1; the positive value shows that W-side is positive with respect to the IL-phase and vice versa. For example, $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi^{\prime}$ for [ $\mathrm{Hex}_{4} \mathrm{~N}$ ] $\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ is very negative ( -0.17 V ) due to the high hydrophobicity of $\mathrm{Hex}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$; it suggests that the extraction of cations from W to IL is suppressed and that the IL surface carries considerable negative charges.

When W contains electrolytes, the partitioning of ions from W to IL affects $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi$. But this occurs only when relatively hydrophobic ions are contained in W. When W
contains only hydrophilic ions, their dissolution in IL is negligible, and $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi$ is still determined by the partitioning of the ions constituting the IL: $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi$ stays constant irrespective of the type and concentration of the hydrophilic electrolyte in W.

### 14.2.2 <br> Salt Bridges and Reference Electrodes

As an application of the constancy of $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi$, Kakiuchi et al. proposed an IL salt bridge [19]. The $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi$ values for a $\left[\mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{MIm}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ salt bridge, gelled with poly (vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene), were constant when immersed in aqueous solutions containing from $2 \times 10^{-5}$ to 2 M KCl , were stabilized within 1 min and were stable within $\pm 1 \mathrm{mV}$ for at least 3 months. The constant $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi$ values were also obtained for $\mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{LiCl}$ and NaCl solutions of concentrations $<0.2 \mathrm{M}$. If this salt bridge is inserted between two aqueous solutions, such as $\mathrm{W}_{1}|\mathrm{IL}| \mathrm{W}_{2}$, the $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi$ at $\mathrm{W}_{1} \mid \mathrm{IL}$ and IL $\mid \mathrm{W}_{2}$ cancel out and the total $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi$ value between $\mathrm{W}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{W}_{2}$ is negligibly small, even when the diffusion potential at $\mathrm{W}_{1} \mid \mathrm{W}_{2}$ is considerably large. Thus, this salt bridge can be used for measurements of purely aqueous systems.

This salt bridge is conceptually different from a concentrated aqueous KCl salt bridge, in which the working principle is the diffusion potential, i.e. a nonequilibrium property. In the case of $\left[\mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{MIm}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ salt bridge, $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi$ is defined by the distribution potential that is an equilibrium property. The drawback of this salt bridge is that the $\Delta_{\mathrm{IL}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi$ value is influenced if the aqueous solution contains a hydrophobic ion.

Kakiuchi et al. [20] also proposed an $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}$ reference electrode based on $\left[\mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{MIm}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ saturated with AgCl . Both gelled and nongelled $\left[\mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{MIm}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ gave $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}$ electrodes with stable potentials against aqueous KCl solutions between 0.05 mM and 2 M , making possible the miniaturized and solid-state reference electrodes. However, $\left[\mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{MIm}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ dissolves five times more AgCl than in W and, moreover, it dissolves in W up to $1.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$ and W dissolves in it up to $0.9 \mathrm{wt} \%$. Thus, it is desirable to separate the electrode compartment and the salt bridge, such as $\mathrm{Ag}|\mathrm{AgCl}| \mathrm{AgCl}$-satd. $\left[\mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{MIm}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right] ; \stackrel{:}{:}\left[\mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{MIm}\right]\left[\mathrm{NTf}_{2}\right]$ !;aqueous solution.

### 14.2.3 <br> Potential Windows and Voltammograms

Voltammetry that measures ion transfer at a polarized IL/W interface began very recently [21].

The wider the potential window of the IL/W interface the larger the hydrophobicity of the ions constituting the IL, i.e. the smaller the solubility of the IL into W, and the larger the hydrophilicity of the supporting electrolyte in W [18]. When the hydrophilicity of the supporting electrolyte in W is large enough, the potential window of IL, $\mathrm{C}^{+} \mathrm{A}^{-}$, is determined by the standard ion transfer potential, $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{C}^{+}}^{0}$ and $\Delta_{\mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{A}^{-}}^{0}$. For example, the difference between $\Delta_{\mathrm{NB}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{Hex}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}}^{0}$ and $\Delta_{\mathrm{NB}}^{\mathrm{W}} \phi_{\mathrm{NTf}_{2}}^{0}$ is 0.605 V and the actual potential window is about 0.3 V . Of course, the location of the potential window is of concern, in addition to its width. For ILs between $N$-octadecylisoquinolinium
( $\mathrm{C}_{18} \mathrm{Iq}^{+}$) or trioctylmethylammonium (TOMA ${ }^{+}$) and tetrakis[3,5-bis(trifluoromethyl)pheny]borate ([TFPB-]), the potential windows are about 0.85 V at $56^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, if the supporting electrolyte in W is 0.1 M LiCl . In this case, the potential limits on both sides are due to the transfers of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$from W to IL . The widest potential window hitherto obtained is 1.1 V at $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in IL of tetraheptylammonium ( $\mathrm{Hep}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+}$) and [TFPB ${ }^{-}$] and W containing $\mathrm{MgSO}_{4}$ [22]. This is wider than the potential window at the conventional ITIES and enables electrochemical studies of various charge transfer processes at the IL/W interface.

Figure 14.9 shows the voltammograms for the facilitated transfer of alkali metal ions in W with dibenzo-18-crown-6 (DB18C6) in [ $\mathrm{C}_{18} \mathrm{Iq}$ ][TFPB] [23]. The cell

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_7c440fa85051e8b62e3ag-390.jpg?height=1272&width=656&top_left_y=650&top_left_x=350)
Figure 14.9 Cyclic voltammograms for the transfer of various ions, facilitated by dibenzo-18-crown-6 (DB18C6). IL = [ $\left.\mathrm{C}_{18} \mid \mathrm{q}\right]$ [TFPB] (see Table 14.1). The concentration of the ions in W is 100 mM and of DB18C6 in IL is 20 mM [23].

construction is $\mathrm{Ag}|\mathrm{AgCl}| 100 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}, 1 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{C}_{18} \mathrm{IqCl}(\mathrm{W}) \mid \gamma \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{DB} 18 \mathrm{C} 6$, ( $\left[\mathrm{C}_{18} \mathrm{Iq}\right] [\mathrm{TFPB}])|x \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{MCl}(\mathrm{W})| \mathrm{AgCl} \mid \mathrm{Ag}$, where $\mathrm{M}^{+}$is either $\mathrm{Li}^{+}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{K}^{+}, \mathrm{Rb}^{+}$or $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$, and the typical values of $x$ and $y$ are 100 and 10, respectively. The stoichiometry of the complexes in [ $\mathrm{C}_{18}$ Iq][TFPB] is $1: 1$ for $\mathrm{Li}^{+}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{K}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Rb}^{+}$, while for the $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$ transfer both 1: 1 and 1: 2 complexes are likely to be formed. The logarithms for the formation constants $\log K_{\mathrm{f}}$ are $5.0,7.0,8.2$ and 7.3 for $\mathrm{Li}^{+}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{K}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Rb}^{+}$, respectively. Because $\left[\mathrm{C}_{18} \mathrm{Iq}\right][$ TFPB $]$ has high viscosity ( 55.7 cP at $56^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), cyclic voltammograms must be measured at the interface formed at the tip of a micropipette with a radius of about $20 \mu \mathrm{~m}$.

[^0]:    1) 'Green' chemistry is the utilization of a set of principles that reduces or eliminates the use or generation of hazardous substances in the design, manufacture and application of chemical products (Anastas, P.T., Warner, J.C. (1998) Green Chemistry, Theory and Practice, Oxford University Press, New York, p. 11). Under the framework of green chemistry, efforts are being made to find environmentally benign media (green solvents) for chemical processes; among such media are harmless
    nonaqueous solvents, immobilized solvents, ionic liquids, supercritical fluids, aqueous reaction systems and solvent-free reaction systems. For the recent situation, see, for example, Abraham, M.A., Moens, L. (eds) (2002) Clean Solvents: Alternative Media for Chemical Reactions and Processing, Oxford University Press, New York; Nelson, W.M. (2003) Green Solvents for Chemistry: Perspectives and Practice (Green Chemistry Series), Oxford University Press, New York.
[^1]:    ${ }^{a}$ From McManis, G.E., Golovin, M.N. and Weaver, M.J. (1986) J. Phys. Chem., 90, 6563; Barthel, J., Bachhuber, K., Buchner, R., Gill, J.B. and Kleebauer, M. (1990) Chem. Phys. Lett., 167, 62; Galus, Z. (1995) Advances in Electrochemical Science and Engineering, vol. 4 (eds H. Gerischer and C.W. Tobias), Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH, Weinheim, p. 222.
    $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{s}}$ : static permittivity; $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{op}}$ : optical permittivity; $\varepsilon_{\infty}$ : infinite frequency permittivity; $\left(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{op}}^{-1}-\varepsilon_{\mathrm{s}}^{-1}\right)$ :
    solvent Pekar factor; $\tau_{\mathrm{D}}$ : Debye relaxation time and $\tau_{\mathrm{L}}$ : longitudinal relaxation time.

[^2]:    Donor number ( $D N$ ), acceptor number ( $A N$ ) and autoprotolysis constants ( $\mathrm{p} K_{\mathrm{SH}}$ ) (with relative permittivities $\left(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}\right)$ ).
    ${ }^{a}$ For the numbers before the solvent names, see Table 1.1.
    ${ }^{b}$ G means gas and L means liquid.

[^3]:    9) For example, Marcus [23] proposed the $\mu$ scale between the sizes of the hard acids, $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ for the softness of solvents. If the Gibbs energy of transfer of species $i$ from water to solvent $S$ is expressed by $\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}(i, W \rightarrow S)$ (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ), $\mu$ is defined by $\mu=\left\{(1 / 2)\left[\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{W} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)+\right.\right. \left.\left.\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{K}^{+}, \mathrm{W} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)\right]-\Delta \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{W} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}\right)\right\} / 100$. This scale is based on the fact that the size of the soft acid, $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$( 0.115 nm in radius), is ( 0.102 nm ) and $\mathrm{K}^{+}$( 0.138 nm ). Examples of the $\mu$ values are as follows [2]: AN 0.34; DMF 0.11, DMTF 1.35; DMSO 0.22; FA 0.09; NB 0.23; NM 0.03; NMP 0.13, NMTP 1.35; PC -0.09 ; Py 0.64 . Here, DMTF and NMTP are soft bases, and Py and AN are between soft and hard.
[^4]:    11) A volatile organic compound is defined by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) as any compound of carbon, excluding carbon monoxide, carbon
[^5]:    1) For an electrically neutral molecule, the where $z_{i}$ is the ionic charge and $\chi$ is the surface solvation energy $\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}$ is equal to the Gibbs energy of transfer $\Delta G_{t}^{\circ}$ of the molecule from a vacuum into the solvent. However, for an electrically charged ion, the following relationship holds: potential at the vacuum/solution interface. For an electrically neutral electrolyte, the influence of the surface potential cancels out between the cation and the anion. Thus, the electrolyte can be treated like a neutral molecule.

    $$
    \Delta G_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ}=\Delta G_{\mathrm{sv}}^{\circ}+z_{i} F \chi
    $$

(ii) For $\left(z_{+}, z_{-}\right)$-type electrolytes, Onsager derived $\Lambda=\Lambda^{\infty}$ $\left(A^{\prime} \Lambda^{\infty}+B^{\prime}\right) I^{1 / 2}$, where $A^{\prime}=2.80 \times 10^{6} \times$
    $\left|z_{+} z_{-}\right| q /\left\{\left(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} T\right)^{3 / 2}\left(1+q^{1 / 2}\right)\right\}, B^{\prime}=41.25 \times \left(\left|z_{+}\right|+\left|z_{-}\right|\right) /\left\{\eta\left(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} T\right)^{1 / 2}\right\}, q=\left\{\left|z_{+} z_{-}\right| /\right. \left.\left(\left|z_{+}\right|+\mid z_{-}\right)\right\}\left\{\left(\lambda_{+}^{\infty}+\lambda_{-}^{\infty}\right) /\left(\left|z_{+}\right| \lambda_{+}^{\infty}+\left|z_{-}\right| \lambda_{-}^{\infty}\right)\right\}$, and $I=\left(\sum c_{i} z_{i}^{2}\right) / 2$.
